quizzes.scenario: Chief Okoro executes a document to change his name and addresses it to the whole world. This document involves only him.
options: Deed of Assignment
Deed Poll
Indenture
Deed of Rectification
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: The rule is that a Deed Poll involves just one person and is addressed to the whole world, whereas an Indenture involves two or more parties [1].

quizzes.scenario: Mr. Ade, a solicitor, drafts a deed of assignment which is sealed and delivered by the parties, but they forgot to sign it. The purchaser now seeks to enforce the deed in court.
options: The deed is valid as signature is not mandatory
The deed is void and inadmissible in evidence
The deed is valid since it was sealed and delivered
Only the seal is required for a company
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: The rule under Section 97 of the PCL 1959 and Section 83(4) of the Evidence Act 2011 is that a deed must be signed by the parties to be valid and admissible in evidence; an unsigned document is worthless and void [2-4].

quizzes.scenario: Baba Ijesha, an illiterate, affixes his thumbprint to a deed of assignment. However, the document does not state that it was translated to his mother tongue or read to him in the presence of a magistrate.
options: The deed is valid because of the thumbprint
The deed is void for lack of an illiterate jurat
Only the signature of a witness is needed
It is valid if he understood English
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: The rule under Sections 2 and 3 of the Illiterate Protection Act and Section 76(3) of the LRL is that where an illiterate is involved, there must be a jurat stating the document was translated and read to him, and it must be attested by a Magistrate, Notary Public, or Commissioner for Oaths [5, 6].

quizzes.scenario: XYZ (Nig.) Limited executes a deed of assignment. The company director and secretary sign the document, but the company's common seal is not affixed.
options: The deed is invalid for lack of common seal
The deed is valid as a common seal is no longer mandatory
A deed must always have a common seal
Only a Notary Public can validate it
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: The rule under Sections 98, 102, and 103 of CAMA 2020 is that the affixing of a common seal is no longer mandatory for a company to validly execute a deed, provided it is signed by a director and secretary or two directors [4, 7].

quizzes.scenario: In a property dispute in Lagos, a party produces a deed that is signed and attested by witnesses but has no physical red wax seal. The opposing party challenges its validity as a deed.
options: The deed is invalid because it is not under seal
The deed is presumed sealed and delivered due to signature and attestation
Only a red wax seal is recognized in Lagos
The letters "LS" must be present
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: The rule under Section 159 of the Evidence Act 2011 and Section 76(1)(d) of the Land Registration Law of Lagos State is that a deed is presumed to have been sealed and delivered if there is signature and attestation [8, 9].

quizzes.scenario: A vendor executes a deed of assignment and delivers it to his solicitor with instructions not to hand it over until the purchaser pays the full balance. Before the purchaser pays, the vendor changes his mind and wants to withdraw.
options: The vendor can withdraw as the delivery was conditional
The vendor cannot withdraw if the condition is fulfilled within a reasonable time
A deed in escrow is not binding until the condition is met
Only the court can stop the vendor
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: The rule in Beesely v Hallywood Estates Limited and Awojugbagbe Light Industries Ltd. v Chinukwe is that once a deed is executed in escrow, the executing party cannot escape its effect provided the other party fulfills the condition within the agreed or a reasonable time [10].

quizzes.scenario: A purchaser fulfills the condition of a deed in escrow on June 15th, although the deed was originally executed and delivered in escrow on May 1st. What is the effective date of the interest transfer?
options: June 15th
May 1st
The date Governor's consent is obtained
The date of registration
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: The doctrine of relation back, as seen in Anambra State Housing Development Corporation v. Emekwe, provides that where a condition in escrow is performed, the effective date of delivery is the date the instrument was executed/delivered as escrow [10, 11].

quizzes.scenario: A Power of Attorney (POA) to execute a deed in Nigeria is executed by a donor resident in London. It is signed by the donor but not attested by a Notary Public.
options: It is valid as attestation is never mandatory
It is invalid as attestation is compulsory for a POA to be used abroad
It is valid if two lay witnesses sign
It is only valid if signed by a Judge in Nigeria
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: The rule under Section 150 of the Evidence Act and Ezeigwe v. Awudu is that a deed executed outside the country for conferring power of attorney must be attested by a notary public, judge, or magistrate to be presumed validly executed [12, 13].

quizzes.scenario: A deed of assignment is duly signed, sealed, and delivered but the date space is left blank. The parties later argue that the deed is invalid because it is undated.
options: The deed is invalid for lack of a date
The deed is valid as a date is not mandatory for validity
Only the date of registration matters
The deed must be dated within 30 days to be valid
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: The rule in Awojugbagbe Light Industries Ltd v. Chinukwe and Jegede v. Citicon Nig. Ltd. is that the absence of a date in a deed does not invalidate it; it takes effect from the date of delivery [13, 14].

quizzes.scenario: Chief Buba sells his land in an urban area of Lagos to Mr. Dele. They execute a deed of assignment, but do not seek the consent of the Governor.
options: The transfer is valid between the parties
The transfer of legal interest is null and void
Consent is only needed for agricultural land
Only the Minister of FCT gives consent
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: The rule under Sections 22 and 26 of the Land Use Act and Savannah Bank v. Ajilo is that any transfer of a statutory right of occupancy without the Governor's consent is null and void [15, 16].

quizzes.scenario: A deed of assignment is prepared by Mr. Musa, a non-lawyer, and is used for a land transaction. The lawyer who eventually handles the perfection signs his name and address on the face of the deed.
options: This is proper practice
This is a breach of the Legal Practitioners Act and RPC
Only the client's address is required
Non-lawyers are allowed to prepare deeds
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: The rule under Section 22(1)(d) of the LPA and Rule 10 of the RPC is that only a legal practitioner can prepare such instruments, and franking (endorsing the lawyer's name/address) is mandatory for the lawyer who prepared it [17, 18].

quizzes.scenario: An executor of a deceased person's estate wants to convey land to a beneficiary named in the Will. He is unsure if he must execute a formal Deed of Assignment.
options: A deed is mandatory for all land transfers
An Assent is required and it is not required to be by deed
Only a Court order can move the land
A letter of administration is the only way
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: The rule under Section 77(2) of the PCL is that an Assent by a personal representative does not require a deed, though it must be in writing [19, 20].

quizzes.scenario: A landlord grants a lease of a warehouse to a tenant for a term of five years. The agreement is made in writing but not under seal (not by deed).
options: The lease is valid as a simple contract
The lease is void for the purpose of conveying a legal estate
Leases for 5 years do not need a deed
Only oral leases are void
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: The rule under Section 77(1) of the PCL and Section 74 of the Land Registration Law of Lagos is that a lease exceeding three years must be by deed to be valid for conveying a legal estate [21, 22].

quizzes.scenario: In a deed of assignment, the Recital states the property is 2 hectares, but the "All That" (Parcel) clause specifies it as 1.5 hectares. Which description prevails?
options: The Recital description
The Parcel clause description
The deed is void for ambiguity
The average of both descriptions
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: The rule in Ex parte Davies is that a specific description in the operative part (Parcel clause) of a deed is not controlled by a general description in the recital where the operative part is clear [23].

quizzes.scenario: A vendor executes a deed that includes a clause stating: "In consideration of the sum of N10,000,000 (the receipt of which the vendor acknowledges)". Does the purchaser need a separate receipt?
options: Yes, a separate receipt is always required
No, the receipt clause dispenses with the need for a separate receipt
Only if the transaction is in Lagos
A separate receipt is needed for Governor's consent
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: The rule under Section 54 of the CA and Section 92 of the PCL is that the inclusion of a receipt clause in a deed dispenses with the need to issue a formal separate receipt [24, 25].

quizzes.scenario: A purchaser's solicitor drafts a Deed of Assignment but omits the Habendum clause. The vendor argues the deed is invalid.
options: The deed is invalid
The deed is valid as a total residue transfer is implied
Only a lease needs a habendum
The habendum is only for recitals
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: The rule in Stephen Idugboe v. Anenih is that while the Habendum defines the quantum of interest, its absence in an assignment does not affect validity because it is implied that the entire residue is assigned [26].

quizzes.scenario: After a deed of assignment has been executed, the parties realize the property description is wrong and they cross it out and write the correct one.
options: The alteration is valid if initialed
A Deed of Rectification is required after execution
Only the solicitor can cross it out
Alterations after execution are never possible
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: The rule is that while alterations before execution can be initialed, once a deed has been executed, a Deed of Rectification is required to make changes [27].

quizzes.scenario: Mr. X is named as a party in an Indenture (Deed of Assignment) but he refuses to sign it. The other parties want to enforce a covenant in the deed against him.
options: He is bound as he was named as a party
He is not bound as he did not sign the document
Only the vendor's signature is required
His solicitor's signature is enough
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: The rule of Privity of Contract, as seen in Tweddle v. Atkinson, is that a legal document cannot be enforced against a person who did not sign it as they are deemed not to be a party to it [28].

quizzes.scenario: A vendor is expressed to convey a property in his capacity as a "Beneficial Owner" in the deed of assignment. What is the implication?
options: No covenants are implied
Covenants for good title and right to convey are implied
He is acting as a trustee
He is exempt from all liabilities
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: The rule under Section 7(a) & (b) of the CA and Section 100(1)(a) & (b) of the PCL is that conveying as a "Beneficial Owner" implies covenants of good title, right to convey, quiet enjoyment, and freedom from encumbrances [29, 30].

quizzes.scenario: Mr. Gambo gives a Power of Attorney to his agent to execute a Deed of Assignment on his behalf. How must the Power of Attorney be created?
options: Orally
By simple writing
By Deed (under seal)
By a letter of authority
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: The rule in Abina v. Farhat is that where a Power of Attorney authorizes an attorney to execute a deed, the instrument of appointment (the POA itself) must also be made by deed [31-33].

quizzes.scenario: Chief Emeka grants a 4-year term of his warehouse in Kano to Garba via a simple written agreement signed only by the parties. Garba pays the full rent, but a dispute arises regarding the validity of the interest. What is the legal status of this transaction?
options: Valid legal lease
Void as a legal lease but valid as an equitable lease
Valid tenancy agreement
Void for all purposes
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: The rule is that all leases for a term exceeding three years must be created by Deed to pass a legal estate, as provided by Section 77(1) of the PCL and the Real Property Act; however, under the rule in Walsh v Lonsdale, such an agreement may still operate in equity [1-4].

quizzes.scenario: Alhaji Musa grants a fixed term of two years to Tunde for a residential apartment in Surulere, Lagos. On the day the term expires, Alhaji Musa demands that Tunde vacates immediately, but Tunde insists he is entitled to a six-month notice to quit. What is the correct legal position?
options: Tunde is entitled to 6 months' notice
Tunde is entitled to 3 months' notice
No notice to quit is required
Tunde is entitled to 1 month's notice
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: The rule is that a lease for a fixed term certain is determined by effluxion of time and the tenant is not entitled to a notice to quit, but only a seven-day notice of the owner's intention to recover possession under Section 13(5) of the Tenancy Law of Lagos [5].

quizzes.scenario: A landlord in Ikeja, Lagos, is negotiating a new yearly tenancy with a prospective tenant. He demands that the tenant pays two years' rent in advance before taking possession. The tenant's lawyer objects. What is the maximum advance rent the landlord can legally collect?
options: 3 months
6 months
1 year
2 years
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: The rule under Section 4 of the Tenancy Law of Lagos State 2011 is that it is unlawful for a landlord to receive rent in excess of one year from a new yearly tenant; receiving more attracts a fine or imprisonment [6, 7].

quizzes.scenario: Mr. Okon executes a document granting a lease of his shop to Musa "to commence when the current renovation of the Third Mainland Bridge is completed." Musa pays the rent and takes possession, but Mr. Okon later seeks to evict him, claiming the lease is void. What is the status of the lease?
options: Valid because the event is ascertainable
Valid because rent was paid
Void for uncertainty of commencement
Void because it is a future lease
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: The rule is that the commencement date of a lease must be certain or ascertainable; a lease for a future term is void unless a definite time for commencement is fixed or can be inferred, and an event like bridge renovation is not a date certain [8, 9].

quizzes.scenario: Madam Kofo leases the upper floor of her duplex to a tenant but insists on keeping the duplicate keys to the main entrance and a spare bedroom for her son's occasional visits. The tenant claims he does not have a valid lease. Is the tenant correct?
options: No, because he has exclusive occupation
Yes, because he lacks exclusive possession
No, because he pays rack rent
Yes, because the term is less than 3 years
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: The rule is that a valid lease must confer exclusive possession, which is the right to exclude all persons including the landlord; keeping a key for a third party's use prevents the grant of exclusive possession, rendering the arrangement a mere license [10, 11].

quizzes.scenario: A lease agreement contains a clause stating the tenant "shall not assign or sublet the premises without the landlord's written consent." The tenant finds a reputable replacement, but the landlord refuses consent without giving any reason. What type of bar is this?
options: Absolute bar
Balanced interest
Conditional bar
Statutory bar
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: The rule is that a covenant not to sublet without consent constitutes a conditional bar, which is a qualified prohibition that uses a subjective test, allowing the lessor to reject a sub-tenant without providing reasons [12].

quizzes.scenario: Mr. Ade is a yearly tenant in a luxury apartment in Ikoyi, Lagos (VIIA). His landlord served him a three-month notice to quit, citing the Tenancy Law of Lagos 2011. Mr. Ade's lawyer argues the notice is invalid under that specific law. Why?
options: Ikoyi is excluded from the Tenancy Law
Yearly tenants need 12 months' notice
The law only applies to shops
The notice must be 6 months
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: The rule under Section 1(3) of the Tenancy Law of Lagos 2011 is that the law does not apply to Victoria Island, Ikoyi, Ikeja GRA, and Apapa; in these areas, the Rent Control and Recovery of Residential Premises Law applies instead [13, 14].

quizzes.scenario: A 10-year lease agreement for a commercial plaza in Abuja includes a "Rent Review Clause" but fails to specify the method for calculating the new rent. When the landlord attempts to double the rent, the tenant objects. How should the new rent be determined?
options: The landlord has the final say
The rent stays the same forever
Through expert valuation or arbitration
By the prevailing inflation rate
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: The rule is that a rent review clause should include a method for calculation, such as open market rate or expert valuation; where parties cannot agree, the matter is typically referred to a single arbitrator under the Arbitration and Mediation Act [15, 16].

quizzes.scenario: A tenant of a 5-year lease in Delta State wants to renovate the kitchen. The lease contains an "Absolute Bar" against alterations. The tenant renovates anyway, claiming it improved the property's value. What is the landlord's remedy?
options: No remedy as value increased
Forfeiture if provided for in the lease
Mandatory rent review
None, as absolute bars are illegal
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: The rule is that where there is an absolute bar against alterations, the lessee cannot make changes without consent; if done, the landlord may sue for damages, an injunction, or forfeiture if a re-entry clause exists [17-19].

quizzes.scenario: A yearly tenant in a house in Jos, Plateau State, fails to pay his rent for two consecutive years. The landlord, without serving any notice, forcefully removes the tenant's belongings and changes the locks. Is the landlord's action lawful?
options: Yes, due to the two-year default
Yes, if it is a periodic tenancy
No, he must comply with statutory recovery procedures
No, because Joz is a CA jurisdiction
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: The rule is that a landlord cannot resort to self-help to recover possession; he must comply with the procedural requirements of the relevant Recovery of Premises statutes, which include serving statutory notices and obtaining a court order [17, 20].

quizzes.scenario: A 20-year lease for a factory in Ogun State is silent on who should pay the tenement rates. The local government serves a demand notice on the tenant. Who is primarily responsible for this payment under property law principles?
options: The Landlord
The Tenant
Both equally
The Mortgagee
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: The rule is that usual covenants imply that the tenant pays rates and taxes except those expressly reserved for the landlord; additionally, statutes often identify the "occupier" as the party liable for tenement rates [21-23].

quizzes.scenario: A tenant holds a 10-year lease with an "Option to Renew" for another 5 years. Six months before expiration, the tenant writes to the landlord requesting a 10-year renewal at the old rent. Is this a valid exercise of the option?
options: Yes, because it was in writing
Yes, if he has performed all covenants
No, because he changed the terms of the offer
No, because he needs Governor's consent
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: The rule is that an option to renew is an offer that must be accepted in its entirety by the lessee; a request containing new terms is not a valid exercise of the option as held in Iita v Khawam [24, 25].

quizzes.scenario: During a 7-year lease in Lagos, the roof of the demised building is destroyed by a freak storm. The lease is silent on "Abetment of Rent." The tenant refuses to pay rent while the building is uninhabitable. Is his refusal legally justified?
options: Yes, under the doctrine of frustration
No, rent continues to run
Yes, because the landlord must repair the roof
No, but he can sue for specific performance
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: The rule is that generally, the doctrine of frustration is inapplicable to leases, and rent continues to run even if the property is destroyed unless an "Abatement of Rent" clause is expressly included in the agreement [26-28].

quizzes.scenario: A landlord grants a monthly tenancy to a tenant in Abuja. After three months, the landlord wants to terminate the tenancy to give the room to his brother. How much notice to quit must the landlord serve on the tenant?
options: 1 week
2 weeks
1 month
6 months
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: The rule under the Recovery of Premises Act (applicable in Abuja) is that for a monthly tenancy, one month's notice to quit must be given by either party to determine the relationship [5].

quizzes.scenario: A tenant has an "Option to Purchase Reversion" in his 15-year lease. The landlord, midway through the term, sells the property to a third party without notifying the tenant. What can the tenant do?
options: Nothing, as the landlord owns the property
Sue to set aside the sale to the third party
Stop paying rent immediately
Claim a refund of all past rent
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: The rule is that an option to purchase reversion creates an equitable interest in favor of the tenant, and the tenant may enforce this by specific performance or sue to set aside a sale to a third party as held in Owosho v Dada [26, 29].

quizzes.scenario: A 50-year-old man grants a 5-year lease of his property in Lagos to a 17-year-old university student. The student's father signs as a guarantor. Is the student a valid party to this lease?
options: Yes, because he is a student
No, he lacks legal capacity
Yes, if the father is a party
No, unless the Governor consents
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: The rule under Section 7 of the Land Use Act is that the age of maturity for acquiring or dealing in land is 21 years; a person below this age cannot generally be a party to a lease unless a guardian or trustee is appointed [30-33].

quizzes.scenario: A landlord and tenant enter into an oral lease for a term of 2 years in Lagos. The tenant takes possession and pays the full rack rent for the period. Is this lease enforceable despite not being in writing?
options: No, it must be by Deed
Yes, as an exception to the Statute of Frauds
No, because it lacks a seal
Yes, but only for 1 year
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: The rule is that an oral lease is enforceable if the term does not exceed three years, the lessee takes possession, and it reserves the best rent (rack rent) without a premium, satisfying Section 3 of the Statute of Frauds [34].

quizzes.scenario: A tenant's 2-year fixed term tenancy in Kaduna expires. The tenant remains in possession, and the landlord accepts another year's rent from him without signing a new agreement. What is the tenant's current status?
options: Periodic tenant
Tenant at sufferance
Statutory tenant
Trespasser
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: The rule is that where a lease has expired but the lessor continues to accept rent, the lessor is deemed to have renewed the lease on the same terms as the expired lease, typically creating a periodic tenancy [35].

quizzes.scenario: A lease for a term of 10 years in Lagos is executed by a company. The company affixes its common seal in the presence of one director and one witness who is the company's driver. Is this execution valid under current Nigerian law?
options: No, needs two directors
Yes, if authorized by the company's statute
No, must be attested by a Magistrate
Yes, but only with Governor's consent
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: The rule under CAMA 2020 (Sections 102 and 103) is that a company may execute a deed without a common seal, and where a seal is used, it can be valid if signed by persons authorized by law or the company's statute [36-38].

quizzes.scenario: A 5-year sublease in Lagos is executed but not registered at the Land Registry. The sub-lessee seeks to use the document in court to prove his title against a trespasser. Will the document be admitted?
options: Yes, for all purposes
No, it is inadmissible to prove title
Yes, if it is stamped
No, because it is void
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: The rule under the Land Registration Law of Lagos is that a registrable instrument (like a sublease above 3 years) that is not registered is inadmissible in court to prove title, although it may be admissible for other limited purposes [39, 40].

quizzes.scenario: Alhaji Kola orally agrees to sell his ancestral land in a village in Oyo State to Mr. Emeka for N5 million. Mr. Emeka pays the full purchase price and is put into possession in the presence of the village head and three other witnesses. When Alhaji Kola's son challenges the sale for lack of a written agreement, what is the legal position?
options: The sale is void because all land transactions must be in writing under the Statute of Frauds
The sale is voidable at the instance of the family members regardless of the witnesses
The sale is valid and enforceable as an exception to the requirement of writing under customary law
The sale is invalid because the Governor's consent was not obtained before the oral agreement
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Under native law and custom, an oral contract for the sale of land is enforceable if there is payment of the full purchase price, the purchaser is put into possession, and the transaction is witnessed by credible adult witnesses [1].

quizzes.scenario: Mr. Bode and Mrs. Kemi signed a simple receipt for the sale of a house in Kaduna. The receipt clearly states the names of the parties, the description of the property, the price of N20 million, and is signed by both. Mrs. Kemi later refuses to execute a formal deed. What type of contract is this?
options: A void contract for lack of professional drafting
An open contract
A formal contract of sale
A privileged contract
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: An open contract is one that provides only the minimum requirements of the Statute of Frauds: parties, property, price, and signature. It is valid and enforceable even without strict formality [2, 3].

quizzes.scenario: During negotiations for a duplex in Abuja, Mr. Okon pays N2 million to the vendor's solicitor before a formal contract is signed to show his commitment. The transaction fails because Mr. Okon changes his mind. What is the likely fate of the N2 million?
options: It must be refunded in full as it was part-payment
It is forfeited to the vendor as a deposit
The solicitor must keep it as a stakeholder until a court order is made
It is shared equally between the vendor and the purchaser
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: A deposit is money paid as security before the conclusion of a contract to show commitment. If the purchaser defaults, the deposit is generally treated as forfeited to the vendor [4-6].

quizzes.scenario: 19-year-old Tobi inherits N50 million and wishes to purchase a commercial plot in Ikeja, Lagos. He approaches a vendor to sign a contract of sale. How should the transaction proceed under the Land Use Act?
options: Tobi can sign personally as long as his parents consent
The Governor cannot grant consent to the alienation to Tobi unless a guardian or trustee is appointed on his behalf
The transaction is valid as the age of maturity for property in Lagos is 18
Tobi must wait until he is 21 as he has no legal capacity to hold land until then
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: By virtue of Section 7(a) of the Land Use Act, the Governor cannot grant a statutory right of occupancy or consent to the assignment of one to a person under 21 years old, except where a guardian or trustee is appointed for them [7, 8].

quizzes.scenario: A Chinese investment firm wants to buy a large expanse of land in the Idu Industrial Area of Abuja from a local company. What special consent is required for this transaction?
options: Only the consent of the Minister of the FCT
Consent of the National Council of States
Consent of the President of the Federal Republic of Nigeria
No special consent is needed beyond the usual Governor's consent
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Section 46(1) of the Land Use Act provides that a non-Nigerian cannot be granted a right of occupancy or have one assigned to them except with the consent of the National Council of States [9, 10].

quizzes.scenario: A solicitor is investigating the title of a property in Enugu (a Conveyancing Act State). How far back must the root of title date to be considered a "good root" under Nigerian law?
options: 20 years
30 years
40 years
12 years
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: In states governed by the Conveyancing Act (former Northern and Eastern regions), a good root of title must date back at least 40 years [11, 12].

quizzes.scenario: Mr. Obi is buying land in Aba, Abia State. His solicitor insists on seeing title documents dating back 40 years. The vendor argues that 30 years is sufficient. Who is correct?
options: The solicitor, because Abia is an Eastern State governed by the CA 40-year rule
The vendor, because Abia State Law of Property has specifically reduced the period to 30 years
Neither, because the Land Use Act made all titles date back to 1978
The solicitor, because the Evidence Act requires 40 years for all states
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Although Abia is in the former Eastern region, Section 70(1) of the Abia State Law of Property is an exception that reduces the required period for deducing title to 30 years [12, 13].

quizzes.scenario: After the exchange of a formal contract for the sale of a beachfront villa in Lagos, but before completion, a massive tidal wave destroys the structure. The contract is silent on risk. Who bears the loss?
options: The Vendor, because legal title has not yet passed
The Purchaser, because risk passes to them immediately upon exchange of contract
Both parties share the loss under the doctrine of frustration
The insurance company of the Vendor, who must then refund the Purchaser
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: In a sale of land, risk in the property passes to the purchaser immediately after the contract is exchanged, and the purchaser remains liable to pay the balance even if the property is destroyed before completion [14-16].

quizzes.scenario: A single solicitor represents both the Vendor and the Purchaser in a friendly sale of a flat in Surulere. The parties sign the contract in the solicitor's office. Is a formal "exchange of contract" necessary?
options: Yes, the solicitor must mail a copy to himself to signify exchange
No, exchange is not necessary when one solicitor acts for both parties; it is binding upon signing
Yes, the solicitor must physically hand the document from one hand to the other
No, because exchange only applies to deeds, not contracts
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: An exchange of contract is only necessary where different solicitors act for the parties. Where one solicitor acts for both, the contract is binding once signed by the parties [17].

quizzes.scenario: Chief Dan is selling his farmland in a rural, non-urban area of Ekiti State. The land is held under a customary right of occupancy. Whose consent is required for this assignment?
options: The Governor of Ekiti State
The Minister of Lands
The appropriate Local Government
No consent is required for rural land
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Under Section 21 of the Land Use Act, any transfer of a customary right of occupancy in a non-urban area (not under a court order) requires the approval of the appropriate Local Government [7, 18, 19].

quizzes.scenario: A Limited Liability Company is purchasing a warehouse from an individual in Lagos. To which authority should the stamp duty on the Deed of Assignment be paid?
options: Lagos State Board of Internal Revenue (LIRS)
Federal Inland Revenue Service (FIRS)
The Stamp Duties Commissioner of the High Court
The Land Registry
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Stamp duties on instruments where a corporate body is a party (either as assignor or assignee) are paid to the Federal Government through the Federal Inland Revenue Service (now Nigeria Revenue Service) [20-22].

quizzes.scenario: Mr. A sells the same plot of land to Mr. B on Monday and then to Mr. C on Wednesday. Mr. B fails to register his deed, but Mr. C, unaware of B, registers his deed immediately. Who has priority?
options: Mr. B, because he was first in time
Mr. C, because his registered instrument ranks higher in priority
The Vendor, Alhaji A, because both transactions are now void
The state government, as the land reverts to the Governor
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Under the Land Instrument Registration Laws, instruments rank in priority according to the date of registration, not the date of execution [23-25].

quizzes.scenario: A solicitor wants to confirm the status of a property in Lagos. He applies to the Land Registry for a search. What is the prescribed form for the official search report he will receive under LIMS?
options: Form 1
Form 2
Form 3
Form 4
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Under the Lagos State Land Registration Law 2015 (LIMS), the Registrar issues an official search report using Form 4 [26-29].

quizzes.scenario: While investigating a vendor's title, a purchaser's solicitor discovers that a previous mortgage on the property was not clearly discharged in the abstract of title. What is the appropriate next step?
options: Rescind the contract immediately for breach of title
File a caveat at the Land Registry
Raise a requisition of title asking for the Deed of Release
Sue the vendor for misrepresentation
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Requisitions are formal questions or queries raised by the purchaser's solicitor to the vendor concerning doubts or gaps discovered during the investigation of the vendor's title [12, 30, 31].

quizzes.scenario: A contract of sale for a fully furnished mansion is silent on whether the custom-built, wall-embedded wardrobes are included. Can the vendor remove them before completion?
options: Yes, because they are personal property (chattels)
No, because under the principle of "quid quid plantatur solo solo cedit," fixtures form part of the land
Yes, if the vendor can prove he paid for them separately
No, but the purchaser must pay extra for them at completion
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Under the maxim "quid quid plantatur solo solo cedit," anything permanently attached to the land (fixtures) forms part of the land and passes with it unless expressly excluded in the contract [32, 33].

quizzes.scenario: A purchaser insists on taking possession of a house in Lagos immediately after the contract stage but before completion. How should the solicitor draft this to protect the vendor's right to evict if the deal fails?
options: Draft it as a periodic yearly tenancy
Grant possession as a mere licensee or a tenant at will
State that the purchaser is now the legal owner in possession
There is no way to protect the vendor once possession is given
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: To avoid the difficulties of recovering premises under the Tenancy Law, a purchaser allowed into possession before completion should enter as a licensee or tenant at will, making it easier to evict them if the contract fails [34, 35].

quizzes.scenario: At the completion stage of a sale of 2 plots out of a larger 10-plot estate, the vendor refuses to hand over the original Certificate of Occupancy. What must the vendor provide to the purchaser instead?
options: A photocopy of the C of O signed by a Magistrate
Nothing, the purchaser only needs the Deed of Assignment
An acknowledgement for production and an undertaking for safe custody of the original document
A sworn affidavit that the original is lost
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Where a vendor retains the original title documents (e.g., because they relate to other lands he still owns), the purchaser is entitled to a clause for acknowledgement of the right to production and an undertaking for safe custody [36-38].

quizzes.scenario: A solicitor representing both the vendor and the purchaser discovers halfway through the transaction that the vendor's title is actually defective. What is his ethical duty?
options: He must keep the information confidential from the purchaser to protect the vendor
He must continue the representation and try to patch the title quietly
He must disclose the defect to both and advise them to retain separate solicitors due to the conflict of interest
He must side with the purchaser and sue the vendor
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: If a solicitor acting for both parties discovers that the title is not sound or their interests conflict, he must advise the parties to retain their respective solicitors to avoid a breach of professional conduct [39-42].

quizzes.scenario: A contract for the sale of land specifies completion shall be "on or before October 30th." The vendor fails to show up on that day. Can the purchaser terminate the contract on October 31st?
options: Yes, because the date was fixed
No, because generally time is not of essence in land sales unless expressly stated or a notice to complete is served
Yes, but only if the deposit was more than 10%
No, because the purchaser must wait for the Governor's consent first
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: In land transactions, time is generally not of the essence unless the parties expressly agree so, or the innocent party serves a "notice to complete" after a breach of the agreed date [43, 44].

quizzes.scenario: Mr. Dada is buying a house in Victoria Island, Lagos. He discovers the land was registered under the Registration of Titles Law (RTL) before it was repealed in 2015. Does the vendor need to deduce title for 40 years?
options: Yes, because the CA 40-year rule applies once a law is repealed
No, because titles registered under the RTL are indefeasible and conclusive proof of ownership, and this status is saved by the LRL 2015
Yes, because the LRL 2015 requires a fresh 40-year deduction for all lands
No, but he must deduce title for 12 years of adverse possession
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: The Lagos Land Registration Law (LRL) 2015 saved all titles registered under the repealed RTL. Because such registered titles are indefeasible/conclusive, there is no need for the vendor to deduce title for the statutory period [45-48].

quizzes.scenario: Mr. Okafor wants to purchase a parcel of land in Asaba, Delta State. He approaches his solicitor to know the primary legislation governing property transactions in that area.
options: The Conveyancing Act 1881
The Land Registration Law of Lagos
The Property and Conveyancing Law (PCL) 1959
The Registration of Titles Law
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Delta State, being part of the old Mid-Western region, is governed by the Property and Conveyancing Law (PCL) 1959 which is applicable to the old Western Region states [1, 2].

quizzes.scenario: A real estate developer is registering a deed of assignment for a property in Victoria Island, Lagos. Which specific law currently governs the central management and registration of titles and interests in Lagos?
options: Land Instrument Registration Law
Land Registration Law of Lagos State 2015
Conveyancing Act 1881
Statute of Frauds 1677
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: The Land Registration Law of Lagos State 2015 repealed previous laws like the RTL and LIRL to create a uniform system of registration for the entire state [3-5].

quizzes.scenario: 19-year-old Musa inherited a plot of land in Kaduna. He wants to sell the land to a friend to raise money for school. What is the legal position regarding his capacity to dispose of this land under the Land Use Act?
options: He can sell it because he inherited it
He cannot sell it because he is under 21 years of age
He can sell it only if he is married
He can sell it if he has a National ID card
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Under Section 7 of the Land Use Act, the Governor cannot grant a statutory right of occupancy or consent to the assignment of one to a person under 21, except where a guardian or trustee is appointed on his behalf [6, 7].

quizzes.scenario: Chief Emeka agreed orally to sell his land in Enugu to Mr. Obi for N10 million. Mr. Obi paid the full price and moved in, but Chief Emeka now refuses to sign a deed. Can Mr. Obi enforce this contract?
options: No, because land contracts must be in writing
Yes, under the doctrine of part performance
No, because oral contracts are criminal
Yes, because the price was over N5 million
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: While the Statute of Frauds 1677 and PCL require land contracts to be in writing, the rule in equity allows enforcement of oral contracts where there is sufficient part performance, such as payment of full price and taking possession [10-12].

quizzes.scenario: A deed of assignment was executed by a director of ABC Ltd in the presence of one witness, but the company's common seal was not affixed. The purchaser's lawyer objects to the validity of the deed. Is the lawyer right?
options: Yes, a common seal is mandatory for all companies
No, under CAMA 2020, a deed is validly executed by a director in the presence of a witness without a seal
Yes, two directors must always sign
No, only the Secretary can sign without a seal
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Under Sections 102 and 103 of CAMA 2020, the use of a common seal is no longer mandatory, and a deed is valid if executed by a director in the presence of at least one witness [13-15].

quizzes.scenario: A solicitor is handling a land sale in Ilorin, Kwara State. Which property law jurisdiction does this state fall under?
options: Property and Conveyancing Law 1959
Conveyancing Act 1881 & 1882
Lagos State Properties Protection Law
Land Registration Law of Lagos
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Kwara State (specifically Ilorin) follows the Conveyancing Act 1881 & 1882 jurisdiction, which applies to the old Northern and Eastern regions of Nigeria [3].

quizzes.scenario: A purchaser is investigating a title in Umuahia, Abia State. For how many years must the vendor deduce title to establish a good root?
options: 40 years
20 years
30 years
12 years
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Although Abia is in the former Eastern region which usually requires 40 years under the CA, Section 70(1) of the Abia State Law of Property has reduced the period for deducing title to 30 years [2, 25].

quizzes.scenario: A businessman wants to mortgage his property located on Federal Government land in Lagos. Whose consent is required for the transaction to be valid under the Land Use Act?
options: The Governor of Lagos State
The Minister of the FCT
The Minister in charge of Federal lands
The Local Government Chairman
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Under Section 51 of the Land Use Act, where land is owned or held by the Federal Government or its agency, the power to grant consent resides with the Minister [26].

quizzes.scenario: A vendor produces a deed of conveyance executed 25 years ago as his root of title. Can the purchaser's solicitor rely on the facts stated in this document without further proof?
options: No, deeds are never proof of facts
Yes, there is a presumption of regularity for recitals in documents 20 years old or more
Only if it is registered in Lagos
No, only 40-year-old documents are presumed correct
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Section 162 of the Evidence Act 2011 provides a presumption of regularity for facts, statements, or recitals contained in a deed or instrument which is at least 20 years old [36, 37].

quizzes.scenario: A German citizen resident in Lagos wishes to acquire a Statutory Right of Occupancy. What is the statutory requirement for this grant to be valid?
options: No requirement, foreigners can buy land freely
Consent of the President of Nigeria
Consent of the National Council of States
Approval of the German Embassy
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Under Section 46(1) of the Land Use Act, a non-Nigerian cannot be granted a right of occupancy nor can one be transferred to him except with the consent of the National Council of States [6, 26, 40].

quizzes.scenario: A deed of mortgage is executed between XYZ Nig. Ltd and a commercial bank. The solicitor needs to pay stamp duty. Which authority is competent to collect this duty?
options: The State Board of Internal Revenue
The Local Government Council
The Federal Inland Revenue Service (FIRS)
The Ministry of Lands
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Under the Stamp Duties Act, the Federal Government (through FIRS/NRS) has exclusive authority to collect duties on instruments executed between a company and another party [8, 9].

quizzes.scenario: Mr. Ade sold his house for a profit of N20 million. What is the statutory rate of Capital Gains Tax he is expected to pay on the gain?
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Under the Capital Gains Tax Act, the rate of tax charged on the gains accruing on the disposal of assets is 10% [16, 17].

quizzes.scenario: A property owner in Lagos receives a bill for "Land Use Charge". He argues he should only pay Tenement Rate. Which law consolidates these property-related taxes in Lagos?
options: Land Use Act 1978
Tenancy Law 2011
Land Use Charge Law 2018
Personal Income Tax Act
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: The Land Use Charge Law 2018 in Lagos consolidates Ground Rent, Tenement Rate, and Neighborhood Improvement Levy into a single property tax [18, 19].

quizzes.scenario: A solicitor has just concluded the delivery of a Deed of Assignment. Within how many days must the document be stamped to avoid penalties for ad valorem duty?
options: 40 days
60 days
30 days
90 days
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Section 23 of the Stamp Duties Act provides that instruments requiring ad valorem duty, such as a Deed of Assignment, must be stamped within 30 days of execution/delivery [30-32].

quizzes.scenario: A solicitor charges N2 million for professional fees in a property transaction. What is the current rate of VAT the solicitor should charge and remit to the FIRS?
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: While the original VAT Act specified 5%, the current rate under the Finance Act is 7.5% of the value of all taxable goods and services, including legal services [38, 39].

quizzes.scenario: A landlord in Abuja receives 6 years of advance rent from a corporate tenant. How will this income be treated for tax purposes?
options: It is tax-free capital
It is treated as personal income of an income nature
It is subject only to Stamp Duty
It is a gift
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Under the Personal Income Tax Act, gain or profit arising from a right granted for the use or occupation of property (rent) is taxable as personal income [41, 42].

quizzes.scenario: A solicitor is asked to represent both the Vendor and the Purchaser in a sale of land where the title is known to be subject to multiple family disputes. What is the most appropriate ethical action?
options: Represent both to save costs
Represent the Vendor only
Advise both parties to retain separate solicitors
Represent the Purchaser only
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Under the Rules of Professional Conduct, where interests are likely to conflict (such as a defective title), the solicitor must advise the parties to retain their respective solicitors to ensure competence and diligence [20-22].

quizzes.scenario: A personal representative executes a document to transfer a deceased's house to a beneficiary. The solicitor describes this as a "land transaction". Is this description correct?
options: Yes, all land transfers are transactions
No, an assent is not a land transaction, though it affects an interest in land
Yes, it is a mortgage
No, it is a power of attorney
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: The rule is that while sale, mortgage, and lease are land transactions, a Will and an Assent are not considered land transactions, though they affect interests in land [23, 24].

quizzes.scenario: An illiterate woman is selling her land in Benin. The solicitor prepares the deed but fails to include a jurat, claiming she understood the deal. What is the effect on the deed?
options: It is valid if she signed with a mark
It is voidable at the option of the buyer
It is invalid/void for failure to comply with the Illiterate Protection Act
It is valid if the price was paid
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: The rule under the Illiterate Protection Act is that failure to include an illiterate jurat, which involves reading and interpreting the document, vitiates the deed [27-29].

quizzes.scenario: A law firm allows an estate agent to use the firm's stamp and seal to frank deeds of assignment for a 10% commission. This practice violates which specific ethical duty?
options: Duty to be competent
Duty to avoid conflict of interest
Duty not to aid the unauthorized practice of law
Duty to keep client's funds separate
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Rule 3 of the RPC prohibits a lawyer from aiding a non-legal practitioner in the practice of law, which includes letting them prepare and frank legal documents [33-35].

quizzes.scenario: Chief Emeka, who is travelling to the UK for medical treatment, orally instructs his friend, Mr. Tunde, to execute a Deed of Assignment for his property in Enugu on his behalf. Mr. Tunde executes the deed as instructed. What is the legal status of the Deed of Assignment executed by Mr. Tunde?
options: The deed is valid since the instruction was clear.
The deed is voidable at the option of the purchaser.
The deed is unenforceable because the authority to execute a deed must be by deed.
The deed is valid if Mr. Tunde is a legal practitioner.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: The rule in Abina v. Farhat states that where a donee is to represent a donor in the execution of an instrument under seal (a deed), the authority (Power of Attorney) must also be made by deed [1-3].

quizzes.scenario: Amina, a 19-year-old married woman, executes a Power of Attorney in favor of her husband to manage her inherited estate in Ibadan. A relative challenges the document, claiming Amina lacks the legal capacity to grant a Power of Attorney due to her age. Is the challenge likely to succeed?
options: Yes, because she is under 21 years of age according to the Wills Act.
No, because a minor who is a married woman has the capacity to be a donor as if she were of full age.
Yes, because an infant can only grant a POA for the supply of necessaries.
No, because property matters are governed by the personal law of the husband.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Under Section 146 of the Property and Conveyancing Law (PCL) and Section 40 of the Conveyancing Act (CA), a minor who is a married woman has the capacity to be a donor or donee of a Power of Attorney as if she were of full age [4, 5].

quizzes.scenario: Alhaji Musa granted a Power of Attorney to Mr. Dele to collect rents on his shops in Kano. Three months later, Alhaji Musa personally collects the rents from all the tenants before Mr. Dele could do so. Mr. Dele sues the tenants for the rent. What is the effect of Alhaji Musa's action?
options: Mr. Dele is entitled to the rents as his power is subsisting.
The Power of Attorney is expressly revoked by Alhaji Musa's action.
The Power of Attorney is impliedly revoked because the donor personally exercised the power granted.
Alhaji Musa must pay damages to Mr. Dele for breach of contract.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: The rule in Chime v. Chime provides that the grant of a POA does not extinguish the donor's right to personally exercise the same power; however, doing so acts as an implied revocation of the power granted in respect of that specific act [6, 7].

quizzes.scenario: A Power of Attorney is executed by Mr. Okafor in favor of Z-Bank, stating it is "irrevocable for 18 months" from the date of execution. No valuable consideration was given for this irrevocability. Mr. Okafor seeks to revoke the power after 13 months. Can he validly do so?
options: No, it remains irrevocable for the full 18 months as stated.
Yes, because without consideration, a Power of Attorney is only irrevocable for a fixed period not exceeding 12 months.
No, because all Powers of Attorney expressed to be irrevocable are absolute.
Yes, but only if he obtains a court order for revocation.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Under Section 144 PCL and Section 9(1) CA 1882, where a POA is expressed to be irrevocable for a fixed period (whether for consideration or not), it is only legally irrevocable for a period not exceeding 12 months [8, 9].

quizzes.scenario: Chief Williams grants an irrevocable Power of Attorney to a developer, Apex Ltd, to build and sell apartments on his land after Apex Ltd paid him 50 million Naira for the right. Two years later, while the project is ongoing, Chief Williams dies. What is the status of the Power of Attorney?
options: It is revoked by the death of the donor by operation of law.
It is suspended until his personal representatives are appointed.
It remains irrevocable and is not affected by the death of the donor until the interest is realized.
It must be re-executed by the executors of Chief Williams' estate.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Under Section 143(1) PCL and Section 8(1) CA 1882, where a POA is given for valuable consideration and expressed to be irrevocable, it shall not be revoked by the death, insanity, or bankruptcy of the donor until the consideration is realized [9, 10].

quizzes.scenario: Mr. Johnson, resident in New York, executes a Power of Attorney authorizing his brother in Lagos to sell his house. The document is signed by Mr. Johnson but is not attested by any official. The brother attempts to register the sale at the Lagos Land Registry. What will be the likely outcome?
options: The registry will accept it as it is a private document.
The document will be rejected because a POA executed outside Nigeria must be attested by a Notary Public or similar official.
The document is valid but requires a 100,000 Naira fine for non-attestation.
Registration is unnecessary for a Power of Attorney in Lagos.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: By virtue of Section 150 of the Evidence Act and Section 76(2) of the Lagos State Land Registration Law (LRL), a POA executed outside Nigeria for use in Nigeria must be attested by a Notary Public, judge, magistrate, or Nigerian consul to be presumed valid [11-14].

quizzes.scenario: Pa Ibe, who cannot read or write, executes a Power of Attorney to his son to manage his farmlands. The son reads the document to him in English, and Pa Ibe affixes his thumbprint. No interpreter was present, and no jurat is included. Is the Power of Attorney valid?
options: Yes, because thumbprinting is a valid form of signature.
Yes, because the son is a family member.
No, it is invalid because it lacks an illiterate jurat as required by the Illiterate Protection Act.
No, unless it is also signed by the Village Head.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: The Illiterate Protection Act and Section 76(3) LRL require that where a donor is illiterate, the document must contain a jurat stating it was read and interpreted to him in a language he understands, and failure to comply renders the POA worthless and invalid [15-18].

quizzes.scenario: Mr. Udoh, the donee of a Power of Attorney granted by Chief Akpan, wishes to sue a tenant for recovery of premises. Mr. Udoh files the suit in his own name as "Mr. Udoh (Attorney to Chief Akpan)". The tenant's lawyer objects to the locus standi. What is the rule regarding the name in which an attorney sues?
options: An attorney can sue in his own name if the POA is registered.
An attorney must sue in the name of the donor, as the donee acts only in a representative capacity.
A donee can only sue in his own name if he has paid valuable consideration.
The objection is baseless because "Attorney" is a legal title.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: As established in Ude v. Nwara, a donee of a POA must act and execute in the name of the donor and cannot sue in his own name, as the donee's interest is purely representative [1, 19].

quizzes.scenario: Landmark Properties Ltd wants to register an irrevocable Power of Attorney that transfers interest in land in Lagos. The Registrar refuses to register the document because it does not have the Governor's consent endorsed on it. Is the Registrar's action correct?
options: No, because Governor's consent is only for Deeds of Assignment.
Yes, because in Lagos, an irrevocable POA relating to transfer of interest in land requires Governor's consent before registration.
No, because a Power of Attorney is a private document.
Yes, but only if the property is a State grant.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Under Section 57 of the Lagos State Land Registration Law (LRL) 2015, the Registrar shall not accept for registration any irrevocable Power of Attorney on which Governor's consent has not been endorsed [14, 20, 21].

quizzes.scenario: A Power of Attorney is granted by a family head to "The Branch Manager, First Bank Plc, Marina" to execute documents. The Manager attempts to sign a deed based on this power. Is the appointment valid?
options: Yes, it is an appointment by office.
No, because a Power of Attorney can only be granted to a legal person (natural or juristic), and "Branch Manager" is not a juristic person.
Yes, if the Bank's common seal is also affixed.
No, unless the Branch Manager is also a lawyer.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: In National Bank of Nigeria Ltd v. Korban Brothers, the court held that a "Manager" or "Chairman" is not a juristic person, and only persons in law (capable of suing and being sued) can be appointed as attorneys [4, 22, 23].

quizzes.scenario: Mr. Salami grants a Power of Attorney to "manage my estate at Ikoyi, collect rents, and do all things necessary and incidental as I may lawfully do." The attorney attempts to sell the property using the "incidental" clause. Will the court uphold the sale?
options: Yes, the omnibus clause gives the attorney all the powers of the donor.
No, because the Power Clause is strictly construed and the general clause is interpreted using the ejusdem generis rule.
Yes, provided the attorney acts in good faith.
No, because the property is in Ikoyi.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Powers of Attorney are strictly construed (NBA v. Iteogu). General or omnibus clauses are limited by the specific powers preceding them according to the ejusdem generis rule; thus, a power to "manage" and "collect rent" does not include a power to sell [24-26].

quizzes.scenario: Chief Gbadamosi grants a revocable Power of Attorney to his nephew. Chief Gbadamosi is later certified insane by a medical doctor. The nephew, unaware of the certification, sells a parcel of Chief's land to Mr. Obi. Is the sale valid?
options: Yes, because the nephew was unaware of the insanity.
No, the Power of Attorney is revoked by operation of law upon the donor's insanity.
Yes, if the nephew makes a statutory declaration of non-revocation.
No, unless the nephew is Chief Gbadamosi's next-of-kin.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Under the rule in Uba v. Registrar of Titles, a Power of Attorney is revoked by operation of law upon the death, insanity, or bankruptcy of the donor, as a POA cannot cure a legal disability [27].

quizzes.scenario: A donee exercises his power to sell a donor's car 10 days after the donor died. The donee was unaware of the death. To protect the purchaser, the donee makes a statutory declaration within two months stating he had no notice of the death at the time of sale. What is the legal effect?
options: The declaration is invalid as it was made after the sale.
The declaration is conclusive proof of non-revocation in favor of the third party.
The sale is void regardless of the declaration.
The donee is liable for criminal prosecution for making a false declaration.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Section 142(2) PCL and Section 7(2) CA provide that a statutory declaration made by a donee before or within 3 months after exercising a power, stating he had no notice of revocation (by death or otherwise), is conclusive proof of non-revocation [28, 29].

quizzes.scenario: Mrs. Adebayo was forced at gunpoint to sign a Power of Attorney in favor of her kidnapper to sell her house. The kidnapper sells the house to an innocent third party. Can Mrs. Adebayo challenge the POA?
options: No, the third party is a bona fide purchaser.
Yes, a Power of Attorney can be invalidated if fraud, duress, or undue influence is established.
No, because the POA was executed in writing.
Yes, but only if she had previously registered a caveat.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: In Agbo v. Nwikolo, it was established that a Power of Attorney, like any other legal instrument, can be invalidated if it was obtained through fraud, duress, or undue influence [10].

quizzes.scenario: Mr. Benson prepares a Power of Attorney for his client, Chief Z, appointing Mr. Benson (the lawyer) as the attorney to sell Chief Z's land. Mr. Benson then proceeds to act as the solicitor for the purchaser in the same transaction. What ethical rule has been breached?
options: Rule on advertising and solicitation.
Rule against charging excessive fees.
Rule against conflict of interest, as a lawyer should not be the donee and the drafting solicitor for the same transaction.
There is no breach as lawyers are trusted professionals.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Under the Rules of Professional Conduct (RPC) and general principles of ethics, a solicitor who is a donee of a POA should not draft the POA, and a solicitor should avoid conflict of interest by not acting for both sides where interests diverge [30-32].

quizzes.scenario: A Power of Attorney is granted to Mr. X, imposing an obligation on him to renovate the donor's house before collecting rent. Mr. X does not sign the document. The donor later sues Mr. X for failing to renovate. Can the donor enforce the obligation?
options: Yes, because the donee accepted the document.
No, because an obligation cannot be enforced against a person who has not executed (signed) the document.
Yes, if the donor has a copy of the POA.
No, unless the POA was under seal.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: While a POA is typically a deed poll (executed by one party), it is desirable for the donee to execute it where it imposes obligations on him, as one cannot enforce obligations against a non-signer [23, 33].

quizzes.scenario: A donor grants a revocable POA to an attorney. The donor later sends an email to the attorney revoking the power. Before receiving the email, the attorney sells the donor's property. Is the attorney liable?
options: Yes, revocation is effective the moment the email is sent.
No, revocation is only effective when the attorney receives notice of the revocation.
Yes, because the power was revocable.
No, provided the sale was registered.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: For express revocation, the attorney's authority does not cease until he receives a Notice of Revocation; acts done prior to receiving notice are valid and the attorney is not liable [6, 34].

quizzes.scenario: Mr. K buys land in Abuja and is given an irrevocable POA for value because the Minister's consent for a formal assignment is delayed. Mr. K sues a trespasser in his own name, claiming the POA passed interest to him. What is the Supreme Court's position?
options: A POA never passes interest in land.
A POA can pass interest in land in non-contentious circumstances where value is paid and irrevocability is declared.
Mr. K must wait for the Minister's consent before suing.
Only the donor can sue.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: In Ibrahim v. Obaje, the Supreme Court held that in specific non-contentious circumstances where a POA is backed by value and declared irrevocable, it may pass interest in land as an exception to the general rule [2, 35, 36].

quizzes.scenario: Chief A grants a POA to Mr. B. Later, without revoking the power to Mr. B, Chief A grants the same power to Mr. C. What is the status of the second grant to Mr. C?
options: It impliedly revokes the grant to Mr. B.
It is valid as a joint power.
The subsequent grant is deemed invalid as it cannot act as an implied revocation of the existing POA.
Both Mr. B and Mr. C must act jointly.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: In Adegbokan v. Akinsanya, it was held that a subsequent grant of POA to another person for the same subject matter without expressly revoking the first is not an implied revocation and the second grant is invalid [37].

quizzes.scenario: A Power of Attorney is prepared to allow a brother to sell a car. What is the stamp duty requirement for this document?
options: Ad valorem duty based on the car's value.
It is exempt from stamp duty.
It attracts a fixed stamp duty (e.g., N50 or N3 in Lagos).
It must be stamped at the same rate as a Deed of Assignment.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: A Power of Attorney attracts a fixed stamp duty (nominal fee) rather than ad valorem duty, which is based on the value of the transaction [38-40].

quizzes.scenario: Mr. Eze intends to create a legal mortgage over his statutory right of occupancy in Enugu. His solicitor advises that the most effective way to ensure the bank can transfer the legal title upon default without a court order is to assign the unexpired residue of his interest.
options: This is valid only in PCL states
This is a valid mode of creating a legal mortgage in CA states
This is an equitable mortgage
This mode is prohibited by the Land Use Act
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: In states governed by the Conveyancing Act (CA), such as Enugu (former Eastern region), a legal mortgage of a leasehold is created by assignment of the unexpired residue of the term, subject to a proviso for cesser on redemption [1, 2].

quizzes.scenario: Chief Okon creates a legal mortgage over his property in Abeokuta by way of sub-demise for a term of years absolute, one day shorter than his own term. The mortgage deed does not contain a power of attorney or a trust declaration.
options: The mortgagee cannot sell the legal estate upon default
The mortgage is void for lack of remedial devices
The mortgagee can sell the legal estate because the PCL grants this power
The mortgage is merely equitable
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Under Section 112 of the Property and Conveyancing Law (PCL), applicable in Ogun State (Abeokuta), a mortgagee by sub-demise has the statutory power to sell the mortgaged property and vest the mortgagor's reversionary interest in a purchaser without needing remedial devices like a trust declaration [3, 4].

quizzes.scenario: A company, XYZ Ltd, creates a mortgage over its warehouse in Lagos in favour of a commercial bank on June 1, 2026. The bank's solicitor seeks to protect the bank's interest against subsequent creditors.
options: The mortgage must be registered at the CAC within 30 days
The mortgage must be registered at the CAC within 90 days
Registration at the CAC is optional for companies
The mortgage is perfected by mere delivery of the deed
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Under Section 222 of the Companies and Allied Matters Act (CAMA), every charge or mortgage created by a company must be registered with the Corporate Affairs Commission (CAC) within 90 days of its creation to be valid against subsequent creditors or liquidators [5-7].

quizzes.scenario: Mr. Ade obtains a loan from a bank and deposits his original Certificate of Occupancy for his land in Jos with the bank manager, intending it to serve as security. No formal deed is executed.
options: This creates a legal mortgage
This is a mere lien and confers no interest
This creates an equitable mortgage
This is a pledge under customary law
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: An equitable mortgage is created by the mere deposit of title deeds with a clear intention that the deeds should be used as security for a loan [8, 9].

quizzes.scenario: A mortgage deed for a property in Kaduna specifies that the legal due date for repayment is December 31, 2026. The mortgagor fails to pay an installment in July 2026. The bank immediately attempts to sell the property.
options: The sale is valid because the power of sale is exercisable
The sale is void because the power of sale has not arisen
The sale is valid if notice was given
The sale is valid as a judicial sale
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: The power of sale only arises when the mortgage is by deed, there is no contrary intention, and the legal due date has passed. Since the legal due date (Dec 31) has not passed, the power of sale has not arisen, rendering any purported sale void [10-12].

quizzes.scenario: Mr. Benson creates an equitable mortgage in favour of Zenith Bank with the Governor's consent. Two years later, he seeks to convert this into a legal mortgage in favour of the same bank.
options: Fresh Governor's consent is mandatory
Governor's consent is not required for the conversion
The conversion is prohibited by the Land Use Act
Only the Minister of FCT can grant consent
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Under Section 22(a) of the Land Use Act, Governor's consent is not required for the creation of a legal mortgage in favour of a person in whose favour an equitable mortgage has already been created with the consent of the Governor [13, 14].

quizzes.scenario: Alhaji Musa has three separate mortgages with the same bank on three different properties in Benin City. He approaches the bank to redeem only one of the mortgages. The bank refuses, insisting he must redeem all three.
options: The bank is right as consolidation is automatic
The bank is wrong unless there is an express covenant to consolidate
The bank is wrong because the PCL prohibits consolidation
The bank is right if the properties are in the same state
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Section 115 of the Property and Conveyancing Law (PCL) and Section 17 of the Conveyancing Act (CA) prohibit the consolidation of mortgages unless a contrary intention is expressed in the mortgage deeds [15, 16].

quizzes.scenario: A mortgage deed contains a clause stating that the mortgagor's right to redeem the property is extinguished if the loan is not repaid within exactly five years.
options: This is a valid "term certain" clause
This is a void clog on the equity of redemption
This is valid under the Mortgage and Property Law
This is valid only for company debentures
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: The principle "once a mortgage, always a mortgage" dictates that any provision in a mortgage contract that clogs or removes the mortgagor's equity of redemption is void [17, 18].

quizzes.scenario: A bank sells a mortgaged property in Lagos after the power of sale has arisen and become exercisable. The bank's managing director buys the property through a proxy at the public auction.
options: The sale is valid as it was a public auction
The sale is voidable as a mortgagee cannot sell to itself or its agents
The sale is valid if the price was fair
The sale is valid if the mortgagor was notified
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: A mortgagee must act in good faith and is prohibited from selling the mortgaged property to itself, its servants, agents, or privies to prevent conflict of interest [19, 20].

quizzes.scenario: After a valid sale of a mortgaged property for N50 million, the bank finds that the total debt (principal, interest, and costs) is N40 million.
options: The bank can keep the N10 million as profit
The bank must pay the N10 million to the state government
The bank must pay the N10 million balance to the mortgagor
The bank must use the balance to buy another property for the mortgagor
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Upon the conclusion of a sale, the mortgagee becomes a trustee of the proceeds and must pay any residue to the mortgagor after settling prior encumbrances, costs of sale, and the mortgage debt [21-23].

quizzes.scenario: Mr. John mortgages his property in Ikeja, Lagos. The bank's solicitor suggests creating the mortgage by way of a "charge by deed expressed to be by way of legal mortgage."
options: This is not possible in Lagos
This is valid under Section 16 of the MPL
This creates only an equitable mortgage
This requires the appointment of a receiver to be legal
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Under Section 16 of the Mortgage and Property Law (MPL) of Lagos State, a legal mortgage of a leasehold can be created by a charge by deed expressed to be by way of legal mortgage [24, 25].

quizzes.scenario: A bank grants an additional loan to Mr. Kalu, secured by the same property already under a legal mortgage to the bank. The solicitor prepares a document to "up-stamp" the mortgage.
options: Fresh Governor's consent is required for the new amount
Governor's consent is not required for up-stamping
Up-stamping is prohibited in Nigeria
Up-stamping creates a new mortgage that replaces the old one
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: The Supreme Court held in Owoniboys Technical Services Ltd v. UBN Ltd that Governor's consent is not required for the up-stamping of a mortgage deed when the parties and the property remain the same [26, 27].

quizzes.scenario: A bank wishes to appoint a receiver for a mortgaged property in Lagos because the mortgagor has defaulted on interest payments for three months. The mortgage was created by deed.
options: The bank must apply to the High Court for an order
The bank can appoint a receiver out of court
A receiver can only be appointed if the mortgagor dies
Only the mortgagor can appoint a receiver
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: A mortgagee, where the mortgage is by deed and the power of sale has arisen, has the statutory power to appoint a receiver of the income of the mortgaged property without recourse to the court [28-30].

quizzes.scenario: A mortgage deed for a property in Kano (CA State) is created by sub-demise. The bank's solicitor insists on adding a clause where the mortgagor appoints the bank as his attorney to sell the property upon default.
options: This clause is illegal
This is a "Trust Declaration"
This is a "Remedial Device" necessary to sell the legal estate in CA states
This clause is only for equitable mortgages
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: In CA jurisdictions, a mortgagee by sub-demise lacks the legal reversion to convey the full title. An Irrevocable Power of Attorney or Trust Declaration is needed as a remedial device to enable the mortgagee to sell the mortgagor's entire interest [31-33].

quizzes.scenario: Mr. Bassey fails to pay his mortgage debt. The bank obtains a "foreclosure nisi" order from the court. Two months later, the bank finds a buyer and attempts to complete the sale.
options: The sale is valid as the court has intervened
The bank cannot sell until the foreclosure is made absolute after six months
The bank can sell immediately after the order nisi
The bank must wait for twelve months
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: A foreclosure order is granted in two stages; the second stage (foreclosure absolute) is usually granted six months after the nisi order, and only then is the equity of redemption extinguished [34-36].

quizzes.scenario: A bank takes actual possession of a mortgaged factory after default. The bank fails to maintain the machinery, which then deteriorates significantly.
options: The bank is not liable as it is the owner
The bank is liable to account for the deterioration due to its negligence
The mortgagor must pay for the repairs
Taking possession is illegal in Nigeria
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: A mortgagee in possession is liable to account to the mortgagor not only for actual profits but also for any loss or damage to the property resulting from the mortgagee's gross negligence [37].

quizzes.scenario: Mr. Bello executes a legal mortgage in favour of a bank in Abuja. He is a natural person, and the bank is a limited liability company. The solicitor proceeds to stamp the deed.
options: Stamping is done at the FCT Internal Revenue Service
Stamping must be done at the Federal Inland Revenue Service (FIRS)
No stamping is required for mortgages in Abuja
Stamping is done at the CAC
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Where a corporate body (the bank) is a party to an instrument, stamp duty is payable to the Federal Government through the FIRS [38, 39].

quizzes.scenario: A mortgage deed specifies an interest rate of 20%, but adds that if the mortgagor pays on or before the due date, the bank will accept 18%.
options: This is a void penal clause
This is a valid clause "by way of encouragement"
Both rates are illegal under the CBN guidelines
The bank must accept 18% regardless of when payment is made
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Courts distinguish between a penalty (increasing rate for late payment) and encouragement (stipulating a higher rate but accepting a lower one for punctual payment), the latter being valid [40].

quizzes.scenario: Mr. Dapo has paid off his mortgage debt in full to a bank in Ogun State. He now wants to clear the encumbrance from his title at the Land Registry.
options: He needs a "Deed of Release" or "Statutory Receipt"
He only needs a letter from the bank manager
He must apply for a court order of discharge
The mortgage clogs his title forever
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Under the Property and Conveyancing Law (PCL), a mortgage can be discharged by a deed of release, surrender, or a statutory receipt endorsed on the mortgage deed [41, 42].

quizzes.scenario: A bank's search report on a company-owned property reveals an existing "floating charge" in favour of another bank. The solicitor advises on the priority.
options: A floating charge always takes priority over a legal mortgage
A legal mortgage created without notice of a restriction in the floating charge takes priority
Floating charges are not recognized in Nigerian law
Priority is determined by the date of incorporation
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: A legal mortgage generally enjoys priority over an earlier equitable interest (like a floating charge) if the legal mortgagee is a bona fide purchaser for value without notice of any negative pledge clause in the floating charge [43, 44].

quizzes.scenario: Chief Emeka presents a Deed of Assignment for registration at the Ikeja Land Registry. The Registrar notices that while the deed is properly executed and stamped, it does not have a survey plan attached. What is the legal implication of this omission?
options: The Registrar will register the deed but require the plan later
The Registrar shall not register the assignment
The deed is void for all purposes
The Registrar will charge a double registration fee
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: By virtue of Section 12(3) and Section 101 of the Land Registration Law (LRL) of Lagos State, the submission of a survey plan is a condition precedent to the registration of any document under the law, and the Registrar shall not register any assignment or sub-lease unless the land has been surveyed to the satisfaction of the Surveyor-General [1], [2], [3], [4].

quizzes.scenario: A prestigious law firm in Lagos wishes to conduct an electronic search and download information directly from the Land Information Management System (LIMS). What must the firm do first to be permitted to do this?
options: Apply for a court order
Obtain a letter of accreditation after paying prescribed fees
Get written permission from the Governor
Submit a copy of their Tax Clearance Certificate
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Under Section 25 of the LRL and LIMS procedures, law firms and other organizations must pay prescribed fees and be issued a Letter of Accreditation to conduct searches or download information directly on the LIMS [5], [6].

quizzes.scenario: Mr. Kunle granted a sub-lease of his property in Lekki to a tech company for a fixed term of 4 years. The company's solicitor insists on applying for Governor's consent before registration. Advise the company.
options: Governor's consent is mandatory for all sub-leases
