Governor's consent is only required if the term is 5 years or above
No registration is needed for a 4-year term
Consent of the Local Government is sufficient
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: According to Section 42 of the LRL and Section 8, while a sub-lease of 3 years and above requires registration, Governor's consent is only mandatory for a sub-lease of 5 years and above, or one with an option to renew for an aggregate of 5 years or more [3], [7], [4], [8].

quizzes.scenario: An investor submits an Irrevocable Power of Attorney relating to the transfer of land in Epe for registration. The Registrar observes that the Governor's consent has not been endorsed on the document. What action should the Registrar take?
options: Register it as a revocable Power of Attorney
Reject the document for registration
Register it but withhold the Land Certificate
File it in the Day List only
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Section 57 of the LRL expressly provides that the Registrar shall not accept for registration any irrevocable Power of Attorney on which Governor's consent has not been endorsed [9], [10], [11].

quizzes.scenario: A family in Ikorodu with 15 principal members wants all their names to be registered as family representatives at the Land Registry to prevent "land grabbers" from selling their land. How many members can be registered?
options: All 15 members
Not more than 4 members
Not more than 10 members
Exactly 2 members
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Under Section 89(5) of the LRL, the Registrar shall not entertain an application for registration of family representatives where the number of representatives exceeds ten persons [12], [13], [14].

quizzes.scenario: A Deed of Assignment was executed in London between two Nigerians regarding a property in Badagry. For this document to be accepted for registration in Lagos, whose attestation must be attached?
options: A London Police Officer
A Nigerian or foreign Judge, Magistrate, or Notary Public
Any British citizen of good standing
The Nigerian High Commissioner only
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Section 76(2) of the LRL requires that a document executed outside Nigeria must have a certificate attached indicating it was signed in the presence of a Nigerian or foreign Judge, Magistrate, Justice of the Peace, Notary Public, or Consul [11], [15], [16].

quizzes.scenario: Segun is a registered holder of a large parcel of land in Magodo. He wishes to sell only two plots out of the six plots covered by his title. What must he do before the Registry can register the new interest?
options: Simply execute a Deed of Assignment for the two plots
Sub-divide the land first
Apply for a new C of O for the whole land
Obtain a sworn affidavit of partial sale
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Sections 62 and 63 of the LRL provide that the transfer of part of a registered land shall not be allowed unless the holder has first sub-divided the land, after which the new interest can be registered [17].

quizzes.scenario: A dispute arises over a property in Somolu. An aggrieved party files a caveat at the Land Registry on July 1st, 2026. If no further action is taken or re-entered, when will this caveat cease to be effective?
options: January 1st, 2027
October 1st, 2026
August 1st, 2026
It never expires
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Under the LRL and Lagos High Court Rules, the lifespan of a caveat is 3 months from the date of its filing unless it is re-entered or a probate action is pending [18], [19], [20], [21].

quizzes.scenario: Mrs. Shola is an illiterate trader who wishes to assign her interest in a shop at Balogun Market. She affixes her thumbprint to the Deed in her house in the presence of her two children. Is this execution valid for registration?
options: Yes, two witnesses are sufficient
No, it must be executed in the presence of a Magistrate, Notary Public, or Commissioner for Oaths
Yes, provided an illiterate jurat is included
No, illiterates cannot assign land interests
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Section 76(3) of the LRL mandates that where a grantor is an illiterate, the document must be executed in the presence of a Magistrate, Judge, JP, Notary Public, or Commissioner for Oaths and be duly attested by them [22], [23], [15], [16].

quizzes.scenario: A developer buys land from a registered holder under the LIMS. Later, a third party claims they had an unregistered equitable interest in the land from 2018. Can this unregistered interest defeat the developer's registered title?
options: Yes, because the interest was earlier in time
No, a registered purchaser for value is not affected by unregistered interests
Yes, if the developer had constructive notice
Only if the third party is currently in possession
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Section 111 of the LRL provides that the interest of a registered holder is indefeasible and a registered purchaser for value is not affected by express or implied notice of any unregistered interest of a previous holder [24].

quizzes.scenario: A foreign diplomat from Ghana wants to buy a residential building in Ikoyi. What specific consent must be sought before the interest is validly transferred?
options: Consent of the Governor of Lagos State
Consent of the Minister of Foreign Affairs
Consent of the National Council of State
Consent of the President of Nigeria
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: By virtue of Section 46 of the Land Use Act, which is integrated into property practice under the LRL, where a foreigner is an assignee, the consent of the National Council of State must be sought [25], [26].

quizzes.scenario: A Registrar at the Lagos Land Registry is confused about the interpretation of a complex clause in a trust deed submitted for registration. What is the Registrar's lawful course of action?
options: Reject the document immediately
Apply to the High Court for direction
Ask the Attorney General for an opinion
Use personal discretion to register it
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Section 110 of the LRL empowers the Registrar, when in doubt or encountering difficulty in relation to any question of law or fact, to apply to the court for direction [27], [28].

quizzes.scenario: Following the death of a sole executor, his own executor, Mr. Ben, wishes to take over the administration of the original testator's estate in Lagos. How is this appointment described?
options: Appointment by Implication
Appointment by Operation of Law
Substitutional Executorship
Appointment by Court
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Under Section 8 of the Administration of Estates Law (AEL) of Lagos, this is a "Chain of Representation" or appointment by operation of law, where the executor of a sole or last surviving executor becomes the executor of the original testator [29], [30].

quizzes.scenario: A family appoints 8 representatives for their registered land. One of the representatives dies. The remaining 7 members sell a portion of the land and execute the deed. Is the sale valid under the LRL?
options: Yes, a majority is enough
No, it must be executed by all representatives appearing on the register
Yes, provided the family head is among the 7
Only if the death was not reported to the Registry
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Section 92 of the LRL states that a disposition of family property shall not be valid if it is executed by a number of family representatives less than those whose names appear on the register [31], [14].

quizzes.scenario: A company, Zenith Estates Ltd, has three existing mortgages with ABC Bank on three different properties. The bank insists that Zenith cannot redeem one property without redeeming all three. What is this practice called?
options: Up-stamping
Consolidation
Tacking
Foreclosure
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: This is the practice of "Consolidation of Mortgages," which is permitted under Section 52 of the LRL (and Section 28 of the MPL) provided it is registered in the Registry [7], [32], [33]-[34].

quizzes.scenario: A client has been in undisturbed possession of a piece of private land in Epe for 13 years. The original owner, who holds a registered title, now wants to evict him. Can the client claim title?
options: No, possession must be for 20 years
Yes, by adverse possession after 12 years
No, registered titles cannot be defeated by possession
Only if the original owner died
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Section 112 of the LRL provides that the holding of land may be acquired by adverse possession against an individual after a period of 12 years (and 20 years against the State) [35], [36], [24], [37].

quizzes.scenario: A solicitor submits a Deed of Sub-lease for registration but provides a version in German as the parties are from Germany. He promises to provide an English summary. What should the Registrar do?
options: Register it based on the summary
Refuse registration
Register it and charge a translation fee
Accept it but do not issue a Land Certificate
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Under Section 346 (refusal of registration), the Registrar may refuse registration of documents not in the English language unless accompanied by a translation certified by the relevant Embassy or High Commission [12].

quizzes.scenario: A Deed of Assignment is submitted for registration. The Registrar notices that it was executed 90 days after Governor's consent was granted. What is the consequence?
options: The deed is void
The deed is invalid
The applicant will be liable for a penalty for late registration
The Governor must grant a new consent
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Sections 26 and 28 of the LRL provide that registrable instruments must be registered within 60 days of execution or the grant of Governor's consent, and failure to do so attracts a fine/penalty [38], [39], [40], [41].

quizzes.scenario: A 17-year-old inherits a registered property in Victoria Island and wishes to sell it to pay for his university education abroad. How should the transaction be handled for Registry purposes?
options: He can sign the deed personally as it is for "necessaries"
He must be represented by a duly appointed Guardian
He must wait until he is 21
He must get a court order only
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Sections 94(3), (4) and 95 of the LRL provide that for the purpose of dealing in land, a minor must be represented by a Guardian duly appointed for that purpose, who must produce evidence of appointment [42], [43], [44].

quizzes.scenario: A Registrar mistakenly registers two different people as holders of the same plot of land in Ajah. What is the statutory remedy for this error?
options: The first person to register wins
The Registrar must cancel both and start over
Rectification of the Register
The Governor must decide who the owner is
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Under Sections 96-100 of the LRL, "Mistaken Registration" where two or more persons are mistakenly registered as holders of the same land is a ground for Rectification of the Register [45]-[46].

quizzes.scenario: Chief Ebuka died testate in Lagos on July 1, 2026. His eldest son, Chidi, discovers the original Will in a locked safe on July 10, 2026. Chidi is unsure of the procedure for lodging the Will at the Probate Registry. What is the time limit within which Chidi must deliver the Will to the Probate Registry in Lagos?
options: Within 14 days of discovery
Within 3 months of knowledge of death
Within 21 days of death
Within 6 months of discovery
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: The rule under Order 62 Rule 1 of the Lagos State High Court (Civil Procedure) Rules 2019 is that any person in custody of a Will must deliver it to the Probate Registry within three months of gaining knowledge of the testator's death. [1]

quizzes.scenario: Alhaji Musa died in Abuja on August 1, 2026, leaving a valid Will. The named executors are eager to begin the administration to preserve the estate. What is the earliest date from the death of Alhaji Musa that the Probate Registry in Abuja can grant probate to the executors?
options: August 22, 2026
August 15, 2026
August 8, 2026
September 1, 2026
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: The rule is that probate or Letters of Administration with the Will annexed may not be granted until after seven (7) days of the death of the testator. [2, 3]

quizzes.scenario: Mr. Thompson died testate, but all the executors named in his Will predeceased him. His estate includes a large residuary portion held in trust for his minor grandchildren. Who among the following has the highest priority to apply for Letters of Administration with the Will annexed?
options: The next of kin of the deceased
Any creditor of the deceased
Any residuary legatee or devisee holding in trust for any other person
The Administrator-General
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: According to the order of priority for Letters of Administration with the Will annexed, a residuary legatee or devisee holding in trust for any other person ranks first when there is no executor to act. [4, 5]

quizzes.scenario: Following the reading of a Will in Lagos, a daughter of the deceased, Ada, believes the Will is a forgery and intends to stop the executors from obtaining probate. What is the appropriate document she should file at the Probate Registry to prohibit the grant?
options: A Writ of Summons
A Citation
A Caveat
An Affidavit of Due Execution
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Any person who wishes to ensure that no grant is sealed without notice to himself may enter a Caveat (Notice to Prohibit) in the registry to make the process contentious. [6-8]

quizzes.scenario: An interested party filed a caveat against a probate application in the FCT High Court on January 10. The executors have not yet issued a warning. If no further action is taken by either party, when will this caveat naturally expire?
options: April 10
July 10
January 10 of the following year
October 10
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: The rule is that a caveat ceases to be effective after six months in Abuja (or three months in Lagos) unless it is re-entered. [7, 9]

quizzes.scenario: After a caveat was filed in Lagos, the executors issued a "Warning" to the caveator to disclose his interest. The caveator failed to enter an appearance or serve a summons for directions. How many days does the caveator have to respond before the caveat may cease to be effective?
options: 14 days
21 days
8 days
30 days
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: The rule under Order 63 Rule 18(8) of the Lagos Rules and Order 60 of the Abuja Rules is that a caveator has 8 days to enter an appearance or respond to a warning/citation. [9-11]

quizzes.scenario: A Will is presented for probate in Lagos, but the Registrar notices that the attestation clause is completely missing, although the testator and two witnesses signed at the end. What must the Registrar require before admitting the Will to probate?
options: The Will must be re-executed
An Affidavit of Due Execution from one of the attesting witnesses
The Will must be declared invalid
A new Will must be drafted
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Where there is no attestation clause or the clause is insufficient, the Registrar shall require an Affidavit of Due Execution to be sworn by one of the attesting witnesses or another person present during execution. [12, 13]

quizzes.scenario: Probate was granted to two out of four named executors in 2024 because the other two were minors at the time. In 2026, the remaining two executors turn 18 and wish to join the administration. What type of grant should they apply for?
options: A fresh Grant of Probate
Letters of Administration de bonis non
Double Probate
Grant Durante Minore Aetate
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Where an initial grant is made and a subsequent grant is applied for by executors who were previously unable to join (e.g., due to being minors), the subsequent grant is called a Double Probate. [14, 15]

quizzes.scenario: Mr. Dele died in Lagos with only N85,000 in his savings account and no real property. His widow wants to claim the money without the rigour of full probate. Which law provides a simplified procedure for this?
options: The Wills Law of Lagos State
The Administration of Estates Law of Lagos State
The Administration of Estates (Small Estate Payments Exemption) Law 2005
The Marriage Act
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: In Lagos, an estate comprising only personal property not exceeding N100,000 in value is a "Small Estate" governed by the Administration of Estates (Small Estate Payments Exemption) Law 2005. [8, 16]

quizzes.scenario: A legal dispute has arisen regarding the validity of a Will. The estate includes a poultry farm with thousands of birds that require daily management. What limited grant can the court make to preserve the assets while the suit is pending?
options: Grant Durante Absentia
Grant Pendente Lite
Grant Durante Minore Aetate
Double Probate
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: A Grant Pendente Lite is a limited grant made pending the determination of a suit over a Will or entitlement to a grant, essentially to preserve the estate. [17, 18]

quizzes.scenario: An executor who was named in a Will begins selling off the deceased's cars and collecting rent from tenants without applying for probate. After 4 months, he still hasn't applied. What is the legal status of this executor?
options: Executor by representation
Administrator ad litem
Executor de son tort
Trustee of the estate
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: An executor or person who intermeddles with the estate without authority (such as before obtaining probate) is an "inter-meddler" known as an Executor De Son Tort. [19-21]

quizzes.scenario: A Bini man died intestate in Edo State. His brother immediately sues a tenant to recover arrears of rent owed to the deceased. The tenant's lawyer challenges the suit's competence. What is the specific customary restriction on such an action?
options: The suit must be brought by the eldest son only
No action can be maintained until after the second burial
The suit requires the consent of the Enogie
The brother must first obtain a C of O
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Under Bini native law and custom, no action can be maintained in respect of a deceased man's estate until after the performance of the second burial rites. [22]

quizzes.scenario: An administrator in Lagos has just been granted Letters of Administration. He is worried about personal liability for the deceased's unknown debts. What should his lawyer advise him to do to be relieved of liability before distributing assets?
options: Apply for a court indemnity
Resign as administrator
Advertise the grant and a time frame for claims
Transfer all assets to a trust corporation
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: To avoid liability to unknown creditors, Personal Representatives should advertise the grant and provide a time frame for claimants to make their claims known before distribution. [23]

quizzes.scenario: An executor in Lagos wants to know how often he is required by law to file an account of his administration at the Probate Registry. What is the correct frequency?
options: Every 6 months
Every 12 months
Only at the completion of administration
Whenever a beneficiary asks
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: In Lagos and Rivers States, the law requires every Executor or Administrator to file an account of his administration every 12 months from the date of the grant until completion. [24, 25]

quizzes.scenario: Probate was granted to Mr. A in Lagos. It is later discovered that the deceased had a block of flats in Kaduna. Mr. A wants to sell the flats in Kaduna. What procedure must he follow to make the Lagos probate effective in Kaduna?
options: Apply for a fresh probate in Kaduna
Apply for resealing of the Lagos grant in the Kaduna High Court
File a Writ of Summons in Kaduna
Execute a Deed of Assignment directly
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Under the Probate Resealing Act, a grant made in one state must be resealed in the High Court of another state where the deceased's property is located to be effective there. [26-28]

quizzes.scenario: A testator's estate is found to be insolvent. The executors need to pay funeral expenses, wages of domestic staff, and the cost of probate. In what order should these be paid according to the Administration of Estates Law?
options: Wages first, then funeral expenses
Funeral expenses, then testamentary/admin expenses, then wages
Probate costs first, then creditors
All are paid simultaneously pro-rata
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: For an insolvent estate, the order of priority under Part 2 of the Schedule to the AEL is: 1. Funeral expenses; 2. Testamentary and administration expenses (probate costs); 3. Rates and taxes; 4. Wages. [29]

quizzes.scenario: An executor wishes to transfer a house specifically devised to a beneficiary in a Will in Lagos. The beneficiary wants to know which document will formally vest the legal title in him. What should the executor execute?
options: A Deed of Assignment
A Contract of Sale
A Vesting Assent
A Power of Attorney
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: In states with their own Wills Law (like Lagos), a Personal Representative must execute an Assent in writing to vest the legal estate of real property in a beneficiary. [30-32]

quizzes.scenario: Two executors are administering an estate. One executor decides to sell a piece of land belonging to the estate without informing the other. He signs the Deed of Assignment alone. What is the effect of this conveyance?
options: It is valid as executors have joint and several authority
It is void as executors must act jointly for real property
It is valid only if he is the eldest executor
It is voidable at the option of the other executor
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: While power to sell personal property is joint and several, no executor has the power to sell or convey real property (realty) without the concurrence of all other executors. [33, 34]

quizzes.scenario: A person dies intestate leaving a widow, three adult children, and an aged father. They all want to be administrators. What is the maximum number of administrators the court can grant Letters of Administration to in Lagos?
options: One
Two
Four
Seven
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: The rule under Section 24(1) of the Administration of Estates Law of Lagos is that probate or administration shall not be granted to more than four persons in respect of the same property. [35-37]

quizzes.scenario: A caveator entered an appearance to a warning, making the probate application contentious. The matter went to trial, and the court pronounced the Will invalid. What happens to the estate?
options: The court will appoint a new executor
The Administrator-General takes over automatically
The deceased is presumed to have died intestate
The estate is forfeited to the state
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: When a Will is not proved or is declared invalid, the presumption is that the deceased died intestate, and the property devolves according to the relevant personal law or intestacy rules. [38]

quizzes.scenario: Chief Okon, resident in Ikeja, executed his will at age 19 disposing of his duplex in Maryland. His relatives challenge the will's validity on the ground of age.
options: The will is valid because the testator has attained the age of 18
The will is invalid because the testator is under 21
The will is valid only if it is a privileged will
The will is voidable at the instance of the executors
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Pursuant to Section 3 of the Wills Law of Lagos State, a person who has attained the age of 18 years has the legal capacity to make a valid will, unlike the Wills Act 1837 which prescribes 21 years. [1, 2]

quizzes.scenario: During the execution of Mr. Ade's will in Abuja, he signed the document while Witness A was looking at him, but Witness B was in the restroom. Witness B returned and signed after Mr. Ade acknowledged his signature.
options: The will is valid as both witnesses eventually signed
The will is invalid because both witnesses were not present at the same time when the testator signed or acknowledged
The will is valid because it was executed in Abuja under the Wills Act
The will is valid if the executors prove it in solemn form
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Section 9 of the Wills Act 1837 requires that the testator's signature must be made or acknowledged in the presence of at least two witnesses present at the same time. [3-5]

quizzes.scenario: Chief Bada's will leaves a specific legacy of a gold watch to his longtime driver, Musa. Musa is one of the two attesting witnesses to the will.
options: Musa will receive the gold watch as the will is valid
The entire will is invalid because a beneficiary witnessed it
The will is valid but the gift to Musa is void
Musa can only receive the gift if the executors allow it
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Under Section 15 of the Wills Act and Section 8 of the Wills Law of Lagos, a beneficiary or the spouse of a beneficiary who attests to a will loses any benefit under that will, although the will itself remains valid. [6, 7]

quizzes.scenario: Mr. Osula, a Bini man subject to customary law, devises his "Igiogbe" (principal residence) to his youngest daughter in his will. His eldest son, who performed all burial rites, challenges this.
options: The daughter takes the house because the will is the last wish
The devise of the Igiogbe is invalid and it passes to the eldest son
The will is entirely void
The daughter and son will share the house equally
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Section 1(1) of the Wills Law of Lagos and similar provisions in the old Western Region (applicable in Edo) restrict the power to dispose of property subject to customary law; under Bini custom, the Igiogbe must go to the eldest son. [8, 9]

quizzes.scenario: Alhaji Yunusa, a devout Muslim in Oyo State, dies leaving a will that gives 80% of his estate to a local orphanage, leaving his statutory heirs with 20%.
options: The will is valid as he has testamentary freedom
The will is invalid because he is a Muslim
The disposition is limited to one-third of his estate for non-heirs
The will is valid if the heirs consent
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Section 3(1)(b) of the Wills Law of Oyo State (and similar laws in Northern states) mandates that a person subject to Islamic law cannot dispose of more than one-third of their estate to persons who are not their Islamic heirs. [10-12]

quizzes.scenario: Mr. Emeka's will gives "my 2023 Mercedes Benz S-Class with registration number ABC 123" to his nephew. Two months before his death, Emeka sold the car to buy a boat.
options: The nephew gets the boat
The nephew gets the cash value of the car
The gift fails by ademption
The executors must buy a similar car for the nephew
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Specific legacies are subject to the doctrine of ademption; if the specific item described in the will no longer exists in the testator's estate at the time of death, the gift fails. [13-15]

quizzes.scenario: Dr. Kalu's will leaves his library to his son, Obinna. Obinna dies in a car accident one week before Dr. Kalu. Obinna is survived by a daughter, Chichi.
options: The gift lapses and falls into the residue
The gift goes to Chichi
The gift goes to Obinna's widow
The gift fails for uncertainty
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Under Section 33 of the Wills Act and Section 24 of the Wills Law of Lagos, the doctrine of lapse does not apply where a gift is made to a child of the testator who predeceases the testator but leaves an issue surviving the testator. [16, 17]

quizzes.scenario: Segun makes a valid will in 2020. In 2022, he marries Funmi under the Marriage Act. Segun dies in 2023 without making a new will.
options: The 2020 will is valid
The will is revoked by the subsequent statutory marriage
The will is valid if Funmi consents to it
The will is only partially revoked
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Section 18 of the Wills Act and Section 11 of the Wills Law of Lagos provide that a will is revoked by the operation of law upon the subsequent solemnization of a statutory marriage, unless made in contemplation of that marriage. [18, 19]

quizzes.scenario: Mrs. Obi gave clear instructions to her lawyer for her will while she was sane. On the day of execution, she was in a semi-conscious state but recognized she was signing the document her lawyer prepared.
options: The will is invalid due to lack of sound disposing mind at execution
The will is valid under the rule in Parker v Felgate
The will is valid only if a doctor witnessed it
The will is voidable
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Under the rule in Parker v Felgate, a will is valid if the testator was of sound mind when giving instructions, the will was prepared in accordance with those instructions, and at the time of execution, the testator understood they were executing the document previously instructed. [20-22]

quizzes.scenario: A soldier on active military service in a counter-insurgency operation in Borno State writes his will on a piece of paper and has it witnessed by only one fellow soldier.
options: The will is invalid for lack of two witnesses
The will is valid as a privileged will
The will is valid only if it relates to real property
The will must be proved in solemn form
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Section 11 of the Wills Act and Section 276 of the Armed Forces Act allow military personnel on active service to make "privileged wills" which are exempt from the strict formal requirements of Section 9, such as the need for two witnesses. [23, 24]

quizzes.scenario: In his will, Chief Dele gives "N5,000,000 to be paid from my savings account with First Bank Plc" to his daughter. At his death, the account only has N1,000,000.
options: The daughter gets only N1,000,000
The gift fails entirely by ademption
The executors must pay the N4,000,000 balance from the general estate
The daughter gets nothing as the source is insufficient
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: This is a demonstrative legacy (a gift from a specific pool). Unlike specific legacies, demonstrative legacies do not adeem; if the specific source is insufficient, the balance is satisfied out of the general estate. [25, 26]

quizzes.scenario: Mr. Johnson, wanting to revoke his will, calls his lawyer on the phone and instructs him to burn the original will kept in the lawyer's safe. The lawyer does so immediately.
options: The will is validly revoked by destruction
The will is not revoked because the destruction was not in the testator's presence
The will is revoked by the oral declaration
The lawyer is liable for professional negligence
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Section 20 of the Wills Act and Section 13 of the Wills Law of Lagos require that for a will to be revoked by destruction, the act must be performed by the testator or by someone in their presence and by their direction. [27, 28]

quizzes.scenario: After executing his will, Mr. Peters decides to change a beneficiary's name. He crosses out the old name and writes the new one, but does not sign next to the change.
options: The change is valid
The entire will becomes invalid
The change is ineffective and the original text remains if apparent
The will must be re-published by the executors
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Section 21 of the Wills Act provides that no alteration made after execution shall be valid unless the alteration is executed in the same manner as a will (signed by the testator and witnesses in the margin). [29, 30]

quizzes.scenario: In Lagos, a testator devises a house in Lekki to his son. After probate is granted, the son wants to register his title at the Land Registry.
options: The son uses the Probate grant to register his title
The son must obtain an Assent executed by the executors
The son must enter a Contract of Sale with the executors
Title passes automatically upon death
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: In states with their own Wills Law like Lagos, a grant of probate does not automatically vest legal title in a devisee; an "Assent" in writing signed by the personal representatives is required to vest the legal estate in the beneficiary. [31-33]

quizzes.scenario: A father (age 70) and his son (age 35) die in a plane crash, and it is impossible to determine who died first. The father's will leaves everything to the son.
options: The son is presumed to have died first
The father is presumed to have died first
Both are presumed to have died at the same instant
The property passes to the government as bona vacantia
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Under the doctrine of Commorientes and Section 164(2) of the Evidence Act 2011, where two people die simultaneously, the elder is presumed to have predeceased the younger for the purpose of determining title to property. [16, 34, 35]

quizzes.scenario: Mr. Coker dies in Lagos, leaving a multi-million naira estate but made no provision for his wife of 30 years in his will.
options: The wife has no remedy as the will is the testator's wish
The wife must challenge the will within 21 days of death
The wife can apply for reasonable financial provision within 6 months of the grant of probate
The will is automatically void
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Section 2 of the Wills Law of Lagos allows certain dependents (spouse/children) to apply to the court for reasonable financial provision if the will fails to make such, provided the application is within 6 months of the grant of probate. [36, 37]

quizzes.scenario: A testator appoints his lawyer as an executor. The will does not contain a charging clause. The lawyer spends 100 hours on probate matters and sends a bill to the estate.
options: The lawyer is entitled to his full professional fees
The lawyer is not entitled to fees for professional work as executorship is a labor of love
The lawyer can charge 10% of the estate value
The lawyer is entitled to fees only if the beneficiaries agree
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Personal representatives are generally not entitled to remuneration for their services (it is a "labour of love") unless the will specifically contains a "charging clause" or the court orders it. [38-40]

quizzes.scenario: Husband and Wife execute separate wills leaving their respective estates to each other, agreeing that the survivor will leave the combined estate to their only daughter. After the Husband dies and the Wife inherits, she makes a new will leaving everything to her new boyfriend.
options: The Wife can change her will at any time
This is a mutual will and the Wife's new will is a breach of trust
The daughter has no remedy
The Husband's will is revived
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Mutual wills are created where two people agree to make wills in a particular form and not to revoke them; once the first person dies, a trust is created that binds the survivor's estate in equity. [41]

quizzes.scenario: Mr. Taiwo's will disposes of his cars and houses to specific people but does not mention his N50 million fixed deposit or his shares in various companies. The will also lacks a residuary clause.
options: The executors take the remaining assets
The remaining assets are distributed among the named beneficiaries proportionally
There is partial intestacy regarding the bank account and shares
The will is void for being incomplete
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: A residuary clause is essential to dispose of any property not specifically mentioned; without it, any omitted or failed gifts result in "partial intestacy" and are distributed according to the rules of intestate succession. [42-44]

quizzes.scenario: Mr. Akpan makes a will in 2015. In 2018, he executes a document titled "Codicil" that changes his executors but ends with the phrase "in all other respects I confirm my said will".
options: The 2015 will is revoked
The 2015 will is republished as at the date of the codicil
The codicil is invalid if not physically attached to the will
The will must be re-signed by the witnesses
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: A codicil that refers to and confirms an existing will effectively "republishes" that will, meaning the will is treated as having been made on the date the codicil was executed. [45-47]

quizzes.scenario: Mr. Emeka, a solicitor, was briefed to prepare a Deed of Assignment for a property in Ikeja valued at N200 million. Without a written agreement on fees, he sent a bill of N30 million (15%) to the client. The client objects, claiming the fee is excessive.
options: The fee is valid as it is below 20%
The fee is invalid as it must comply with Scale A of the 2023 Remuneration Order in the absence of a written agreement
The solicitor can charge any amount since the property is in a high-brow area
The client must pay because the solicitor has a lien on the property
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Rule 48 of the RPC and the Legal Practitioners (Remuneration for Business, Legal Services and Representation) Order 2023 provide that in the absence of a written agreement, a solicitor must charge according to the prescribed scale (Scale A for conveyancing) [1-3].

quizzes.scenario: A solicitor acts for both the Vendor and the Purchaser in a land sale in Lagos where the title is sound and there is no conflict. He issues a single bill of N5 million to the Purchaser for representing both parties.
options: The solicitor is entitled to double the scale fee
The solicitor may only act for one party in property matters
The solicitor should charge a reduced fee as prescribed by the 2023 Remuneration Order for acting for both parties
The solicitor cannot charge any fee for acting for both parties
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: While a single solicitor can represent both parties under certain conditions (Smith v Mansil), the 2023 Remuneration Order prescribes specific billing rules for such scenarios to avoid excessive charging [1, 2, 4].

quizzes.scenario: Chief Kola appoints his lawyer, Mr. Segun, as the sole Executor of his Will. Mr. Segun spends six months administering the estate and sends a bill for "professional services" to the beneficiaries. The Will contains no charging clause.
options: Mr. Segun is entitled to 10% of the estate value
Mr. Segun is not entitled to professional fees as the role is a "labour of love"
The beneficiaries must pay based on quantum meruit
Mr. Segun can sue for his fees under the Legal Practitioners Act
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: In Nigerian property law, the role of an executor is generally gratuitous (a labour of love); a professional executor can only charge for services if the Will contains an express "charging clause" [5, 6].

quizzes.scenario: A solicitor, Ms. Amina, completes an assignment for a client and charges N2 million as professional fees. She fails to include Value Added Tax (VAT) in her bill but later demands it from the client.
options: Ms. Amina is not entitled to VAT as it was not in the original bill
VAT is not applicable to legal services in Nigeria
VAT is a compulsory 7.5% charge on professional fees which the solicitor must remit to the FIRS
Only the client can pay VAT directly to the government
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Under the VAT Act (as amended by the Finance Act), professional services rendered by legal practitioners are vatable at 7.5%, and the solicitor is a "VATable person" required to charge and remit it [7].

quizzes.scenario: Mr. Dele, a solicitor, receives N50 million from a purchaser as a deposit for a property sale. He is acting as a "stakeholder." Before completion, he deducts his N5 million professional fee from the deposit.
options: The deduction is valid as he has performed the work
The deduction is invalid as he holds the money as a trustee for both parties and must not touch it until completion
He can only deduct the fee if the vendor agrees
He should have deducted 10% instead of 5%
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: When a solicitor receives a deposit as a "stakeholder," he acts as an agent for both parties and holds the money as a trustee; he cannot deduct his fees from such funds until the transaction is completed and the party entitled to the money is determined [8, 9].

quizzes.scenario: A client refuses to pay a solicitor's bill of charges for a mortgage transaction completed two months ago. The solicitor immediately files a suit at the High Court to recover the fees.
options: The suit is proper as the debt is liquidated
The suit is premature because the solicitor must first serve a signed bill of charges and wait for one month
The solicitor should have filed at the Magistrate Court
The solicitor must wait for six months before suing
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Under Section 16 of the Legal Practitioners Act, a solicitor cannot sue for professional fees until the expiration of one month after a signed bill of charges has been served on the client [2, 10].

quizzes.scenario: A solicitor, Mr. Okoro, prepares a Power of Attorney for a donor. He charges the client ad valorem (based on property value) instead of a fixed fee. The client contests the billing method.
options: Ad valorem is the correct method for all property documents
A Power of Attorney usually attracts a fixed fee for preparation, distinct from the ad valorem stamp duty paid on assignments
The solicitor can choose any method he prefers
The solicitor should not charge for a Power of Attorney
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: While some documents like Deeds of Assignment attract fees/duties ad valorem, a simple Power of Attorney is a deed poll that traditionally attracts a fixed or nominal professional fee for drafting [11, 12].

quizzes.scenario: An Administrator-General is appointed to manage the unrepresented estate of a deceased man in Lagos. After six months, the Administrator-General claims 15% of the estate's income as remuneration.
options: The claim is valid under the Administration of Estates Law
The claim is invalid as the statutory limit for remuneration in Lagos is 10%
Remuneration for Administrator-General is 5% only
The Administrator-General is not entitled to any remuneration
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Under Order 63 Rule 10 of the Lagos High Court Rules, the court may order remuneration for an administrator not exceeding 10% of the income of the estate [13].

quizzes.scenario: Mr. John, an executor, employs a firm of solicitors to help prove a Will. One of the partners of the firm witnessed the Will. The Will has a charging clause. The firm sends a bill for their services.
options: The firm is entitled to the full bill
The firm loses the right to benefit from the charging clause because a partner witnessed the Will
Only the partner who witnessed the Will loses his share
The charging clause is void for the whole firm
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Under Section 15 of the Wills Act and the rule in Re Pooley, a witness to a Will (or their spouse) cannot benefit from the Will, including benefits under a charging clause for professional services [5, 14].

quizzes.scenario: A solicitor, Barrister Tunde, prepares a completion statement for a sale of land. He fails to list the "out-of-pocket" expenses for the search at the Land Registry, intending to bill them separately later.
options: He can bill them at any time
The completion statement must be clear and include all disbursements and solicitor's charges to ensure accountability
Only the purchase price should be in the statement
He can only charge for his time, not for the search
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: A completion (financial) statement must be clear and comprehensible, showing all sums paid/received, disbursements (like search fees), and solicitor's charges to ensure accountability between the solicitor and the client [15, 16].

quizzes.scenario: In a mortgage transaction, the mortgagee's solicitor carries out an extensive search and valuation. He sends the bill for these services to his client, the Bank (Mortgagee), who in turn demands payment from the Mortgagor (Borrower).
options: The Bank must pay its own solicitor
The Mortgagor is traditionally responsible for the costs of creating the mortgage, including the mortgagee's solicitor's fees
The solicitor should not have billed for a search
The fees should be split equally between the Bank and the Mortgagor
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: In Nigerian mortgage practice, it is the duty of the Mortgagor (borrower) to bear the costs of the transaction, including the professional fees of the mortgagee's (lender's) solicitor [17, 18].

quizzes.scenario: A solicitor, Mrs. Obi, agrees to handle a land perfection process for a "success fee" of 50% of the land's value if the title is successfully registered. The client later claims the fee is unconscionable.
options: The fee is valid as it was a mutual agreement
The fee is likely unconscionable and violates Rule 48 of the RPC regarding adequate but not excessive fees
Success fees are mandatory in land matters
The solicitor is entitled to 100% since she took the risk
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: While solicitors can enter into fee agreements, Rule 48 of the RPC and the 2023 Remuneration Order mandate that fees must be adequate and not excessive; a 50% success fee for mere perfection is generally considered unconscionable [2, 3].

quizzes.scenario: A lawyer, Mr. Salami, is asked by a client to "under-state" the purchase price in a Deed of Assignment from N50 million to N10 million to reduce the solicitor's scale fee and government taxes. Mr. Salami complies to save the client money.
options: Mr. Salami has acted in the best interest of his client
Mr. Salami has committed professional misconduct by aiding tax evasion and illegal conduct
This is a standard practice called "tax avoidance"
Only the client is liable for the under-statement
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: A lawyer has a duty not to knowingly engage in illegal conduct or aid tax evasion; under-stamping by reducing the consideration in a deed violates Rule 15(2) of the RPC and the Stamp Duties Act [19, 20].

quizzes.scenario: An administrator is appointed in Abuja (FCT) for an intestate estate. He applies to the court for remuneration for his labour in realizing the assets.
options: He is entitled to 10% of the property value
He is entitled to a maximum of 5% on the amount of the realized property under the Abuja Rules
He must work for free
He can only charge for out-of-pocket expenses
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Under Order 62 Rule 38 of the Abuja (FCT) High Court Rules, an administrator's remuneration shall not exceed 5% of the amount of the property realized and accounted for [13].

quizzes.scenario: A solicitor, Mr. Kayode, represents a client in the purchase of 10 different plots of land from 10 different vendors. He issues one single bill of charges based on the total value of all 10 plots.
options: This is the correct method for bulk purchases
He must issue separate bills of charges for each distinct transaction to comply with Scale A
He should charge based on the number of pages in the deeds
Bulk billing is only allowed for corporate clients
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Each land transaction with a different vendor is a separate "business" or "service" under the 2023 Remuneration Order and should be billed individually according to the value of each specific property [1, 2].

quizzes.scenario: A client pays his solicitor, Barrister Joy, N5 million for the "perfection of title" (Consent, Stamping, Registration). Barrister Joy uses N2 million for the process and keeps N3 million as her "service fee" without disclosing the actual costs.
options: She is entitled to the balance as a bonus
She has a duty to provide a full and accurate statement of all disbursements and account for the balance
She only needs to show the registered deed
The client should have asked for a refund earlier
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: A solicitor owes a fiduciary duty to account for all monies collected for disbursements; keeping the balance of funds intended for government fees without disclosure is a breach of the duty to account under Rule 23 of the RPC [10, 21].

quizzes.scenario: A solicitor, Mr. Bassey, completes a lease agreement for a term of 5 years. He calculates his fee using the scale for "Sales/Assignments" instead of the scale for "Leases."
options: The two scales are the same
The fee is invalid as Scale A provides a different computation for leases based on the annual rent
The client must pay whatever the solicitor demands
Lease billing is not regulated in Nigeria
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: The 2023 Remuneration Order distinguishes between assignments (based on total value) and leases (usually based on the annual rent reserved); using the wrong scale leads to an incorrect and potentially excessive bill [1, 2].

quizzes.scenario: A lawyer, Ms. Faith, is asked to draft a Will for a dying man in a hospital. She charges N500,000. She brings a portable POS machine and insists the man pays before she starts drafting.
options: This is standard "upfront" billing
This may be considered undignified and a violation of the duty to act with devotion in an emergency
She is right to ensure she is paid
Payment should only be made after the man dies
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: While lawyers are entitled to fees, the manner of demand must be professional and dignified; insisting on immediate payment via POS at a deathbed may breach the ethical standards of devotion and the spirit of the RPC in property/probate matters [22, 23].

quizzes.scenario: Mr. Ibrahim, a solicitor, charges a client for a "Search Report" on a property. The client discovers the solicitor never visited the Land Registry but relied on an old report from another lawyer.
options: The solicitor is entitled to the fee for "expert opinion"
The solicitor is guilty of professional negligence and cannot charge for a service not actually performed
The client must prove he suffered a loss first
The solicitor should split the fee with the other lawyer
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: A solicitor has a duty of due diligence and competence under Rule 14 and 16 of the RPC; charging for an investigation not actually conducted is fraudulent and a breach of professional duty [10, 19].

quizzes.scenario: A solicitor, Barrister Funke, shares her professional fee for a land transaction with the Estate Agent who introduced the client to her.
options: This is a valid "referral fee"
This violates Rule 3 of the RPC which prohibits fee-splitting with non-lawyers
It is allowed if the client consents
Only the agent is liable for the breach
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Rule 3 of the RPC prohibits a legal practitioner from sharing legal fees with a non-lawyer (such as an estate agent); this is a strict ethical rule in Nigerian property practice [10, 22].

quizzes.scenario: Chief Benson purchased a plot of land in 2010 for N10,000,000 and spent N2,000,000 on structural improvements. In 2024, he sold the property for N25,000,000 through a solicitor who charged N1,000,000 in fees. What is the Capital Gains Tax (CGT) payable by Chief Benson?
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: The rule is that CGT is 10% of the chargeable gain. Chargeable gain = Consideration (N25m) minus Allowable Expenses (Acquisition N10m + Improvements N2m + Solicitor Fees N1m = N13m). Total Gain = N12,000,000. 10% of N12m is N1,200,000. [1-3]

quizzes.scenario: Red Rock Ltd, a construction company, purchased a warehouse from Mr. Okoro, an individual resident in Enugu. Which authority is responsible for collecting the stamp duty on the Deed of Assignment?
options: Enugu State Board of Internal Revenue
Federal Inland Revenue Service (FIRS)
Nigeria Revenue Service
Joint Tax Board
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: The rule under Section 4(1) of the Stamp Duties Act is that the Federal Government (through FIRS/NRS) has the sole authority to collect duties on instruments where one of the parties is a company. [4, 5]

quizzes.scenario: Alhaji Musa executed a Deed of Assignment for a property in Lagos on June 1, 2024. His solicitor intends to pay the ad valorem stamp duty. Within how many days must this payment be made to avoid a penalty?
options: 21 days
40 days
30 days
60 days
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: The rule under Section 23(3)(a) of the Stamp Duties Act is that ad valorem duty must be paid within 30 days from the date of execution. Fixed duty documents have 40 days. [6, 7]

quizzes.scenario: The Registered Trustees of Hope Foundation, a public charitable organization, sold one of their properties to raise funds for their hospital project. Are they liable to pay Capital Gains Tax on this disposal?
options: Yes, at a reduced rate of 5%
No, they are exempted
Yes, because it is a disposal of an asset
Only if the gain exceeds N50,000,000
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Under Section 26 of the Capital Gains Tax Act, ecclesiastical, charitable, and educational organizations of a public character are exempted from CGT. [8]

quizzes.scenario: Barrister Tunde handled a complex land acquisition for a client and charged a professional fee of N5,000,000. What is the rate of Value Added Tax (VAT) he must charge and remit to the FIRS?
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: While the VATA originally stated 5%, the Finance Act increased the VAT rate to 7.5%, which applies to professional services rendered by legal practitioners. [9]

quizzes.scenario: Mrs. Adebayo owns a residential building in Ikoyi, Lagos. She recently received a bill consolidating her Ground Rent, Tenement Rate, and Neighborhood Improvement Levy. Under what law is this consolidated charge imposed?
options: Lagos State Tenancy Law
Land Use Charge Law of Lagos State 2018
Lagos State Land Registration Law
Property and Conveyancing Law
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: The Land Use Charge (LUC) in Lagos is a consolidation of Ground Rent, Tenement Rate, and Neighborhood Improvement Levy under the Land Use Charge Law 2018. [10, 11]

quizzes.scenario: A tenant paid 6 years of rent in advance to a landlord for a commercial property in Abuja. How will this income be treated for tax purposes?
options: It is tax-free as it is a capital sum
It is treated as Personal Income Tax for the year of receipt
It is subject only to VAT
It is treated as a gift
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: The rule under the Personal Income Tax Act is that advance rent for 5 years and above is taxable as personal income in the nature of an income of the individual. [12, 13]

quizzes.scenario: During a land dispute trial, a party seeks to tender an original Deed of Assignment that has not been stamped. What is the legal implication of this document?
options: It is completely void and of no effect
It is admissible but carries no weight
It is inadmissible in evidence for any purpose except in criminal proceedings
It can only be admitted if it is also registered
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Section 22 of the Stamp Duties Act provides that unstamped documents are inadmissible in civil proceedings. However, the court may allow payment of the penalty to cure the defect. [14, 15]

quizzes.scenario: Mr. John created a legal mortgage over his property in favor of Zenith Bank to secure a loan of N50,000,000. Is this transaction subject to Capital Gains Tax?
options: Yes, at 10% of the loan amount
No, it does not amount to a disposal of assets
Only if the bank sells the property
Yes, because title is transferred
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Section 7(4) of the CGT Act provides that the conveyance or transfer of an asset by way of security (mortgage) does not constitute a disposal for CGT purposes. [16, 17]

quizzes.scenario: A purchaser of land in Lagos is processing his Governor's Consent. He is informed he must pay 8% of the assessed value of the property to the Ministry of Land. What is this specific tax/fee called?
options: Registration Fee
Consent Fee
Stamp Duty
Capital Gains Tax
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Consent fee is the mandatory payment made to the State Government to obtain the Governor's Consent for alienation of interest in land under Section 22 of the Land Use Act. [18]

quizzes.scenario: A solicitor is calculating the registration fee for a Deed of Assignment in Lagos State. What is the current prescribed rate for this fee?
options: 1% of the value
2% of the value
3% of the value
5% of the value
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: In Lagos State, the registration fee for an instrument is calculated at 3% of the assessed value of the property. [19]

quizzes.scenario: In computing CGT for a property sold in Jos, the owner wants to deduct the cost of painting the building himself and the cost of an appeal against the tax assessment. Are these allowable?
options: Both are allowable
Only the painting is allowable
Neither is allowable
Only the cost of the appeal is allowable
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: The rule is that deductions must be "wholly, exclusively and necessarily" incurred for the asset. Personal labor is not allowed (Oram v Johnson), and costs of appealing a tax assessment are not incidental to the acquisition or disposal. [2, 20, 21]

quizzes.scenario: An individual resident in Port Harcourt executes a Power of Attorney (POA). What type of stamp duty does this document typically attract?
options: Ad valorem duty based on property value
7.5% VAT
A fixed nominal rate
10% CGT
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Documents like a Power of Attorney or a Contract of Sale (before assignment) typically attract a fixed nominal stamp duty rather than ad valorem duty. [22, 23]

quizzes.scenario: Mr. Emeka works in a law firm in Lagos Island but has his principal place of residence in Abeokuta, Ogun State. To which authority should his Personal Income Tax be paid?
options: Lagos State Internal Revenue Service
Ogun State Board of Internal Revenue
Federal Inland Revenue Service
Both Lagos and Ogun State proportionally
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Under the Personal Income Tax Act, a taxable person is liable to pay tax in the State where he has his principal place of residence on the 1st day of January of the assessment year. [24, 25]

quizzes.scenario: The Federal Government compulsorily acquired Chief Kole's land in 2022 for public interest and paid him N100,000,000 as compensation. If this land originally cost Chief Kole N40,000,000, is he liable for CGT?
options: No, because it was a compulsory acquisition
Yes, because compulsory acquisition is a disposal of assets
Only if he buys another land
No, it is treated as a gift
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Section 6(1) of the CGT Act includes "compulsory acquisition" in the definition of disposal of assets where a capital sum is derived. Therefore, CGT is payable on the gain. [16, 26]

quizzes.scenario: A client asks his solicitor to state a lower consideration in the Deed of Assignment than what was actually paid, to "save money" on consent fees. What is the ethical implication for the solicitor?
options: It is a smart way to help the client with tax avoidance
It is professional negligence
It contravenes Rule 15(3) of the RPC as it involves aiding illegal conduct/tax evasion
It is permissible if the client gives written indemnity
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: A solicitor's role in circumventing taxes through misrepresentation of consideration is a breach of Rule 15(3) of the Rules of Professional Conduct (RPC) regarding acting within the bounds of the law. [2, 27]

quizzes.scenario: Mr. Williams died leaving a large estate in Lagos worth N500,000,000. His executors are applying for probate. What is the rate of estate duty payable in Lagos?
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: In Lagos State, estate duty is calculated at 10% of the gross value of the deceased's real and personal property. [28]

quizzes.scenario: Upon the death of a testator, his real estate in Kaduna vests in his appointed executors. Does this transfer trigger an immediate liability for Capital Gains Tax?
options: Yes, because it is a transfer of interest
No, devolution of assets on personal representatives is not a disposal
Only if the executors sell it later
Yes, at a fixed rate
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Section 8(4) of the CGT Act provides that the devolution of assets on a personal representative upon the death of a person does not constitute a disposal of assets for CGT purposes. [17]

quizzes.scenario: A deceased person in Lagos left N80,000 in a savings account but no real estate. His children wish to claim the money. Which law allows them to do so without full probate, and are they liable for estate duty?
options: Administration of Estate Law; Yes
Small Estate Payments Exemption Law; No
Wills Law; Yes
Trustee Investment Act; No
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Under the Administration of Estates (Small Estate Payments Exemption) Law 2005 of Lagos, estates not exceeding N100,000 (excluding real estate) are exempt from probate and the applicant is not required to pay estate duty. [29, 30]

quizzes.scenario: A company sold its office building on August 15th, making a significant profit. According to the Finance Act amendment to the CGT Act, by what date must the company file its return and pay the CGT?
options: Within 30 days of sale
By 30th June or 31st December of that year
Within 90 days of sale
By April of the following year
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: The Finance Act amendment to Section 2 of the CGT Act requires that every person disposing of a chargeable asset must compute and pay the tax not later than 30th June and 31st December of the year of disposal. [26]

quizzes.scenario: Chief Ajayi died leaving a Will where he bequeathed his multi-million naira estate to his 10-year-old grandson, Tunde. He appointed his best friend, Mr. Okoro, as the sole Executor. Mr. Okoro applies for probate in Lagos. What is the likely position of the Probate Registrar?
options: Probate will be granted to Mr. Okoro as the sole Executor since he was specifically named in the Will.
Probate will be refused entirely because a minor cannot be a beneficiary of such a large estate.
Probate will be granted to Mr. Okoro, but the court will exercise its discretion to appoint an additional personal representative or a trust corporation to act with him.
Mr. Okoro must wait until Tunde turns 18 before he can apply for probate.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: The rule under Section 24(2) of the Administration of Estates Law (AEL) of Lagos provides that where there is a minority interest or a life interest, probate or administration shall be granted to either a trust corporation or to not less than two individuals. [1, 2]

quizzes.scenario: Dr. Musa died testate in Kaduna, appointing four of his children as Executors. After the grant of probate, the first two executors decided to sell a parcel of land in Victoria Island, Lagos, which formed part of the estate, to settle outstanding medical bills without the consent of the other two executors. What is the legal status of this sale?
options: The sale is valid because executors have joint and several authority over the estate.
The sale is void because all executors to whom probate was granted must join in the conveyance of real estate.
The sale is valid provided the proceeds are actually used to pay the estate's debts.
The sale is voidable at the instance of the other executors within 6 months.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: The rule under Section 4(2) of the AEL Lagos (and similar provisions in other states) provides that while executors may act severally regarding personal property, all executors to whom probate was granted must concur in the sale or conveyance of real property. [3]

quizzes.scenario: Alhaji Shehu died intestate in Kano. His eldest brother, without applying for Letters of Administration, took over the deceased's transport business and started collecting rents from the deceased's tenants to "preserve the estate." He spent some of this money on his own children's school fees. What is his legal status?
options: He is a lawful representative acting as a next-of-kin to preserve the estate.
He is a trustee of the estate by implication of law.
He is an executor de son tort and is personally liable for any waste or conversion of the assets.
He is a statutory administrator until the court appoints a formal one.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: The rule is that any person who intermeddles with the estate of a deceased person without authority (grant of probate or letters of administration) is an executor de son tort and is liable for any loss, waste, or conversion under Section 18 of the AEL. [4, 5]

quizzes.scenario: Mrs. Effiong died leaving a house in Asokoro, Abuja. Probate was granted to her executors in the FCT High Court. The executors now wish to vest the house in the designated beneficiary, her daughter. What is the required document to pass the legal title in Abuja?
options: A Deed of Assignment executed by the executors.
A formal written Assent signed by the executors.
No separate document is required; the legal title passes to the beneficiary immediately upon the grant of probate.
A Vesting Order from the High Court of the FCT.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: The rule is that under the Wills Act 1837 (which applies in the FCT), there is no statutory requirement for an Assent; the beneficiary's title is perfected upon the grant of probate. The requirement for an Assent only applies in states that have enacted their own Wills Laws, such as Lagos. [6, 7]

quizzes.scenario: Mr. Balogun died testate in Lagos, leaving a commercial building in Ikeja to his son, Kunle. Probate was granted to the executors. Kunle wants to sell the building immediately to a third party. The executors have not issued any further documents to him. Can Kunle validly transfer legal title to the buyer?
options: Yes, because as a devisee under a proved Will, he is the legal owner.
No, because in Lagos, the legal title remains in the executors until they execute a formal written Assent in his favor.
Yes, provided he shows the buyer the CTC of the Will and the Grant of Probate.
No, because he must first be registered as the owner at the Land Registry using the Probate Grant.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: The rule under Section 3 and Section 40(1) of the AEL Lagos is that real property vests in the personal representatives, and legal title only passes to a beneficiary when a formal written Assent is executed by the personal representatives. [6, 8]

quizzes.scenario: Chief Obi was the sole surviving executor of the estate of Mr. Emeka. Chief Obi died recently, leaving a Will in which he appointed his daughter, Amaka, as his sole executrix. Amaka has obtained probate for Chief Obi's Will. What is her relationship to Mr. Emeka's estate?
options: She has no relationship to Mr. Emeka's estate and must apply for Letters of Administration with Will Annexed.
She is the executrix of Mr. Emeka's estate by the chain of representation.
She is an administrator de bonis non of Mr. Emeka's estate.
She is a trustee of Mr. Emeka's estate but not an executrix.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: The rule under Section 8 of the AEL Lagos provides for the "chain of representation," where the executor of a sole or last surviving executor becomes the executor of the original testator, provided the chain is not broken by intestacy or failure to obtain probate. [9, 10]

quizzes.scenario: A prominent Lagos lawyer was appointed as an executor in his client's Will. The Will contains no clause regarding payment for his services. After six months of rigorous administration, the lawyer withdraws N2,000,000 from the estate account as his professional fees. Is this action lawful?
options: Yes, because a lawyer is entitled to be paid for professional services rendered.
Yes, provided the fees are reasonable and at the prevailing market rate.
No, because the office of an executor is gratuitous and he cannot charge unless the Will contains a "Charging Clause."
No, unless he obtains a court order authorizing the payment after the work is done.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: The rule is that executors are generally not entitled to remuneration (it is a "labor of love") unless there is a specific "Charging Clause" in the Will or a court order under Order 63 Rule 10 (Lagos). [11-13]

quizzes.scenario: Mr. Williams was appointed as one of three executors in a complex Will. He is overwhelmed and wishes to renounce his appointment regarding the deceased's foreign investments only, while continuing to manage the local properties. Advice Mr. Williams.
options: He can renounce part of the estate by filing a partial renunciation form.
He can only renounce his appointment if the other two executors agree to take over his duties.
Renunciation must be total; he cannot renounce probate in part while accepting it in another part.
He should apply for a limited grant of probate instead.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: The rule established in Paul v. Moodie is that renunciation of the office of executor must be total and not partial; a person cannot "pick and choose" which parts of the estate to administer. [14]

quizzes.scenario: Madam Kofo died and her Will was read on January 10th. The beneficiaries, eager to receive their shares, filed an action on February 20th to compel the executors to distribute the assets immediately. The executors contend they need more time. Who is correct?
options: The beneficiaries, because the executors must distribute gifts immediately after the Will is proved.
The executors, because they are not bound to distribute the estate until the expiration of one year from the testator's death.
The beneficiaries, provided they can prove they are in urgent financial need.
The executors, but only if they obtain a court order to postpone distribution.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: The rule under Section 47 of the AEL Lagos (the "Executor's Year") provides that a personal representative is not bound to distribute the estate of the deceased before the expiration of one year from the date of death. [15, 16]

quizzes.scenario: In his Will, Mr. Dapo stated: "I desire that my old friend, Mr. John, shall pay all my just debts and funeral expenses out of my cash in the bank, and ensure my children's school fees are settled." He did not use the word "Executor." Is Mr. John an executor?
options: No, he is merely a trustee for the specific purpose of paying debts.
Yes, he is an executor according to the tenor of the Will.
No, the estate is intestate as to the appointment of executors.
Yes, but he must apply for a court order to be formally recognized as an executor.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: The rule is that where a testator fails to expressly appoint an executor but charges a person with the essential duties of an executor (like paying debts and funeral expenses), that person is an "Executor according to the tenor." [17]

quizzes.scenario: An executor discovers that the estate is solvent but there are many debts to be paid. He decides to pay the specific legacies first because those items are already identified. A creditor whose debt is due objects. What is the correct order of deduction?
options: Specific legacies are paid first, then debts, then residuary gifts.
Debts and liabilities must be settled first from the residuary estate, then general legacies, then specific legacies.
All beneficiaries and creditors are paid pro-rata based on the available funds.
Specific legacies are exempt from debt settlement in a solvent estate.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: The rule under Section 38 and Part 1 of the Schedule to the AEL provides that in a solvent estate, the residuary estate is the first fund for paying debts, followed by general legacies, and finally specific legacies. [18]

quizzes.scenario: Mr. Gbadamosi died insolvent. The executors have N5,000,000 left. There is a claim for funeral expenses (N1,000,000), outstanding wages for the deceased's housemaid for the last 3 months (N200,000), and a personal loan from a friend (N4,000,000). What should be paid first?
options: The personal loan must be paid first because it is the largest debt.
All claims must be paid pro-rata.
The funeral expenses must be paid first, followed by testamentary/administration expenses.
The housemaid's wages take priority over everything else.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: The rule under Part 2 of the Schedule to the AEL for insolvent estates is that funeral, testamentary, and administration expenses have absolute priority. [19]

quizzes.scenario: Two executors of an estate in Lagos wish to reward themselves for their hard work. They decide to "appropriate" the deceased's vintage Mercedes Benz (valued at N15m) to themselves in satisfaction of their potential commission, without informing the beneficiaries. Is this valid?
options: Yes, executors have the power to appropriate any part of the estate to any person, including themselves.
No, because executors cannot appropriate assets to themselves without an independent professional valuation and the consent of the beneficiaries.
Yes, provided they file the details in the annual accounts.
No, because executors are never allowed to benefit from the estate assets.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: The rule is that while PRs can appropriate assets under Section 44 AEL, they cannot appropriate to themselves without a valuation by an independent professional valuer to ensure fairness to the beneficiaries. [20]

quizzes.scenario: Chief Egwu, a Bini man, died in Lagos leaving a Will. His son, who is also an executor, immediately sues a tenant for unpaid rent on the deceased's property in Benin City. The tenant objects that the suit is premature. Why?
options: Because the son has not yet been granted probate in Edo State.
Because under Bini native law and custom, no action can be maintained regarding the estate until after the "second burial."
Because rent matters must be handled by the rent tribunal, not the High Court.
Because all executors must join in the suit, not just one.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: The rule, supported by cases like Ovensiri v. Osagiede, is that in the case of a deceased Bini man, no action can be maintained in respect of his estate until after the performance of the second burial rites. [21]

quizzes.scenario: Aminu applied for Letters of Administration for his father's estate. While the application was pending, he sued a trespasser on his father's land, styling himself as the "Administrator of the Estate of Mr. X." He eventually got the grant two months later. What is the effect of the grant on the suit?
options: The grant validates the suit from the beginning under the doctrine of "relation back."
The suit is incurably defective and must be struck out because Aminu had no capacity to sue as "Administrator" before the grant.
The court will allow Aminu to amend the writ to show the date the grant was eventually made.
The trespasser cannot object because Aminu is the rightful heir in any event.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: The rule is that an administrator derives his power from the grant (unlike an executor). Suing in that capacity before the grant is made is a nullity, as established in Ingall v. Moran. The doctrine of relation back only applies if he sued in a different capacity (like "Next of Kin") and later amended it. [22, 23]

quizzes.scenario: Testator appointed three adults and his 15-year-old nephew as executors. The adults apply for probate immediately. What will be the nature of the grant?
options: Probate will be granted to the three adults only; the nephew's appointment is void.
Probate will be granted to all four, but the nephew cannot sign any documents until he is 18.
Probate will be granted to the three adults, with power reserved for the nephew to apply for "Double Probate" when he attains majority.
The grant must be delayed until the nephew is of age to ensure the testator's wishes are fully met.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: The rule under Sections 64 and 65 of the AEL provides that where a minor is appointed alongside adults, probate is granted to the adults, and power is reserved for the minor to join them (via double probate) once he turns 18/21. [24, 25]

quizzes.scenario: Probate was granted to Executors in Oyo State. They discover a property belonging to the deceased in Ikoyi, Lagos. They attempt to use the Oyo probate to register an Assent at the Lagos Land Registry. Will they be successful?
options: Yes, probate granted in one state is valid throughout Nigeria.
No, they must first apply to the High Court of Lagos State to have the Oyo probate "Resealed."
No, they must apply for an entirely new grant of probate in Lagos State.
Yes, provided they pay the required stamp duties and registration fees in Lagos.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: The rule under Section 2 of the Probate (Resealing) Act is that a grant made in one state has no effect on property in another state until it is produced to and resealed by the High Court of that other state. [26, 27]

quizzes.scenario: Letters of Administration (intestate) were granted to the widow of Mr. Bankole. Six months later, an original Will made by Mr. Bankole is discovered in his bank vault. The Will appoints no executors. What is the correct procedure?
options: The widow continues as Administrator but must now follow the terms of the Will.
The widow must apply to "convert" the existing grant into a probate grant.
The existing Letters of Administration must be revoked, and an application made for "Letters of Administration with the Will Annexed."
The Will is ignored because Letters of Administration have already been granted and acted upon.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: The rule is that the discovery of a valid Will after a grant of administration (intestate) is a ground for revocation of the grant, and a new grant (LA with Will Annexed) must be obtained. [28, 29]

quizzes.scenario: A distant cousin of a deceased millionaire in Lagos approaches the Probate Registry, demanding to inspect the annual accounts filed by the executors. The executors refuse to show him. Can he compel them?
options: Yes, probate accounts are public documents accessible to anyone upon payment of a fee.
No, only the court or the Probate Registrar can look at the accounts.
Only if he can satisfy the Probate Registrar that he is a "person interested" in the administration of the estate.
Yes, but he must first obtain an order from the High Court.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: The rule under Order 61 Rule 16(9) of the Lagos Rules (2019) is that the accounts of an estate are open for inspection only to persons who satisfy the Probate Registrar that they have a legitimate interest in the estate. [30, 31]

quizzes.scenario: There are three executors of a Will in Lagos. They wish to issue an Assent to vest a property in the residuary legatee. Only two of the executors are available to sign the document as the third is on vacation. Is the Assent valid?
options: Yes, provided a majority of the executors sign.
No, all executors who proved the Will must sign the Assent to validly pass the legal estate.
Yes, but only if the third executor gives a Power of Attorney to the others.
Yes, but it will only pass an equitable interest until the third executor signs.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: The rule, as stated in Renner v. Renner, is that for an Assent to be valid and effective in passing legal title, it must be signed by all the personal representatives who took out the grant. [7, 32]

quizzes.scenario: Tunde and Eric wish to incorporate a logistics company in Lagos. They are informed that they must primarily deal with a specific agency for registration. Which body oversees the incorporation of all companies in Nigeria?
options: Securities and Exchange Commission
Corporate Affairs Commission
Nigerian Investment Promotion Commission
Federal Inland Revenue Service
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: The Corporate Affairs Commission (CAC) is the chief regulator established under Section 1 of CAMA 2020, responsible for the registration, regulation, and supervision of the formation and incorporation of companies. [1-3]

quizzes.scenario: A legal practitioner with 5 years of post-call experience and 2 years of corporate law practice wants to be appointed as the Registrar-General of the CAC. Is he qualified?
options: Yes, he is a legal practitioner
No, he needs 10 years post-call and 10 years corporate experience
No, he needs at least 10 years post-call and 8 years of corporate law experience
Yes, if the Minister recommends him
