Only small companies are exempted, not all private companies.
These are only required if the company has foreign participation.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Section 377(3) of CAMA 2020 stipulates that the financial statements of a private company need not include the statement of cash flow, changes in equity, value-added statement, and five-year financial summary. [4], [5]

quizzes.scenario: The Board of Directors of MegaCorp PLC has prepared the company's balance sheet. To save time, only the Managing Director signs the document before it is sent to the CAC.
options: The MD's signature alone is sufficient for public companies.
The balance sheet must be signed by at least two directors on behalf of the board.
The balance sheet must be signed by all directors.
The signature of the company secretary is the only legal requirement.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Section 386(1) of CAMA 2020 requires that a company's balance sheet shall be signed on behalf of the board by two of the directors of the company. [6]

quizzes.scenario: Apex Holdings, a company with three subsidiaries, is preparing its year-end accounts. The directors decide not to prepare group financial statements because the subsidiaries operate in different industries.
options: This is a valid reason for non-consolidation.
Group financial statements are mandatory for all holding companies without exception.
Holding companies must prepare group financial statements unless it is impracticable or misleading.
Only the CAC can waive the requirement for group financial statements.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Section 379 of CAMA 2020 provides that if a company has subsidiaries at the end of its financial year, group financial statements shall be prepared, except where it is impracticable, of no value to members, or would be misleading. [7], [8]

quizzes.scenario: Smart Finance Ltd is a small company. The directors want to submit modified financial statements to the CAC to simplify the filing process.
options: Small companies cannot submit modified statements.
Only the profit and loss account can be modified.
Small companies may deliver modified financial statements including an abbreviated balance sheet.
Modified statements are only for companies limited by guarantee.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Section 393 of CAMA 2020 allows a small company to deliver modified financial statements, and Section 350-353 permits the modified balance sheet to be an abbreviated version. [9], [10]

quizzes.scenario: A shareholder of Alpha PLC demands to see the company's last financial statements three weeks before the AGM. The company demands a fee for the copy.
options: The company can charge a maximum of N100 per page.
Any member is entitled to be furnished with a copy on demand and without charge.
Only members who have been shareholders for 2 years have this right.
The right to copies only exists during the AGM.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Under Section 391 of CAMA 2020, any member or debenture holder is entitled to be furnished on demand and without charge with a copy of the company's last financial statements. [11]

quizzes.scenario: NewTech Ltd has just been incorporated. The promoters want to know who is responsible for appointing the first auditors before the company starts business.
options: The Corporate Affairs Commission appoints the first auditors.
The members at the first Annual General Meeting.
The directors may appoint the first auditors at any time before the company commences business.
The company secretary is responsible for the appointment.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Section 401(1) of CAMA 2020 provides that the first auditors of a company may be appointed by the directors at any time before the company is entitled to commence business. [12], [13]

quizzes.scenario: The directors of Prime PLC failed to appoint auditors before the company commenced business. The company has now reached its first AGM.
options: The directors lose their right to vote at the AGM.
The company must pay a fine to the CAC.
The members at the general meeting may appoint the auditors where the directors have failed to do so.
The CAC will automatically appoint an auditor for the company.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Section 401(5) of CAMA 2020 states that if the directors fail to exercise their powers to appoint first auditors, the company in general meeting may appoint them. [12], [13]

quizzes.scenario: Mr. Obi is the Chief Accountant of Fast Foods Ltd. The board wants to appoint him as the company's external auditor to save costs.
options: This is permissible if he is a chartered accountant.
It is allowed if the members pass a special resolution.
An officer or servant of the company is disqualified from being appointed as its auditor.
He can only be an auditor if he resigns as an employee.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Section 403(1)(a) of CAMA 2020 provides that a person shall not be qualified for appointment as an auditor of a company if he is an officer or servant of the company. [14], [15]

quizzes.scenario: Delta Ltd, a private company, wishes to appoint 'Clean Books Accounting Firm' (a body corporate) as its auditor.
options: A body corporate is qualified to be an auditor under CAMA.
Only the CEO of the body corporate needs to be a chartered accountant.
A body corporate is disqualified from being appointed as an auditor of a company.
It is allowed if the firm has at least 5 years of experience.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Section 403(1)(c) of CAMA 2020 explicitly disqualifies a body corporate from being appointed as an auditor of a company. [14], [15]

quizzes.scenario: Chief Benson is a debtor to Zenith PLC, owing the sum of N750,000. The board of Zenith PLC wants to appoint him as the auditor.
options: He is qualified if he pays N250,000 of the debt first.
Any person who is a debtor to the company in an amount exceeding N500,000 is disqualified from being its auditor.
Debtors are only disqualified in public companies.
Debtors are never disqualified; only employees are.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Section 403(1)(d) of CAMA 2020 provides that a person who is a debtor to the company or its subsidiary in an amount exceeding N500,000 is disqualified from appointment as an auditor. [14], [15]

quizzes.scenario: The members of Retail PLC are dissatisfied with the current auditor and want to remove him before his tenure expires at the next AGM.
options: Auditors cannot be removed before the expiration of their term.
The auditor can only be removed by the CAC.
The company may by ordinary resolution remove an auditor before the expiration of his term, subject to special notice.
Removal requires a special resolution (75% majority).
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Section 409(1) of CAMA 2020 provides that a company may by ordinary resolution remove an auditor before the expiration of his term of office, but Section 410 requires special notice for such a resolution. [16], [17]

quizzes.scenario: A shareholder of Global PLC intends to propose a resolution to remove the company's auditor. He wants to know the notice requirement for such a proposal.
options: 14 days ordinary notice.
21 days special notice.
Special notice of at least 28 days is required for a resolution to remove an auditor.
30 days notice to the CAC only.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Under Section 410(1) of CAMA 2020, special notice of at least 28 days is required for a resolution at a general meeting of a company to remove an auditor before the expiration of his term. [16], [17]

quizzes.scenario: The external auditor of Bright Star Ltd resigned by giving a written notice. He also requisitioned the directors to call a meeting to explain the circumstances of his resignation.
options: The directors can ignore the request if they find it embarrassing.
The auditor has no power to requisition a meeting.
A resigning auditor may requisition the directors to convene an EGM to consider his explanations.
Only the CAC can call a meeting for a resigning auditor.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Section 413 of CAMA 2020 allows a resigning auditor to requisition the directors to convene an extra-ordinary general meeting for the purpose of receiving and considering his explanations regarding the resignation. [18], [19]

quizzes.scenario: The directors of a newly incorporated company appointed the first auditors. They now wish to fix the auditors' remuneration at a board meeting.
options: Remuneration can only be fixed at an AGM.
If auditors are appointed by directors, their remuneration may be fixed by the directors.
The CAC must approve any remuneration fixed by directors.
Remuneration must always be fixed by the audit committee.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Section 408(1)(a) of CAMA 2020 provides that in the case of an auditor appointed by the directors, the remuneration may be fixed by the directors. [20], [15]

quizzes.scenario: During an audit of Construction Ltd, the auditor noticed a major discrepancy in the asset register but failed to report it, leading to a loss for the company.
options: The auditor is not liable if he acted in good faith.
The auditor is only liable to the CAC.
An auditor is liable in negligence if he fails to exercise care, diligence, and skill, resulting in loss to the company.
The directors are solely liable for all financial discrepancies.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Section 415 of CAMA 2020 provides that an auditor is liable for negligence where his failure to exercise the duty of care, diligence and skill results in loss or damage to the company. [21]

quizzes.scenario: The auditor of Tech Solutions PLC has been in office for 10 consecutive years. The board wants to reappoint the same firm for another 5 years.
options: CAMA allows auditors to serve indefinitely.
The SEC Code of Corporate Governance requires rotation of audit firms every 10 years with a 7-year cooling-off period.
Audit firms must rotate every 3 years.
Rotation is only required if the auditor is a sole practitioner.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: According to Paragraph 33 of the SEC Code 2011, audit firms must not be retained continuously for more than ten years, and a cooling-off period of seven years applies before re-appointment. [22]

quizzes.scenario: TopTier PLC is a public company. The board wants to know if they are legally required to establish an audit committee.
options: Audit committees are optional for all companies.
Every public company shall establish an audit committee.
Only quoted companies on the Stock Exchange need an audit committee.
Small companies must have an audit committee.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Section 404(3) of CAMA 2020 provides that every public company shall have an audit committee. [23], [24]

quizzes.scenario: The board of Alpha PLC is constituting its audit committee. They propose to include the Managing Director and the Board Chairman as members.
options: This is the standard practice for public companies.
The committee must consist of 5 members, including three members and two non-executive directors.
The committee must be made up of only executive directors.
Only the company secretary can be a member.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Section 404(3) of CAMA 2020 stipulates that the audit committee shall consist of five members comprising three members (shareholders) and two non-executive directors. [25], [26]

quizzes.scenario: A member of the audit committee of Beta PLC is demanding a monthly sitting allowance and remuneration for his services.
options: Committee members are entitled to the same remuneration as directors.
Audit committee members are not entitled to remuneration.
Remuneration is determined by the MD.
Only the non-executive directors on the committee are paid.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Section 404(3) of CAMA 2020 specifies that the members of the audit committee are not entitled to remuneration. [27], [26]

quizzes.scenario: The shareholders of Gamma PLC are nominating members for the audit committee. One nominee is a high school graduate with no financial experience.
options: Anyone can be a member as long as they are a shareholder.
All members of the audit committee shall be financially literate.
Only the chairman of the committee needs to be financially literate.
Financial literacy is only required for the company auditor.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Section 404(5) of CAMA 2020 requires that all members of the audit committee shall be financially literate. [25], [26]

quizzes.scenario: One of the members of the audit committee of Delta PLC is not a member of any accounting body. The board is worried this violates the law.
options: All 5 members must be chartered accountants.
At least one member of the audit committee shall be a member of a professional accounting body in Nigeria.
Accounting membership is only for the external auditor.
Only the company secretary must be an accountant.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Section 404(5) of CAMA 2020 requires that at least one member of the audit committee shall be a member of a professional accounting body in Nigeria established by an Act of the National Assembly. [25], [26]

quizzes.scenario: The audit committee of Omega PLC wishes to investigate the company's internal inventory control system after reports of theft.
options: The committee cannot investigate; they only review reports.
The committee may authorize the internal auditor to carry out investigations into any activities of the company.
They must get a court order to investigate.
Only the external auditor can investigate internal systems.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Section 404(7)(g) of CAMA 2020 empowers the audit committee to authorize the internal auditor to carry out investigations into any activities of the company which may be of interest or concern to the committee. [28], [29]

quizzes.scenario: Zenith Ltd was incorporated in February 2023. The directors are unsure if they must file annual returns for the 2023 calendar year.
options: Returns must be filed within 14 days of incorporation.
A company needs not make a return in the year of its incorporation.
Returns are only filed every two years.
Only public companies file returns in their first year.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Section 417 of CAMA 2020 provides that a company needs not make an annual return in the year of its incorporation. [28], [29]

quizzes.scenario: Phoenix Ltd held its AGM on June 1st. The company secretary wants to know the deadline for filing the annual returns with the CAC.
options: Within 14 days after the AGM.
Within 42 days after the AGM.
By the 31st of December of that year.
Within 6 months after the AGM.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Section 421(1) of CAMA 2020 provides that the annual return shall be completed and delivered to the Commission within 42 days after the Annual General Meeting for the year. [30]

quizzes.scenario: Green Ventures Ltd is a small company. The directors want to know which form to use for filing their annual returns.
options: CAC Form 1.1
CAC Form 19
CAC Form 21
CAC Form 7
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: CAC Form 19 is the prescribed form for filing annual returns for companies under the Companies Regulations. [31], [32]

quizzes.scenario: A private company, RedRose Ltd, has failed to file its annual returns for the past 12 consecutive years. The CAC has sent a notice of its intention to take action.
options: The CAC will remove the directors and appoint new ones.
The CAC will increase the company's share capital.
Failure to file annual returns for 10 years is a ground for the Commission to strike the name of the company off the register.
The company will be converted to a public company.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Under Section 425(3) of CAMA 2020, where a company fails to file annual returns for a period of 10 consecutive years, the Commission may strike the name of the company off the register. [33]

quizzes.scenario: BlueChip PLC is preparing its annual returns. The secretary insists that certain documents must be annexed to the return before filing.
options: Only the certificate of incorporation is needed.
A certified copy of the balance sheet, profit and loss account, auditors' report, and directors' report must be annexed.
Only the list of shareholders is required.
No annexures are required for public companies.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Section 422 of CAMA 2020 requires that a certified copy of the balance sheet, profit and loss account, auditors' report, and directors' report be annexed to the annual return. [34]

quizzes.scenario: Secure Bank PLC (a banking company) is preparing for its bi-annual filings. The compliance officer mentions a specific statement that must be filed in February and August.
options: Statement of Shareholding.
Statement of Affairs in the form of Schedule 14 of the Act.
Annual Returns.
Directors' Insolvency Statement.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Section 733 of CAMA 2020 (and Section 753) requires banking and insurance companies to file a Statement of Affairs in the prescribed form on the first Monday in February and first Tuesday in August every year. [35]

quizzes.scenario: TinySeeds Ltd has a turnover of N20 million and net assets of N15 million. All directors are Nigerians and it is a private company.
options: It is a large company because it is limited by shares.
It qualifies as a small company under CAMA 2020 and the Finance Act.
It is a small company only if it has less than 5 members.
It is a medium company.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Under Section 394 of CAMA 2020 (as amended by the Finance Act), a company is small if it is a private company, its turnover is not more than N25m, and net assets not more than N12.5m (or N120m/N60m threshold in Section 394(2)). [36], [37]

quizzes.scenario: The directors of MiniScale Ltd (a small company) want to know if they are exempted from auditing their financial statements.
options: Small companies are never exempted from audit.
A small company is exempted from the requirement of auditing its financial statements in a given financial year.
Only companies limited by guarantee are exempted.
Exemption only applies if the company is in its first year.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Section 402(1) of CAMA 2020 provides that a company is exempt from the requirements of this Act relating to the audit of financial statements in respect of a financial year if it qualifies as a small company. [12], [38]

quizzes.scenario: SmallBiz Ltd is a small company. The directors are considering whether they must hold an AGM this year.
options: Every company must hold an AGM without exception.
Small companies and companies with a single shareholder are exempted from holding an AGM.
Small companies must hold an AGM but can do it via Zoom.
AGMs are only for public companies.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Section 237(1) of CAMA 2020 exempts small companies and single-shareholder companies from the requirement of holding an Annual General Meeting. [39], [40]

quizzes.scenario: The accountant of HighGrowth Ltd, a public company, is preparing the year-end accounts. He asks where the audited accounts must be displayed.
options: Only at the registered office.
On the company's website.
In the Federal Gazette.
Only in the company's boardroom.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Section 374(6) of CAMA 2020 requires public companies to display their audited accounts on their websites. [1]

quizzes.scenario: The board of directors of Prime Assurance Ltd (an insurance company) wants to waive the requirement of having an auditor because it is a small company.
options: This is permitted for all small companies.
Insurance and banking companies must at all times have an auditor, regardless of their size.
They can waive it if the CAC gives consent.
Only the CBN can waive it.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Section 402(2) of CAMA 2020 provides that the exemption from audit does not apply to a company that is a government-owned company, a bank, or an insurance company. [12], [38]

quizzes.scenario: A member of Global PLC nominates a new auditor to replace the retiring one at the upcoming AGM. He wants to know the procedure.
options: He must tell the MD on the day of the AGM.
He must give notice to the company at least 28 days before the AGM.
Only the board can nominate auditors.
The nomination must be published in a newspaper 14 days prior.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Under Section 410 of CAMA 2020, special notice of 28 days is required for a resolution appointing as auditor a person other than a retiring auditor. [16], [17]

quizzes.scenario: An auditor of a public company is retiring. The company does not pass any resolution at the AGM to appoint a new one or reappoint him.
options: The office becomes vacant.
The retiring auditor is automatically deemed to be reappointed without any resolution.
The CAC will appoint an auditor.
The company must be wound up.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Section 401(2) of CAMA 2020 provides that at any AGM, a retiring auditor shall be deemed to be reappointed without any resolution being passed unless he is disqualified or a resolution is passed appointing someone else. [41], [13]

quizzes.scenario: Mr. Ade resigned as the auditor of FastTrack PLC. The company needs to fill the vacancy before the next AGM.
options: Only the general meeting can fill a casual vacancy.
The directors may appoint auditors to fill any casual vacancy in the office of auditor.
The CAC must fill the vacancy.
The vacancy must remain until the next AGM.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Section 401(6) of CAMA 2020 provides that the directors may fill any casual vacancy in the office of auditor. [41], [14]

quizzes.scenario: The auditor of Z-Tech Ltd has completed the audit. He wants to know to whom he should address his report.
options: To the Board of Directors.
To the members of the company.
To the Managing Director.
To the Federal Ministry of Finance.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Section 404(1) of CAMA 2020 requires the auditors of a company to make a report to the members on the accounts examined by them. [42], [43]

quizzes.scenario: The CFO of MaxPLC is certifying the audited financial statements. He must state that the statements do not contain any untrue material facts.
options: This is only required for small companies.
This is a statutory corporate responsibility for the CEO and CFO of companies other than small companies.
This certification is done by the auditor, not the CFO.
Certification is only required for tax purposes.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Section 405 of CAMA 2020 requires the CEO and CFO of a company, other than a small company, to certify in each audited financial statement that it does not contain untrue statements of material facts. [44]

quizzes.scenario: A creditor of FailSafe Ltd noticed the company hasn't filed annual returns for 3 years. He wants the CAC to enforce the filing.
options: Only shareholders can ask the CAC to enforce filing.
Within 14 days after the CAC serves notice on the company, a member, creditor or the CAC itself can apply to the court to order compliance.
Creditors have no right to financial information.
Filing returns is purely a voluntary act.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Section 425 of CAMA 2020 allows the Commission, a member, or a creditor to apply to the court for an order directing the company to make good its default in filing annual returns. [33], [45]

quizzes.scenario: SmallScale Ltd, which qualified as a small company in 2022, has just admitted a foreign investor who now owns 20% of its shares.
options: It remains a small company because turnover is low.
It no longer qualifies as a small company because it now has an alien member.
Foreigners are allowed in small companies if they are from ECOWAS.
It becomes a public company automatically.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Under Section 394(3)(d) of CAMA 2020, a small company must not have an alien as a member. [36], [46]

quizzes.scenario: The directors of a company are struggling to prepare their annual returns on time due to a fire at the office. They want more time.
options: Deadlines are strict and cannot be extended.
The company may apply to the Commission for an extension of time to file its annual return.
Extension is only for public companies.
They must pay a double fine to get an extension.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Section 421(2) of CAMA 2020 allows a company to apply to the Commission for an extension of time within which to file its annual return. [30]

quizzes.scenario: The directors of a holding company are aligning the financial years of its subsidiaries. One subsidiary has a different year-end.
options: The subsidiary must keep its different year-end.
The financial year of subsidiaries must align with the holding company unless there are good reasons otherwise.
Alignment is only for tax purposes.
Only the CAC can decide the year-end of a subsidiary.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Section 377(5) of CAMA 2020 provides that the directors of a holding company shall ensure that the financial year of each of its subsidiaries coincides with the company's own financial year. [1]

quizzes.scenario: A director of Mega PLC failed to take all reasonable steps to ensure the company kept proper accounting records.
options: He is not liable if the secretary is qualified.
He is guilty of an offence and liable to imprisonment or a fine unless he acted honestly.
Directors are never personally liable for record-keeping.
Only the CFO is liable for records.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Section 374(7) of CAMA 2020 provides that if a person being a director fails to take all reasonable steps to secure compliance with record-keeping, he commits an offence unless he shows he had reasonable grounds to believe a competent person was charged with the duty. [1], [3]

quizzes.scenario: The auditor of a company is also the person responsible for maintaining the company's register of debenture holders.
options: This is good for coordination.
This person is disqualified from being the company's auditor.
It is allowed in private companies only.
Disqualification only applies to directors.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Section 403(1)(f) of CAMA 2020 disqualifies any person who is responsible for keeping the register of debenture holders from being the auditor of that company. [42]

quizzes.scenario: The annual return of a company limited by guarantee must be in a specific format.
options: It uses the same form as a public company.
It follows the form prescribed in the Ninth Schedule of CAMA.
There is no form; it's a simple letter.
It uses CAC Form 19 with no changes.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Section 420 of CAMA 2020 provides that the annual return by a company limited by guarantee shall be in the form prescribed in the Ninth Schedule. [47]

quizzes.scenario: A public company published an abridged version of its financial statements. It failed to state that the full version had been filed with the CAC.
options: Abridged versions do not need disclosures.
Every publication of abridged financial statements must state that they are abridged and whether full accounts have been delivered to the Commission.
Abridged accounts are illegal.
Only the summary of profits is needed.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Section 389 of CAMA 2020 requires that where a company publishes abridged financial statements, it must state that they are not full accounts and whether full accounts have been delivered to the Commission. [46]

quizzes.scenario: The CAC has sent a default fine notice to the directors of a company that failed to file returns for 2 years.
options: Only the company is liable for the fine.
The company and every director or officer in default are liable to a fine.
The fine is only for the company secretary.
Fines are only for public companies.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Section 425(1) of CAMA 2020 provides that if a company fails to comply with the requirements for annual returns, the company and every director or officer of the company who is in default shall be liable to a penalty. [33], [45]

quizzes.scenario: BlueChip Ltd, a private company, allots 10,000 ordinary shares to Mr. Emeka on June 1. By what date must the company file the return of allotment with the CAC?
options: June 15
June 30
July 1
July 15
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: According to Section 154 of CAMA 2020, as amended by Section 6 of the Business Facilitation Act 2022, a company must file a return of allotment in Form CAC 5 within 15 days of the allotment [1-3].

quizzes.scenario: Zenith Plc intends to issue shares to an expert in exchange for intellectual property. What is the mandatory step regarding the valuation of this non-cash consideration?
options: The directors must value it themselves
An independent valuer must provide a valuation report
The CAC must determine the value
No valuation is required for public companies
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Section 162 of CAMA 2020 requires that where a public company allots shares for a consideration other than cash, the consideration must be valued by an independent valuer who shall submit a report to the company [4-6].

quizzes.scenario: A director at Alpha Ltd suggests issuing 1,000 ordinary shares at a nominal value of N1.00 for N0.80 to attract investors. Is this proposal legally sound?
options: Yes, if authorized by the articles
Yes, if approved by a special resolution
No, it is generally prohibited
Yes, if the court confirms it
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Section 146 of CAMA 2020 strictly prohibits the issuance of shares at a discount, meaning shares cannot be sold for less than their nominal value [7, 8].

quizzes.scenario: Star Ventures Ltd issues N1.00 shares at N1.50. Where should the extra N0.50 per share be recorded in the company's books?
options: General Reserve Account
Retained Earnings
Share Premium Account
Asset Revaluation Account
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Section 145(2) of CAMA 2020 stipulates that when shares are issued at a premium, a sum equal to the aggregate amount of the value of the premiums must be transferred to the "Share Premium Account" [9, 10].

quizzes.scenario: The board of directors of Mega Corp PLC wishes to use funds from the Share Premium Account. Which of the following is a lawful use of these funds?
options: Paying annual directors' fees
Funding a political campaign
Paying up unissued shares to be issued as fully paid bonus shares
Buying a new private jet for the CEO
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Section 145(4)(a) of CAMA 2020 permits the use of the Share Premium Account for paying up unissued shares of the company to be issued to members as fully paid bonus shares [7, 10].

quizzes.scenario: To maintain absolute control, the majority shareholder of XYZ Ltd proposes creating a class of shares that allows 5 votes per share. Is this permissible under CAMA 2020?
options: Yes, if it is a private company
No, weighted shares are generally prohibited
Yes, if the SEC approves
Yes, for all public companies
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Section 140(1) of CAMA 2020 prohibits the issuance of weighted or non-voting shares, providing that every share shall carry one vote except in specific circumstances like preference shares [9, 11].

quizzes.scenario: Mr. Musa, a shareholder in a private company, dies intestate. His son, the administrator of his estate, wishes to be registered as the holder of the shares. What process applies?
options: Allotment
Transfer
Transmission
Forfeiture
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Section 179 of CAMA 2020 provides for the transmission of shares, which is an automatic transfer of ownership by operation of law due to death or bankruptcy of a member [12, 13].

quizzes.scenario: A shareholder in Swift Logistics Ltd has not paid the final call on his shares despite several notices. What action can the directors take if authorized by the articles?
options: Sue for criminal conversion
Forfeit the shares
Sell the company's head office
Increase the nominal value of the shares
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Under Section 165 of CAMA 2020, if a member fails to pay any call or installment on the day appointed for payment, the directors may, if authorized by the articles, pass a resolution for the forfeiture of such shares [14, 15].

quizzes.scenario: Global Tech PLC issues a prospectus for a public offer. Mr. Ade relies on a false statement in the prospectus and suffers a financial loss. Who can he sue for compensation?
options: Only the company
The CAC
The company and every director who authorized the issue
Only the issuing house
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Section 113 of the Investment and Securities Act (ISA) 2025 makes the company, every director, and every person who authorized the issue liable to compensate subscribers for losses resulting from misstatements in a prospectus [16, 17].

quizzes.scenario: A private company, SmallBiz Ltd, wishes to help its manager buy shares in the company by giving him a low-interest loan. Is this allowed?
options: No, financial assistance is always illegal
Yes, if the company's net assets are not reduced below 50% and it is approved by special resolution
Yes, only if it is a public company
No, only banks can do this
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Section 183(4) of CAMA 2020 allows private companies to provide financial assistance for the acquisition of their own shares if their net assets are not reduced or the assistance is from distributable profits, approved by special resolution [18, 19].

quizzes.scenario: BigBank PLC wants to lend money to a client specifically to enable the client to purchase shares in BigBank PLC. Is this transaction valid?
options: Yes, because it is a bank
No, it constitutes prohibited financial assistance
Yes, if the SEC gives a "no objection"
Yes, if the board approves
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Section 183(1) of CAMA 2020 prohibits a public company or its subsidiaries from giving financial assistance, such as a loan, for the purpose of an acquisition of its shares [19-21].

quizzes.scenario: TopTier Ltd decides to buy back 20% of its own issued share capital using its distributable profits. Is this within the legal limit?
options: Yes, the limit is 25%
No, the limit is 15%
Yes, there is no limit for private companies
No, the limit is 5%
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Section 187 of CAMA 2020 limits a share buyback to not more than 15% of the nominal value of the company's issued share capital [22].

quizzes.scenario: After allotting shares to new investors, the secretary of FreshCo Ltd realizes they must issue share certificates. What is the statutory deadline for this?
options: 14 days
1 month
2 months
6 months
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Section 171(1) of CAMA 2020 requires every company to complete and have ready for delivery the certificates of all shares allotted within two months after allotment [2, 23].

quizzes.scenario: Mr. Jones transfers his shares in a public company to Ms. Vera. The transfer is executed on May 1. By what date must the company have the share certificate ready for Ms. Vera?
options: May 15
June 1
July 1
August 1
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Section 171(4) of CAMA 2020 provides that for a transfer of shares, the company must have the share certificate ready for delivery within three months after the date on which the transfer is lodged [23, 24].

quizzes.scenario: A creditor of DebtFree Ltd holds a floating charge over the company's stock-in-trade. The company defaults on its loan. What happens to the charge?
options: It is extinguished
It becomes a fixed charge via crystallization
It remains floating forever
The CAC cancels it
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: A floating charge "crystallizes" and becomes a fixed charge upon the occurrence of a triggering event, such as a default in payment or the appointment of a receiver [25-27].

quizzes.scenario: SecureTrust Ltd creates a legal mortgage over its factory building to secure a debenture. Within how many days must this charge be registered with the CAC?
options: 15 days
30 days
60 days
90 days
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Section 222(1) of CAMA 2020 requires every charge created by a company to be registered with the CAC within 90 days of its creation [28-30].

quizzes.scenario: A company creates a charge but fails to register it with the CAC within the 90-day window. What is the primary legal consequence?
options: The loan is cancelled
The charge is void against the liquidator and other creditors
The directors go to jail immediately
The company is dissolved
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Under CAMA 2020, if a charge is not registered within 90 days, it becomes void against the liquidator and any creditor of the company, and the debt becomes an unsecured debt [29, 31].

quizzes.scenario: ABC Ltd has paid off the loan secured by a registered charge. What document should be filed with the CAC to record this?
options: Form CAC 5
Form CAC 7
Memorandum of Satisfaction
Notice of Allotment
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Section 229 of CAMA 2020 provides for the filing of a Memorandum of Satisfaction with the CAC once the debt for which the charge was given has been paid or satisfied [28, 31].

quizzes.scenario: A debenture holder wants to enforce security. The company has refused to pay for three weeks after a formal demand. What can the debenture holder petition the court for?
options: An increase in share capital
Winding up of the company
Removal of the company secretary
A name change for the company
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Section 571(d) and 572 of CAMA 2020 allow a creditor (including a debenture holder) to petition for the winding up of a company if it is unable to pay its debts after a formal demand is neglected for three weeks [29, 32].

quizzes.scenario: A company issue of debentures is secured by a "Negative Pledge Clause". What is the effect of this clause?
options: It prevents the company from borrowing any more money
It prohibits the company from creating subsequent charges that would rank in priority to the existing charge
It guarantees a 20% interest rate
It makes the debenture convertible to shares
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: A Negative Pledge Clause is a restrictive covenant in a debenture that prohibits the company from creating any subsequent charge over the same assets that would rank equal to or above the current charge [Source Note: Generally practiced in enforcement/priority principles under CAMA].

quizzes.scenario: In the event of winding up, a company's assets are being distributed. Who has priority of payment among the following?
options: Ordinary shareholders
Unsecured creditors
Expenses of the winding up and liquidator's remuneration
Deferred shareholders
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Under the priority of payments in Section 657 of CAMA 2020, the expenses of the winding up, including the liquidator's remuneration, are paid first before other claims [33, 34].

quizzes.scenario: An investor in a public company wishes to buy debentures that can later be turned into ordinary shares. What type of debenture should he look for?
options: Perpetual debentures
Naked debentures
Convertible debentures
Bearer debentures
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Section 197 of CAMA 2020 recognizes convertible debentures, which give the holder the option to convert the debt into shares of the company at a future date [35, 36].

quizzes.scenario: The directors of a company decide to make a call on shares. According to standard principles, what is the maximum fraction of the unpaid amount they can call up at once?
options: The total amount
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Standard principles in Nigerian corporate law (often reflected in model articles) suggest that a company cannot call up more than 1/4 of the nominal value of the unpaid shares at one time [14].

quizzes.scenario: A company wishes to issue "Bearer Shares" where ownership is determined by physical possession of the certificate. Is this allowed under CAMA 2020?
options: Yes, for public companies
Yes, for private companies
No, bearer shares are prohibited
Only with the Minister's consent
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: CAMA 2020 prohibits the issuance of bearer shares; ownership of shares must be registered in the company's register of members [9, 36].

quizzes.scenario: For a public company offering debentures to the general public, what document is mandatory to protect the interests of the various debenture holders?
options: A Partnership Deed
A Debenture Trust Deed
A Bill of Lading
A Power of Attorney
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Section 208 of CAMA 2020 requires that where a public company offers debentures to the public, there must be a debenture trust deed and a qualified trustee appointed to represent the debenture holders [37, 38].

quizzes.scenario: A receiver is appointed over the assets of Mega-Growth Ltd. What is the immediate legal effect on the powers of the company's board of directors?
options: Their powers are increased
Their powers to deal with the charged assets are suspended
They are all fired
They become agents of the CAC
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Once a receiver/manager is appointed, the powers of the board of directors to manage or deal with the assets covered by the receivership are suspended or in abeyance [39, 40].

quizzes.scenario: When applying for the registration of a charge over landed property with the CAC, which of the following must be attached?
options: A copy of the company's birth certificate
The instrument creating the charge (e.g., Deed of Legal Mortgage)
The CEO's resume
A list of all employees
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Section 222(1) of CAMA 2020 requires the delivery of the instrument by which the charge is created or evidenced for registration at the CAC [29, 30].

quizzes.scenario: A shareholder has paid only N0.50 on a share with a nominal value of N1.00. The company goes into liquidation. What is the shareholder's maximum liability?
options: Zero
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: In a company limited by shares, the liability of a member is limited to the amount, if any, remaining unpaid on the shares held by them (Section 21(1)(a) CAMA 2020) [41-43].

quizzes.scenario: A company intends to buy back its shares and must publish a notice in two national newspapers. Within how many days of the resolution must this be done?
options: 7 days
14 days
21 days
28 days
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Section 184(1) of CAMA 2020 requires the company to publish a notice of the proposed share acquisition in two national newspapers within 7 days of passing the resolution [22, 44].

quizzes.scenario: A group of 100 investors contributes funds to a portfolio managed by a professional fund manager. This arrangement is best described as:
options: A Sole Proprietorship
A Business Name
A Collective Investment Scheme
A Private Company
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Section 357 of the ISA 2025 defines a Collective Investment Scheme (CIS) as an investment arrangement where investors pool their money into a managed portfolio overseen by a fund manager [45, 46].

quizzes.scenario: A public company, Foodies PLC, provides a loan to Mr. Adam to enable him to purchase 5% of Foodies PLC's shares. Does this act attract a penalty?
options: No, if the loan is small
No, if Adam is a director
Yes, it is a breach of the prohibition on financial assistance
Only if Adam defaults
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Providing a loan for the acquisition of its own shares is a violation of Section 183 of CAMA 2020 for a public company, and the company and its officers are liable to penalties [19, 21].

quizzes.scenario: To raise funds without giving up ownership, a company issues a long-term debt instrument that creates a charge over its assets. What is this instrument?
options: An Ordinary Share
A Preference Share
A Debenture
A Bonus Issue
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: A debenture is a debt instrument issued by a company acknowledging its indebtedness to a creditor, usually involving a charge over the company's undertaking or property [47-49].

quizzes.scenario: A creditor holds a floating charge over "all the company's assets". The company enters into a member's voluntary winding up. What is the status of the floating charge?
options: It is void
It crystallizes immediately
It remains floating until the liquidator sells the assets
It is converted into a share
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: A floating charge crystallizes and becomes fixed upon the commencement of winding up or the appointment of a liquidator [25-27].

quizzes.scenario: A company wants to increase its share capital. Under the Business Facilitation Act 2022 amendment to CAMA 2020, who has the power to do this?
options: Only the general meeting
Only the CAC
The members at general meeting and now also the board of directors
Only the company secretary
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Section 127(1) of CAMA 2020, as amended by the Business Facilitation Act, now allows the board of directors (in addition to the general meeting) to increase a company's share capital [50-53].

quizzes.scenario: What is the minimum issued share capital for a public company limited by shares in Nigeria?
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Section 27(2)(a) of CAMA 2020 sets the minimum issued share capital for a public company at N2,000,000 [42, 54, 55].

quizzes.scenario: A company's articles are silent on the power of directors to make calls on shares. Can they still make calls?
options: No, the articles must specifically grant the power
Yes, the model articles in CAMA 2020 apply
Only with court permission
Only with SEC approval
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Where the articles of a limited company do not exclude or modify the model articles, the model articles in CAMA 2020 form part of the company's articles and typically grant the power to make calls [15, 56].

quizzes.scenario: A shareholder wants to transfer his shares in a private company. What is the standard restriction he will likely face?
options: He must wait 5 years
He cannot transfer to anyone outside the family
He must first offer the shares to existing members (Pre-emption)
He must pay the CAC 50% of the value
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Section 22(2) of CAMA 2020 requires private companies to restrict the transfer of their shares, often through pre-emption rights where existing members have the first right of refusal [57, 58].

quizzes.scenario: A company's articles provide for a specific class of shares with "cumulative" rights. What does this mean for the shareholder?
options: They get double votes
If a dividend is not paid one year, the right to it carries forward to future years
They can manage the company
They get free lunch
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Cumulative preference shares entitle the holder to arrears of dividends if the company fails to declare a dividend in any given year [59-61].

quizzes.scenario: A company is registered as "Mobil Producing Unlimited". What is the hallmark of the members' liability?
options: Limited to N100,000
Limited to unpaid shares
There is no limit to their liability for company debts
They have no liability at all
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: In an unlimited company, there is no limit to the liability of its members for the debts and liabilities of the company (Section 21(1)(c) CAMA 2020) [41, 62].

quizzes.scenario: A company issue of shares was made without a prospectus. What is the fine for an individual responsible for this under the ISA 2025?
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Section 99(4) of the ISA 2025 imposes a fine of N5,000,000 on any individual or body corporate that issues an application form for securities without a registered prospectus [17].

quizzes.scenario: A shareholder claims she was not given notice of a meeting to allot shares. What is the correct mode of application to court for the rectification of the register of members?
options: Originating Summons
Petition
Originating Motion
Writ of Summons
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Section 115 of CAMA 2020 and Rule 3 of the Companies Proceedings Rules 1992 provide that an application for the rectification of the register of members should be made by Originating Motion [12, 13, 63].

quizzes.scenario: A public company fails to register the interest of an alien who purchased its shares with the SEC. What register is the company specifically failing to maintain?
options: Register of Charges
Register of Interest in Shares
Register of Directors
Register of Debentures
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Section 120 of CAMA 2020 and relevant SEC rules require public companies to maintain a register of substantial interests (especially involving foreign participation) in shares [64, 65].

quizzes.scenario: Mr. Ade wishes to take a loan from a bank to buy shares in a private company. Can the private company guarantee this loan?
options: Yes, provided it is from distributable profits and approved by special resolution
No, never
Yes, only if the company is a bank
Only if the bank owns the company
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Section 183(4) of CAMA 2020 provides an exception for private companies to provide financial assistance for share acquisition if certain conditions like distributable profits and special resolutions are met [18, 19].

quizzes.scenario: A company creates a floating charge over its "raw materials". Does the company need the creditor's permission to use those raw materials in daily manufacturing?
options: Yes, always
No, it can deal with them in the ordinary course of business
Only if the value exceeds N1 million
Yes, if the CEO is away
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: A hallmark of a floating charge is that the company can continue to use and dispose of the assets in the ordinary course of business without the lender's consent until the charge crystallizes [25, 27, 66].

quizzes.scenario: A debenture holder whose security is a fixed charge finds that the company has defaulted. Can they sell the property without a court order?
options: No, a court order is always required
Yes, if the power of sale is conferred by the debenture or trust deed
Only if the CAC approves
Only if the directors agree
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: If the power of sale is expressly granted in the debenture or the trust deed, the secured creditor may exercise the right to sell the property upon default without court intervention [67, 68].

quizzes.scenario: A company is being wound up by the court. A creditor attempts to attach the company's delivery vans to satisfy a debt. Is this attachment valid?
options: Yes, first come, first served
No, it is void
Only if the debt is above N10 million
Yes, if the police assist
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Section 576 of CAMA 2020 provides that any attachment, distress, or execution put in force against the estate or effects of a company after the commencement of winding up by the court shall be void [69].

quizzes.scenario: To ensure transparency, a public company must have its audit committee. How many members of this committee must be non-executive directors?
options: One
Two
Three
All of them
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Section 404(3) of CAMA 2020 provides that the audit committee of a public company shall consist of five members, including three shareholders and two non-executive directors [70].

quizzes.scenario: A company director uses "insider information" to sell his shares before a bad financial report is released. What body regulates and punishes this conduct in the capital market?
options: CAC
Nigerian Bar Association
Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC)
The Ministry of Finance
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: The Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC) is the apex body responsible for regulating all investment and securities business and preventing market abuses like insider trading [71-73].

quizzes.scenario: A company issues debenture stock. What is the key difference between this and a single debenture?
options: It has no interest
It is issued under a trust deed to a large number of people
It is only for directors
It is a gift
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Debenture stock is a securitized loan used when borrowing from a large number of people, typically issued under a trust deed with a trustee appointed for the holders [25, 38].

quizzes.scenario: A company intends to reduce its share capital. Beside a special resolution, what is the most critical requirement to protect creditors?
options: A letter from the Governor
Confirmation of the reduction by the court
A newspaper advertisement in 5 dailies
A new CEO
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Section 131 and 132 of CAMA 2020 provide that a company may only reduce its share capital by a special resolution that is subsequently confirmed by an order of the court [74-76].

quizzes.scenario: The directors of Zenith Ltd are accused of misappropriating company funds for personal vacations. At a general meeting, a majority of the shareholders vote to ratify the directors' actions and forgo legal proceedings. Mr. Okoro, a minority shareholder, wishes to sue the directors on behalf of the company.
options: Mr. Okoro can sue as of right because he is a member.
Mr. Okoro cannot sue because the majority has ratified the conduct under the rule in Foss v Harbottle.
Mr. Okoro must seek the consent of the Corporate Affairs Commission.
Mr. Okoro can only sue if he holds 15% of the shares.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Under the Proper Plaintiff Rule in Section 341 of CAMA 2020 (the rule in Foss v Harbottle), where a wrong is done to a company, the company is the proper plaintiff and the court will not interfere if the act is ratifiable by the majority [1, 2].

quizzes.scenario: The board of directors of Peak Plc decides to invest company funds in a business object that is expressly prohibited by the company's Memorandum of Association. Sarah, a minority shareholder, objects and wishes to restrain the company.
options: Sarah cannot sue as she is a minority shareholder.
Sarah can bring a direct action for an injunction to restrain the ultra vires act.
Sarah must first get the majority to vote against the act at a general meeting.
Sarah must apply for the winding up of the company.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Section 343(a) of CAMA 2020 provides an exception to the majority rule, allowing a member to bring a direct action for an injunction or declaration to restrain the company from entering into an illegal or ultra vires transaction [3, 4].

quizzes.scenario: Chief Bolaji, a member of Delta Ltd, is consistently denied his right to receive notices of general meetings and is blocked from voting. He wishes to sue the directors for this specific breach.
options: Chief Bolaji must bring a derivative action on behalf of the company.
Chief Bolaji must bring a personal action to enforce his individual rights.
Chief Bolaji cannot sue as the decision to exclude him is an internal management issue.
Chief Bolaji must wait for the next AGM to raise the issue.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Section 344(1) of CAMA 2020 allows a member to bring a personal action to enforce a right due to him personally, such as the right to notice and the right to vote [5, 6].

quizzes.scenario: Directors of Alpha Ltd, who are also majority shareholders, sell company property to their own private firm at 20% of its market value. A minority shareholder, Jude, wants to challenge this.
options: Jude cannot sue because the directors are the majority and can ratify the sale.
Jude can sue because this constitutes "fraud on the minority" where the wrongdoers are in control.
Jude must apply to the CAC for investigation first.
Jude can only sue if he holds at least 25% of the share capital.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Section 343(d) of CAMA 2020 provides that a minority shareholder can sue where a fraud is being committed on the company and the wrongdoers are the controllers of the company [7].

quizzes.scenario: A group of minority shareholders in Beta Ltd believe the managing director has breached his fiduciary duties. They wish to sue him in the name of the company because the board refuses to take action.
options: They must apply to the Federal High Court for leave to bring a derivative action.
They can sue directly in their own names for damages.
They must pass a special resolution at a general meeting first.
They must report the matter to the Nigerian Police Force.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Section 346(1) of CAMA 2020 provides that a member may apply to the court for leave to bring an action in the name and on behalf of the company (derivative action) when the company itself fails to do so [8, 9].

quizzes.scenario: Tunde wishes to bring a derivative action against the directors of Global Plc. Before filing, he must satisfy certain conditions regarding notice.
options: He must give the directors at least 14 days' notice of his intention.
He must give the directors reasonable notice of his intention to apply to the court for leave.
He must publish the notice in two national newspapers.
No notice is required for derivative actions.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Section 346(2) of CAMA 2020 requires that the applicant for a derivative action must give reasonable notice to the directors of his intention to apply to the court if they do not bring the action [10, 11].

quizzes.scenario: In a derivative action brought by a shareholder, the court finds the directors liable for negligence. Where should the awarded damages be paid?
options: Directly to the shareholder who brought the action.
Into the company's accounts.
To the Corporate Affairs Commission.
Distributed among the minority shareholders only.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: In a derivative action, the shareholder is enforcing a right that belongs to the company; therefore, the relief granted, such as damages, accrues to the company [9, 12].

quizzes.scenario: A shareholder of Omega Ltd brings a derivative action in good faith. The court finds the action was in the best interest of the company but dismisses the claim on a technicality. Who pays the legal fees?
options: The shareholder must pay all costs.
The court may order the company to pay the reasonable legal fees incurred by the applicant.
The directors must pay the fees personally.
The Federal Government pays the fees from the Consolidated Revenue Fund.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Section 347(2)(d) of CAMA 2020 empowers the court to order the company to pay reasonable legal fees incurred by the applicant in a derivative proceeding [13, 14].

quizzes.scenario: Musa, a minority shareholder, alleges that the majority shareholders are running the company in a way that ignores his interests and excludes him from all dividends while they pay themselves massive "consultancy fees." What is his best course of action?
options: Apply for an investigation of the company.
Bring a derivative action for breach of trust.
Petition the court for relief on grounds of unfairly prejudicial and oppressive conduct.
File a criminal complaint for theft.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Section 354(1) of CAMA 2020 allows a member to petition the court for relief on the grounds that the affairs of the company are being conducted in a manner that is oppressive or unfairly prejudicial to one or more members [12, 15].

quizzes.scenario: A minority shareholder in a small private company, Star Ltd, is unhappy with a one-time decision of the board to change the company's stationery supplier. He petitions for relief under Section 354.
options: The petition will likely succeed as it is a board decision.
The petition will likely fail because the conduct must usually be ongoing and not an isolated act.
The petition will succeed if he proves the new supplier is more expensive.
The petition must be brought by the company secretary.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: For a petition under Section 354 of CAMA 2020 to succeed, the oppressive or prejudicial conduct must typically be a course of conduct or an ongoing state of affairs, rather than a single isolated act [15].

quizzes.scenario: Upon hearing a petition for relief against oppressive conduct in Swift Ltd, the court finds the directors have indeed been acting unfairly. Which of these is NOT a power of the court?
options: Order the winding up of the company.
Order the purchase of the petitioner's shares by other members.
Order the immediate imprisonment of the directors without trial.
Order an investigation to be made by the Commission.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Section 355 of CAMA 2020 lists the powers of the court in cases of oppressive conduct, which include winding up, share buy-outs, and investigations, but does not include summary imprisonment [16-18].

quizzes.scenario: A group of members in Tech Innovations Ltd, holding 12% of the issued shares, believe the company is being managed fraudulently. They want the CAC to intervene.
options: They can apply to the Commission to appoint inspectors to investigate the company's affairs.
They must have at least 25% shares to apply for an investigation.
Only the directors can apply for an investigation.
The CAC only investigates public companies.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Section 357(2)(a) of CAMA 2020 provides that members holding at least one-tenth (10%) of the issued shares can apply to the Commission for an investigation into the company's affairs [19, 20].

quizzes.scenario: The Corporate Affairs Commission (CAC) receives a tip that a company is being used as a front for money laundering. Can the CAC investigate without a shareholder application?
options: No, the CAC must wait for a formal petition from members.
Yes, the Commission may appoint inspectors to investigate suo motu.
No, only the court can order an investigation.
Yes, but only if the company is a public company.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Section 358(2) of CAMA 2020 allows the Commission to investigate a company suo motu (on its own motion) if there are circumstances suggesting fraud, illegality, or oppressive conduct [19, 21, 22].

quizzes.scenario: During an investigation into Royal Plc, an inspector requires an officer of the company to produce specific financial records. The officer refuses.
options: The inspector has no power to compel production.
Refusal to produce documents or answer questions from an inspector is an offense and can be treated as contempt of court.
The officer can only be compelled by the Nigerian Police.
The inspector must pay the officer for the documents.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Inspectors appointed under Section 357 of CAMA 2020 have the power to require officers and agents of the company to produce documents and provide evidence; failure to comply is punishable [23, 24].

quizzes.scenario: The board of directors of Blue Ltd decides to sell 60% of the company's total assets to a third party. This is not in the ordinary course of business.
options: The board can do this by a simple board resolution.
This is a major asset transaction and requires approval by a special resolution of the shareholders.
The board must get approval from the CAC registrar-general.
Only the company secretary can authorize such a sale.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Section 342 of CAMA 2020 stipulates that a major asset transaction (sale of assets exceeding 50% of the company's value) must be approved by a special resolution of the members [19, 25, 26].

quizzes.scenario: A public company, Green Plc, passes a special resolution to re-register as a private company (LTD). A group of members who didn't vote for it want to stop the change.
options: They cannot stop it once the resolution is passed.
They must hold at least 5% of the shares and apply to the court within 28 days to cancel the resolution.
They must hold 15% of the shares to object.
They must report the matter to the Securities and Exchange Commission.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Section 64 of CAMA 2020 allows members holding at least 5% of the company's issued share capital or 50 members to apply to the court for the cancellation of a resolution to re-register from a public to a private company [26-29].

quizzes.scenario: A minority shareholder, Ngozi, believes that a recent ordinary resolution passed by the majority should have been passed as a special resolution according to the company's Articles.
options: Ngozi cannot sue because the majority has spoken.
Ngozi can sue as this is an exception to the rule in Foss v Harbottle involving an irregular procedure.
Ngozi must first sell her shares before suing.
Ngozi can only complain to the company secretary.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: One of the established exceptions to the rule in Foss v Harbottle is where the company performs an act by an ordinary resolution that requires a special resolution (irregular procedure), allowing a minority action to be maintained [7, 30].

quizzes.scenario: Directors of FastTrack Ltd are negligent in a deal, but they also personally profit from the mistake. The majority shareholders refuse to sue them.
options: Minority shareholders can sue because the directors derived a benefit from their breach of duty.
Minority shareholders cannot sue for negligence; only for fraud.
Minority shareholders must wait for the company to be wound up.
The directors are protected by the business judgment rule.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Under Section 343(f) of CAMA 2020 and the case of Daniels v Daniels, a minority action can be brought where directors profit or benefit from their own negligence or breach of duty [31, 32].

quizzes.scenario: A shareholder wants to bring an action because the directors refuse to allow him to inspect the minutes of the last Annual General Meeting.
options: This is a wrong to the company and requires a derivative action.
This is an infringement of a personal right, allowing for a personal action.
The shareholder must get 10% of members to join him.
The minutes of the AGM are confidential and not open to members.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: The right to inspect minutes of a general meeting is a personal right of a member; therefore, its denial justifies a personal action under Section 344 of CAMA 2020 [6, 31].

quizzes.scenario: A minority shareholder brings a representative action on behalf of himself and 10 other members who were all denied dividends. The court finds in their favor.
options: The court can only issue an injunction.
The court can award damages to the members for the loss incurred.
All damages must be paid to the company.
The directors must be removed immediately.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Section 344(2)(a) of CAMA 2020 explicitly allows the court to award damages for any loss incurred on account of a breach of a member's personal or representative right [8, 33].

quizzes.scenario: To bring a derivative action in Nigeria, a member must first obtain what from the court?
options: A certificate of merit.
Leave of court.
A warrant of arrest for the directors.
A letter of no objection from the CAC.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Section 346(1) of CAMA 2020 requires that a member must apply for and be granted leave by the court before they can institute a derivative action in the name of the company [10, 34].

quizzes.scenario: The court is considering whether to grant leave for a derivative action. Which of these is a factor the court MUST consider?
options: The applicant's bank balance.
Whether the action is brought in good faith and is in the best interest of the company.
Whether the applicant is a Nigerian citizen.
Whether the company is profitable.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Section 346(2) of CAMA 2020 sets out the criteria for granting leave for a derivative action, which includes the applicant's good faith and the interest of the company [10, 11].

quizzes.scenario: A minority shareholder, Obi, institutes a personal action against Zenith Ltd. The court awards him costs. When is this possible?
options: Only if his action succeeds.
Whether or not his action succeeds, at the court's discretion.
Only if he owns at least 10% of the company.
Only if the directors acted with malice.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Section 344(4) of CAMA 2020 provides that the court may award costs to a member who institutes a personal or representative action, whether or not the action succeeds [35].

quizzes.scenario: In a derivative action, the shareholder discovers that the majority of shareholders have actually approved the directors' breach of duty. What is the legal effect?
options: The action is automatically dismissed.
The approval does not invalidate the derivative action but may influence the court's decision.
The shareholder is liable for a fine.
The company is immediately wound up.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Under Section 346 of CAMA 2020, the fact that shareholders have approved a breach of duty does not preclude a derivative action, though the court may take it into consideration [13, 36].

quizzes.scenario: A minority shareholder wants to challenge an alteration to the company's object clause. How much of the issued share capital must he and his fellow objectors hold?
options: At least 5%.
At least 10%.
At least 15%.
At least 25%.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Section 51(2)(a) of CAMA 2020 provides that holders of not less than 15% in nominal value of the company's issued share capital may apply to the court to challenge an alteration of the object clause [37-39].

quizzes.scenario: A group of debenture holders, secured by a floating charge, believe the company's object clause was altered illegally. Can they sue?
options: No, only shareholders can sue.
Yes, if they hold at least 15% of the company's debentures.
Yes, but only if the company is in liquidation.
No, debenture holders have no say in company objects.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Section 51(2)(c) of CAMA 2020 allows holders of at least 15% of the company's debentures secured by a floating charge to apply to the court to cancel an alteration of the company's objects [37-39].

quizzes.scenario: The court finds that a company's affairs are being conducted in an unfairly prejudicial manner. It orders that the company purchase the petitioner's shares. What must then happen to the company's capital?
options: It remains the same.
It must be reduced accordingly.
It must be increased to cover the cost.
The company must be re-registered as a public company.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Section 355(2)(d) of CAMA 2020 provides that where the court orders the company to purchase a member's shares, it also orders the reduction of the company's capital accordingly [16, 18].

quizzes.scenario: Under Section 343(g) of CAMA 2020, a minority member can sue for an injunction when the "interest of justice" demands. This is known as:
options: The specific ground.
The personal ground.
The omnibus ground.
The corporate ground.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Section 343(g) of CAMA 2020 is often referred to as the "omnibus ground" for minority actions, covering acts or omissions where the interest of justice requires court intervention [6, 7].

quizzes.scenario: Who is NOT eligible to apply for an investigation of a company's affairs to the CAC?
options: Members holding 10% of shares.
The company itself.
A judgment creditor who is owed ?5,000,000.
The Court suo motu.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Section 357 of CAMA 2020 lists those who can apply for investigation, including 10% of members, the company, and the court, but does not include individual creditors in the general list [19, 20].

quizzes.scenario: An inspector is investigating a company and wants to examine the records of its subsidiary. Is this allowed?
options: No, he is only authorized to investigate the parent company.
Yes, an inspector may also investigate the affairs of any other body corporate that is or has been a subsidiary or holding company of the company.
Only if the subsidiary is also located in the same city.
Only if the subsidiary's directors agree.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Under CAMA 2020, an inspector's powers extend to investigating the affairs of subsidiaries or holding companies related to the company under investigation [22, 40].

quizzes.scenario: A company's articles are altered by a special resolution to remove a provision that protects minority voting rights. The minority believe this is "unfairly prejudicial." How should they proceed?
options: Bring a derivative action.
File a petition for relief under Section 354.
Report the directors to the SEC.
There is no remedy as the majority can alter the articles.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: A petition under Section 354 of CAMA 2020 is the appropriate mode for seeking relief against conduct (including resolutions) that is unfairly prejudicial or discriminatory to a member [12, 15, 41].

quizzes.scenario: In an investigation, the inspectors find that the directors have committed misfeasance. The CAC decides to take legal action. In whose name is the action brought?
options: In the name of the CAC.
In the name and on behalf of the company.
In the name of the Inspector-General of Police.
In the name of the Attorney-General.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Section 364 of CAMA 2020 empowers the Commission to bring civil proceedings in the name and on behalf of the company if the investigation report suggests it is in the public interest [42].

quizzes.scenario: A minority shareholder wishes to bring an action because a director is using company information to benefit his own private business. What type of action is this?
options: Personal action.
Derivative action.
Representative action.
Class action.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Since the wrong (misuse of company info) is committed against the company, the appropriate remedy is a derivative action brought on behalf of the company under Section 346 of CAMA 2020 [8, 43].

quizzes.scenario: A shareholder brings a derivative action but then decides he wants to settle the case out of court with the directors.
options: He can settle at any time.
He can only settle with the approval of the court.
He must get the majority of shareholders to agree to the settlement.
He cannot settle; the case must go to trial.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: A derivative action cannot be discontinued, stayed, settled, or dismissed without the approval of the court under Section 347 of CAMA 2020 [13].

quizzes.scenario: Which court has exclusive jurisdiction to hear a petition for relief on the ground of oppressive conduct?
options: State High Court.
National Industrial Court.
Federal High Court.
Court of Appeal.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Under Section 251(1)(e) of the 1999 Constitution and Section 354 of CAMA 2020, the Federal High Court has exclusive jurisdiction over corporate proceedings and petitions against oppressive conduct [24, 44, 45].

quizzes.scenario: A member of a company limited by guarantee wishes to institute a personal action. How is "member" defined for this purpose?
options: Only the original subscribers.
Includes the personal representatives of a deceased member.
Only those who have paid their dues.
Only those who have been members for at least 5 years.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Section 345 of CAMA 2020 defines "member" for the purpose of direct actions (Sections 343 & 344) to include personal representatives of deceased members and transferees of shares [8, 35].

quizzes.scenario: A shareholder wants to sue because the company's name was changed without following the proper procedure.
options: This is a personal action.
This is a derivative action.
This is a direct action for an injunction under Section 343.
This is not actionable by a minority shareholder.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Challenging an irregular procedure (like a name change without proper resolution) falls under the exceptions to Foss v Harbottle codified in Section 343, allowing a direct action for an injunction or declaration [4, 7].

quizzes.scenario: A petition for winding up on "just and equitable" grounds is brought by a member who has been unfairly excluded from management in a "quasi-partnership" company.
options: The petition will fail because the company is a separate legal entity.
The petition may succeed if the court finds it is "just and equitable" under Section 571(f).
Winding up is only for insolvency.
Only the CAC can petition for winding up.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Section 571(f) of CAMA 2020 allows the court to order winding up on "just and equitable" grounds, which includes cases of deadlock or exclusion in small, private companies resembling partnerships [46-48].

quizzes.scenario: A minority shareholder brings a representative action. He loses the case. Can the court still award him costs?
options: No, costs follow the event.
Yes, the court may award costs to him personally if it sees fit.
Only if he can prove he is indigent.
Only if the company agrees to pay.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Section 344(4) of CAMA 2020 grants the court the discretion to award costs to a member in a personal or representative action regardless of whether the action succeeds [35].

quizzes.scenario: The CAC initiates an investigation into XYZ Ltd. Who pays for the expenses of the investigation?
options: The CAC always pays.
The company or the person who applied for the investigation, as directed by the Commission.
The Federal Government from the police budget.
The directors personally.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Expenses for an investigation are typically defrayed by the company or the applicants, depending on the Commission's direction following the report [42].

quizzes.scenario: A shareholder discovers the directors are about to pay themselves dividends that were never declared by the general meeting.
options: He must wait until they are paid and then sue for theft.
He can bring a direct action for an injunction to restrain the illegal payment.
He must ask the majority to stop them.
He can only complain to the company's auditors.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Restraining illegal acts, such as unauthorized dividend payments, is a key exception to the majority rule under Section 343(a) of CAMA 2020 [3, 4].

quizzes.scenario: A company passes a resolution to alter its objects. Dissenting members hold 16% of the shares. They file a petition in court on the 35th day after the resolution.
options: Their application is valid.
Their application is out of time as it must be made within 28 days.
They must have 25% of shares to apply.
They should have applied to the CAC, not the court.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Section 51(3) of CAMA 2020 requires that an application to the court to cancel an alteration of objects must be made within 28 days after the passing of the resolution [39, 49, 50].

quizzes.scenario: In a petition against oppressive conduct, the court finds a contract between the company and a third party was unfair. What can the court do to the contract?
options: Nothing, the third party is protected by privity.
The court can vary or set aside the transaction and order compensation.
The court can only order the directors to pay damages.
The court must wind up the company immediately.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Section 355(2)(f) of CAMA 2020 empowers the court to vary or set aside transactions or contracts to which the company is a party and award compensation [17, 18].

quizzes.scenario: A shareholder in a private company wants to bring a derivative action. Which court process is typically used to commence this?
options: Writ of Summons.
Originating Summons.
Petition.
Notice of Motion.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Derivative actions are typically commenced by way of an Originating Summons after leave of court has been obtained [13].

quizzes.scenario: A shareholder wants to sue for a breach of a right common to all shareholders except the directors. What type of action is this?
options: Personal action.
Derivative action.
Representative action.
Suo motu action.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: A representative action is brought by a member on behalf of himself and other members (excluding the defendants) to enforce a right common to them all [5, 51].

quizzes.scenario: A minority shareholder brings an action for an injunction under Section 343. He also wants damages for the company.
options: He can get both in one action.
He can only get an injunction or declaration under Section 343; damages require a derivative or personal action.
He cannot get an injunction if he wants damages.
