quizzes.scenario: A liquidator is appointed for a company in voluntary winding up. What is the timeframe for him to notify the Corporate Affairs Commission of his appointment?
options: 7 days.
14 days.
21 days.
30 days.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Under Section 630 and 641 of CAMA 2020, the liquidator shall, within 14 days after his appointment, publish in the Gazette and in two daily newspapers, and deliver to the Commission for registration, a notice of his appointment. [36-38]

quizzes.scenario: The directors of ZZZ Ltd make a declaration of solvency six weeks before the company passes the resolution for members' voluntary winding up.
options: The declaration is valid and effective.
The declaration is invalid as it was made more than five weeks before the resolution.
The declaration is valid if filed with the CAC within 15 days.
The timeframe is irrelevant if the company is actually solvent.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Section 625(2)(a) of CAMA 2020 stipulates that a declaration of solvency has no effect unless it is made within five weeks immediately preceding the date of the passing of the resolution for winding up. [7, 39, 40]

quizzes.scenario: A company in voluntary winding up wishes to appoint its current Chairman, who is also a substantial shareholder, as the liquidator.
options: This is allowed to ensure continuity.
He is disqualified as a director of the company under liquidation.
He is eligible if the creditors pass an ordinary resolution.
Only the Official Receiver can be appointed.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Section 509(1)(e) of CAMA 2020 (as noted in priority/disqualification lists) disqualifies any director of a company under liquidation from being appointed as its liquidator. [15, 41]

quizzes.scenario: In a creditors' voluntary winding up, the creditors decide to appoint a committee to oversee the liquidator. What is the maximum number of members for this committee?
options: 3 members.
5 members.
7 members.
10 members.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Under Section 637 of CAMA 2020, the creditors at their meeting may appoint a committee of inspection consisting of not more than five persons to work with the liquidator. [26, 42, 43]

quizzes.scenario: A winding-up order is made against BBB Ltd. A creditor, who had already filed a suit for debt recovery before the petition, wishes to continue the proceedings.
options: He can proceed without any further steps.
He can only proceed with the leave of the court.
All proceedings are permanently terminated.
He must wait for the company to be struck off.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Section 417 of CAMA 2020 (and Section 580 related) provides that when a winding-up order has been made, no action or proceeding shall be proceeded with or commenced against the company except by leave of the court. [13, 44, 45]

quizzes.scenario: A petition for compulsory winding up is presented. Who is the officer designated by law to act as the default Official Receiver?
options: The Registrar-General of the CAC.
The Managing Director of the company.
The Deputy Chief Registrar of the Federal High Court.
Any accredited Insolvency Practitioner.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: According to the source definitions, the Official Receiver is the Deputy Chief Registrar of the Federal High Court or any other officer designated by the Chief Judge. [46-48]

quizzes.scenario: A company is found to have been formed for the purpose of defrauding its investors and has no legitimate business assets. The court is asked to wind it up.
options: Ground: Inability to pay debts.
Ground: Just and equitable.
Ground: Default in statutory report.
Ground: Reduction of members.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Under Section 571(f) of CAMA 2020, the court may wind up a company on just and equitable grounds if it was formed for a fraudulent or illegal purpose, or is a "bubble." [20, 21, 49]

quizzes.scenario: A creditor obtains a judgment against KKK Ltd for N1,000,000. The court bailiff attempts to levy execution but returns the writ as "unsatisfied."
options: The creditor must serve a 3-week demand notice.
The company is deemed unable to pay its debts immediately.
The creditor must petition the CAC first.
Winding up is only possible if the debt is N2,000,000.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Section 572(b) of CAMA 2020 states that a company is deemed unable to pay its debts if execution or other process issued on a judgment in favor of a creditor is returned unsatisfied in whole or in part. [1, 50]

quizzes.scenario: Following the final meeting in a members' voluntary winding up, how soon must the liquidator file the final account and returns with the CAC?
options: 7 days.
14 days.
15 days.
28 days.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Section 631(3) of CAMA 2020 requires the liquidator to send a copy of the account and returns of the final meeting to the Commission within seven days after the date of the meeting. [51-53]

quizzes.scenario: A liquidator has filed the final returns for ZZZ Ltd. A minority shareholder discovers that the liquidator failed to account for a significant asset and wants to delay the dissolution.
options: He must wait for the company to be dissolved then void it.
He can apply to the court for an order deferring the date of dissolution.
He can file a fresh petition for compulsory winding up.
He must report the liquidator to the police for fraud.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Under Section 631(4) of CAMA 2020, the court may, on the application of the liquidator or any other person who appears to the court to be interested, make an order deferring the date at which the dissolution is to take effect. [54-56]

quizzes.scenario: To preserve the assets of a company after a winding-up petition has been filed but before the court has heard the matter, what officer may be appointed?
options: Official Receiver.
Provisional Liquidator.
Special Manager.
Receiver Manager.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: A Provisional Liquidator is appointed by the court after a petition is filed but before determination to preserve the company's assets and prevent dissipation by the directors. [42, 47, 57]

quizzes.scenario: The Official Receiver in a compulsory winding up requires a person with specific technical expertise to manage the company's business operations temporarily.
options: He can delegate his powers to the MD.
The court can appoint a Special Manager.
The CAC can appoint an interim manager.
He must hire a consultant solicitor.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Sources indicate the court may appoint a Special Manager to assist the Official Receiver in managing the company's business temporarily when the nature of the business requires special expertise. [41, 47, 58]

quizzes.scenario: A creditor serves a demand notice on a company for a debt of N150,000. Under the BFA amendment to CAMA, does this debt allow for a winding-up petition?
options: Yes, as it exceeds the old N2,000 limit.
No, the BFA requires the sum to be determined by a Commission regulation.
Yes, if the company is a public company.
No, trade debts must exceed N500,000.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Section 19 of the Business Facilitation Act (BFA) amended Section 572 of CAMA 2020 to provide that the debt sum must be "a sum to be determined by a regulation issued by the Commission." [1, 3]

quizzes.scenario: Three months before its insolvency, a company transfers its only remaining unencumbered asset to a major creditor who is also a cousin of the Managing Director.
options: Valid settlement of a debt.
Fraudulent Preference.
Ultra Vires transaction.
Arrangement on Sale.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Under Section 658 of CAMA 2020, a transaction where a company makes a payment or gives a preference to a creditor within the hardening period with the intent to put them in a better position is an invalid fraudulent preference. [59, 60]

quizzes.scenario: The Corporate Affairs Commission (CAC) has reasonable cause to believe that a company has not conducted business for 10 years and has filed no annual returns.
options: It must file a petition for compulsory winding up.
It can strike the name of the company off the register as defunct.
It must appoint a provisional liquidator.
It must fine the directors N1,000,000.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Section 692(3) of CAMA 2020 empowers the Commission to strike the name of a company off the register if it has reasonable cause to believe it is no longer carrying on business or in operation for 10 years. [61-63]

quizzes.scenario: A company was compulsorily struck off the register as defunct 4 years ago. An aggrieved former member now wants to restore it to recover corporate property.
options: The application is barred by time.
He can apply to the court for restoration within 10 years of striking off.
He must file a fresh incorporation application.
Only the Attorney General can apply for restoration.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Under Section 692(6) of CAMA 2020, a person aggrieved by the striking off of a company may apply to the court for restoration at any time before the expiration of 10 years from the publication of the notice. [64, 65]

quizzes.scenario: In the statutory priority of payments during winding up, which of the following ranks lowest?
options: Liquidator's remuneration.
Unpaid salaries of junior staff.
Equity shareholders (ordinary shares).
Secured creditors with fixed charges.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Under Section 657 of CAMA 2020, equity holders and deferred shareholders are paid last, after all costs, taxes, wages, and claims of secured/unsecured creditors and preferential shareholders are settled. [59, 62]

quizzes.scenario: A group of minority shareholders wants to wind up a company on the ground that the majority is acting oppressively. What is the correct mode of application?
options: Originating Motion.
Writ of Summons.
Petition.
Originating Summons.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Under Rule 4 of the Companies Proceedings Rules and Section 354 of CAMA, an application for relief on the ground of oppressive conduct or for winding up must be made by petition. [66-69]

quizzes.scenario: The fixed term for the existence of GGG Ltd has expired. The members meet but fail to pass a special resolution. Can they still wind up voluntarily?
options: No, a special resolution is always required.
Yes, an ordinary resolution is sufficient if the articles provided for dissolution on expiry.
Only if the court orders it.
No, the CAC must strike it off instead.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Section 620(a) of CAMA 2020 provides that a company may be wound up voluntarily when the period fixed by the articles expires and a resolution (not necessarily special) is passed requiring voluntary winding up. [70, 71]

quizzes.scenario: A company in voluntary winding up wishes to appoint "Insolvency Experts Ltd," a licensed body corporate, as its liquidator.
options: Valid, if they are qualified practitioners.
A body corporate is disqualified from acting as a liquidator.
Only allowed in creditors' voluntary winding up.
Allowed if approved by the Federal High Court.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Section 509(1)(c) of CAMA 2020 (and source notes) expressly disqualifies a body corporate from being appointed or acting as a liquidator in a winding up. [15, 41]

quizzes.scenario: A liquidator is appointed for a company. Three months later, he is convicted of a felony involving dishonesty in an unrelated matter.
options: He can continue until the winding up is finished.
He is now incompetent to act as liquidator.
He is only removed if members pass a special resolution.
He must appoint a proxy to handle the funds.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Section 509(1)(f) of CAMA 2020 disqualifies any person convicted of an offence involving fraud, dishonesty, or moral turpitude from acting as a liquidator. [10, 15]

quizzes.scenario: HHH Ltd is in the process of voluntary winding up. The directors want to enter a new long-term lease for office space to expand operations.
options: This is a valid exercise of corporate powers.
The company must cease business except for beneficial winding-up purposes.
Valid if sanctioned by the CAC.
Voidable by the minority shareholders.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Section 623 of CAMA 2020 states that the company shall, from the commencement of the winding up, cease to carry on its business, except so far as is required for the beneficial winding up thereof. [14, 31, 37]

quizzes.scenario: During a voluntary winding up, a shareholder attempts to sell his shares to an outsider without the liquidator's knowledge.
options: The transfer is valid and effective.
The transfer is void.
The transfer is valid if for market value.
The transfer is voidable by the directors.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Section 623 (and related S. 576/601 notes) provides that any transfer of shares, not being a transfer made to or with the sanction of the liquidator, made after the commencement of voluntary winding up, is void. [14, 16, 30]

quizzes.scenario: The directors of III Ltd make a declaration of solvency stating that the company can pay its debts within 24 months.
options: This is a valid declaration for members' voluntary winding up.
The declaration is invalid as the period cannot exceed 12 months.
Valid if approved by 75% of creditors.
The period is at the discretion of the directors.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Section 625(1) of CAMA 2020 requires the declaration to state that the company will be able to pay its debts in full within such period, not exceeding 12 months, from the commencement of the winding up. [40, 72]

quizzes.scenario: In a members' voluntary winding up, the liquidator discovers that the company's liabilities are far greater than its assets.
options: He must declare the directors fraudulent.
He must summon a meeting of the creditors.
He must apply for a court-supervised order.
He must stop the winding up immediately.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Under Section 634 of CAMA 2020, if the liquidator is at any time of the opinion that the company will not be able to pay its debts in full, he shall forthwith summon a meeting of the creditors. [9, 73]

quizzes.scenario: The liquidator of JJJ Ltd is ready to hold the final meeting to conclude the winding up. Where must he publish the notice of this meeting?
options: Only on the company's website.
In the Official Gazette and at least two daily newspapers.
By direct email to all members.
In the CAC regional office.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Section 631(2) and 641(2) of CAMA 2020 require notice of the final meeting to be published in the Gazette and in at least two daily newspapers circulating in the locality of the meeting. [51, 53, 74, 75]

quizzes.scenario: The liquidator files the final accounts and returns for KKK Ltd with the CAC on January 1st. On what date is the company deemed dissolved?
options: January 1st.
February 1st.
April 1st (after 3 months).
July 1st (after 6 months).
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Under Section 631(4) and 641(6) of CAMA 2020, the Commission shall register the account and returns, and on the expiration of three months from the registration, the company shall be deemed to be dissolved. [52, 54, 76, 77]

quizzes.scenario: A company has already commenced voluntary winding up, but creditors believe the liquidator is not acting properly. They petition for court intervention.
options: Ground: Compulsory winding up.
Mode: Winding up subject to the supervision of the court.
Mode: Administration.
Ground: Fraudulent preference.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Under Section 649 of CAMA 2020, where a company passes a resolution for voluntary winding up, the court may make an order that the winding up shall continue but subject to such supervision of the court. [4, 77, 78]

quizzes.scenario: What is the legal status of a petition for winding up subject to the supervision of the court?
options: It is an alternative to administration.
It is deemed to be a petition for compulsory winding up by the court.
it is only applicable to public companies.
It prevents the appointment of a liquidator.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Section 650 of CAMA 2020 provides that a petition for the continuance of a voluntary winding up subject to the supervision of the court shall, for the purpose of giving jurisdiction to the court, be deemed to be a petition for winding up by the court. [78, 79]

quizzes.scenario: A company in winding up owes N5,000,000 in PAYE tax deductions from the previous year. How is this ranked?
options: It is paid first as a cost of winding up.
It is paid in priority after costs but before secured creditors.
It ranks as an unsecured debt.
It is waived upon insolvency.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Under Section 657(1)(b) of CAMA 2020, all PAYE tax deductions and other assessed taxes not exceeding one year's assessment rank in priority after winding up costs. [12, 59]

quizzes.scenario: Directors of LLL Ltd make a declaration of solvency but do not attach a statement of assets and liabilities.
options: The declaration is valid if signed by all directors.
The declaration has no effect as it must embody a statement of assets and liabilities.
Valid only for private companies.
Creditors can approve it by ordinary resolution.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Section 625(2)(b) of CAMA 2020 requires the declaration of solvency to embody a statement of the company's assets and liabilities as at the latest practicable date before the making of the declaration. [7, 39, 80]

quizzes.scenario: Can a receiver/manager, appointed by a debenture holder, petition for the compulsory winding up of the company?
options: No, receivers have no such powers.
Yes, if authorized by the instrument under which he was appointed.
Yes, but only with the consent of the MD.
Only if the company has less than 2 members.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Section 573(1)(g) of CAMA 2020 includes a receiver, if authorized by the instrument under which he was appointed, as one of the parties who may petition for winding up. [23, 81]

quizzes.scenario: A prospective creditor wants to petition for the compulsory winding up of MMM Ltd. What additional requirement must he meet?
options: He must prove the debt is over N5,000,000.
He must establish a prima facie case and give security for costs.
He must get a letter of support from the CAC.
He must wait until the debt is 12 months overdue.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Under Section 573(2)(c) of CAMA 2020, the court shall not hear a petition by a contingent or prospective creditor until such security for costs has been given as the court thinks reasonable and a prima facie case for winding up has been established. [23, 46]

quizzes.scenario: A liquidator is appointed but it is later discovered he was an undischarged bankrupt in another country at the time of appointment.
options: His acts are valid until he is removed.
His appointment is void from the beginning.
He can continue if the court grants an extension.
He is only disqualified if the bankruptcy was in Nigeria.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Section 509(1)(d) and (2) of CAMA 2020 provides that an undischarged bankrupt is incompetent to be appointed as a liquidator and any such appointment is void. [15, 41, 82]

quizzes.scenario: A liquidator appointed by the court for NNN Ltd wishes to carry on the company's business to complete existing profitable contracts.
options: He needs a special resolution from members.
He has the power to carry on the business so far as is necessary for beneficial winding up.
He must get a separate court order for each contract.
Carrying on business is prohibited during winding up.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Section 588(1)(b) of CAMA 2020 empowers the liquidator in a winding up by the court to carry on the business of the company so far as may be necessary for the beneficial winding up thereof. [41, 82]

quizzes.scenario: After a compulsory winding-up order is made, a shareholder sells his shares to a third party. The liquidator is not informed and the court did not sanction it.
options: The sale is valid between the parties.
The transfer of shares is void.
The transfer is valid if the buyer acts in good faith.
The seller is liable for a fine of N500,000.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Section 576 of CAMA 2020 provides that in a winding up by the court, any transfer of shares or alteration in the status of members made after the commencement of winding up is void, unless the court orders otherwise. [16, 30, 31]

quizzes.scenario: Upon the making of a winding-up order by the court, who takes control of the company's property if no liquidator has been appointed?
options: The Petitioner's solicitor.
The Board of Directors.
The Official Receiver.
The Registrar-General of the CAC.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Under Section 585 of CAMA 2020, on the making of a winding-up order, if no liquidator is appointed, the official receiver shall by virtue of his office become the liquidator and shall take custody of all property. [42, 47, 48]

quizzes.scenario: Chief Emeka is charged before a Lagos State High Court for "disrespecting an elder," an act not defined as a crime in any written statute, though it is a serious cultural taboo. His lawyer objects to the charge. What is the most likely outcome?
options: The charge will be quashed.
The trial will proceed under customary law.
Emeka will be convicted based on common law.
The matter will be referred to a Sharia court.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Pursuant to Section 36(12) of the 1999 Constitution and the case of Aoko v. Fagbemi, no person shall be convicted of a criminal offence unless that offence is defined and the penalty is prescribed in a written law. [1], [2]

quizzes.scenario: A new procedural law, the Administration of Criminal Justice Act (ACJA), was enacted in 2015. A defendant argues that he cannot be tried under this law for a crime committed in 2014 because it is retroactive. How does the court distinguish between the substantive and procedural laws in this context?
options: Procedural laws cannot be applied to past crimes.
Criminal Law is substantive law while Criminal Litigation is procedural law; the latter applies to the process of trial regardless of when the crime occurred.
Both substantive and procedural laws must be in force at the time of the crime.
Procedural laws are only secondary enactments.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Criminal Law is the substantive law defining rights and duties, while Criminal Litigation is the procedural law governing the process; without the substantive law at the time of the act, there is no crime, but procedural law regulates the trial process itself. [1]

quizzes.scenario: A suspect is arrested in Ikeja for an offence committed entirely within Lagos State. During his trial at the Magistrate Court, the prosecutor attempts to apply the Criminal Procedure Code (CPC). Which law should actually govern the proceedings?
options: The Criminal Procedure Act (CPA).
The Administration of Criminal Justice Law (ACJL) of Lagos State.
The Administration of Criminal Justice Act (ACJA).
The Sharia Criminal Procedure Code.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: In Lagos State, the Administration of Criminal Justice Law (ACJL) governs proceedings in the High Court and Magistrates' Courts; the CPA is no longer applicable. [3]

quizzes.scenario: A legal practitioner is appearing before the Federal High Court in Abuja. A procedural issue arises for which there is no specific provision in the ACJA 2015. How should the court resolve this lacuna?
options: Apply the English High Court Rules of Practice.
Dismiss the case for want of procedure.
Adopt a procedure that will meet the justice of the case.
Apply the Sharia Criminal Procedure Code.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Under Section 492(3) of the ACJA, when there is a lacuna in the Act, the court shall apply a procedure that will meet the justice of the case. [4]

quizzes.scenario: During a high-profile trial of a former governor, the EFCC files the case in the Federal High Court, Kaduna, for an offence alleged to have been committed entirely in Delta State. The defendant objects based on the location. What principle applies here?
options: Doctrine of Res Judicata.
Doctrine of Forum Conveniens.
Principle of Stare Decisis.
Rule of Law.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: In FRN v. Ibori, the Court of Appeal held that criminal cases must be filed where the offence took place to prevent "forum shopping," which is considered malafide. [5]

quizzes.scenario: A Magistrate in Lagos decides to hold a special Saturday session to hear a matter concerning an application for bail for a suspect in custody. Is this sitting valid under the local laws?
options: No, Saturday is a dies non juridicus and any proceeding is void.
Yes, provided the Chief Judge gives oral permission.
Yes, Section 40(2) of the Magistrates' Court Law of Lagos allows at least one court per district to sit on Saturdays for bail and remand matters.
No, only the High Court can sit on Saturdays.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Section 40(2) of the Magistrates' Court Law of Lagos 2009 provides that at least one court in every magisterial district shall be open on Saturdays for hearing matters relating to remand and bail. [6]

quizzes.scenario: During a trial for an offence against morality involving a 15-year-old witness, the Judge orders the general public and the press to leave the courtroom. Is this order constitutional?
options: No, all trials must be held in public under Section 36(4).
Yes, the court has the discretion to exclude the public in the interest of public morality or when a minor is giving evidence in such cases.
No, the press can never be excluded from a courtroom.
Yes, but only if the Minister of Justice requests it.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Section 36(4) of the 1999 Constitution and Section 260 of the ACJA allow the exclusion of the public for the welfare of persons under 18 or on grounds of public decency and morality. [7]

quizzes.scenario: A defendant is brought before a Magistrate Court in Kano for a trial. The Magistrate conducts the entire proceeding in his private chambers rather than the open courtroom. Members of the public were not allowed in. What is the status of this trial?
options: The trial is valid because the Magistrate is a person of authority.
The trial is a nullity as it violates the requirement for trials to be held in public where there is unfettered ingress and egress.
The trial is valid if the defendant does not object.
The trial is valid only for summary offences.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Under Section 36(4) of the Constitution and the case of Oviasu v. Oviasu, a trial must be held in a public place with unfettered ingress and egress for the public; sitting in chambers generally violates this. [8], [9]

quizzes.scenario: A young lawyer arrives at the State High Court to observe a senior colleague. He is not robed. Where should he sit in the courtroom?
options: At the Bar.
On the Bench.
In the Gallery.
In the Dock.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: The Gallery is the part of the courtroom where spectators and unrobed lawyers sit; lawyers must be robed to sit at the Bar in superior courts. [10], [11]

quizzes.scenario: During a criminal trial, the accused person is asked to give his testimony. He chooses to do so from the Dock rather than the Witness Box. What is the legal implication of this choice?
options: He cannot be cross-examined, but the weight attached to his evidence will be low.
He will be cross-examined as usual.
His evidence will be struck out.
He must be sworn on the Quran or Bible while in the dock.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: If an accused testifies from the dock, he is not subject to cross-examination, but the probability and weight attached to such evidence is lower than if he testified from the witness box. [12]

quizzes.scenario: A police officer in Lagos obtains a search warrant on a Monday. He attempts to execute the warrant at a private residence at 11:00 PM on Tuesday. The occupant refuses entry, citing the time. Is the execution of the warrant lawful?
options: Yes, search warrants can be executed at any time in all jurisdictions.
No, in Lagos, a search warrant can only be executed between 5:00 AM and 8:00 PM unless the court directs otherwise.
Yes, as long as the officer has his ID card.
No, search warrants can only be executed on working days.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: In Lagos, per section 108(1) of the ACJL, a search warrant can only be executed between 5:00 AM and 8:00 PM unless the court authorizes another time. [13]

quizzes.scenario: A female suspect is arrested for shoplifting. A male police officer proceeds to search her person immediately at the scene to recover the stolen items. Is this search procedurally correct under the ACJA?
options: Yes, any officer can search any suspect.
No, the search of a woman must be carried out by another woman with strict regard to decency.
Yes, if there is no female officer available within 10 miles.
No, only a medical doctor can search a woman.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Section 9(3) of the ACJA and Section 5(2) of the ACJL Lagos provide that the search of a person shall be made by a person of the same sex and with strict regard to decency. [14]

quizzes.scenario: While executing a lawful search warrant for "stolen electronics," a police officer finds a bag of cocaine. The cocaine was not listed in the warrant. Can the officer lawfully seize the cocaine?
options: No, he can only seize what is listed in the warrant.
Yes, if he reasonably believes the items are relevant in respect of other offences.
No, he must obtain a second warrant first.
Yes, but only if the occupant consents.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Based on Reynolds v. Commissioner of Police, an officer executing a search warrant can lawfully seize items not specified if he reasonably believes they are stolen or evidence of another crime. [15]

quizzes.scenario: A Magistrate in Abuja issues a warrant of arrest for a suspect. Is it permissible for this warrant to be executed on a Sunday?
options: No, Sunday is a non-juridical day for all police actions.
Yes, a warrant of arrest may be issued or executed on any day, including a Sunday or public holiday.
Only if the suspect is seen in a church.
No, execution must wait until Monday morning.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Section 43(1) of the ACJA and Section 27(1) of the ACJL Lagos provide that a warrant of arrest may be executed on any day, including a Sunday or public holiday. [16]

quizzes.scenario: A police officer attempts to arrest a member of the House of Representatives within the chambers of the National Assembly while the House is in session. The officer has a valid warrant. Is this arrest lawful?
options: Yes, a warrant overrides all legislative privileges.
No, court processes or orders shall not be served or executed within the chambers or precinct of a Legislative House without permission.
Yes, but only if the Sergeant-at-Arms assists.
No, members of the National Assembly are immune from arrest for life.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Under Section 23 of the Legislative Houses (Powers and Privileges) Act 2017, court processes cannot be served or executed within the precinct of a Legislative House. [17]

quizzes.scenario: A suspect is arrested without a warrant by a police officer in Lagos. How soon must the suspect be brought before a court of competent jurisdiction?
options: Within 7 days.
Within 24 hours, or 48 hours if a court is not within 40km.
Within 1 month.
Immediately, without any delay for investigation.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Section 35(5) of the 1999 Constitution defines "reasonable time" for bringing an arrested person to court as one day (24 hours) where a court is within 40km, or two days (48 hours) otherwise. [18], [19]

quizzes.scenario: A private citizen witnesses a man committing an armed robbery (an indictable offence). The citizen tackles and detains the man. What is the citizen's immediate legal obligation?
options: He must try the man himself under customary law.
He must deliver the suspect to a police officer or take him to the nearest police station without unnecessary delay.
He must hold the suspect for 24 hours to see if he confesses.
He must release the suspect if no police officer arrives within one hour.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Section 14(1) of the ACJL Lagos and Section 23 of the ACJA require a private person who effects an arrest to hand over the suspect to a police officer or the nearest police station without delay. [20], [21]

quizzes.scenario: A Magistrate in Lagos issues a summons for a defendant to appear in court. The court bailiff serves the summons on the defendant's 10-year-old son at their residence. Is this valid service?
options: Yes, service on any family member is sufficient.
No, substituted service must be on an adult male member of the family and usually requires leave of court.
Yes, provided the son gives it to his father.
No, service must always be personal and cannot be substituted.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Substituted service of a summons is typically done by leaving a copy with an adult male member of the family or affixing it to the residence, often requiring leave of court. [22]

quizzes.scenario: A suspect is detained at a police station for investigation. He tells the officer, "I won't say anything until my lawyer arrives." The officer continues to pressure him to speak. Which constitutional right is being invoked?
options: Right to Life.
Right to remain silent under Section 35(2).
Right to Freedom of Movement.
Right to a Public Trial.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Section 35(2) of the 1999 Constitution provides that any person arrested or detained has the right to remain silent or avoid answering questions until after consultation with a legal practitioner. [23], [24]

quizzes.scenario: A Judge of the High Court of the FCT is informed that a suspect, for whom a warrant of arrest was issued, has absconded to a remote village to avoid trial. What tool can the Judge use to compel his appearance?
options: A fresh warrant of arrest.
A Public Summons published in a newspaper or affixed to his last known residence.
A Writ of Habeas Corpus.
A bench warrant for his wife's arrest.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Under Section 41 of the ACJA, where a court has reason to believe a person has absconded to frustrate a warrant, the Judge may publish a Public Summons requiring them to appear within 30 days. [25]

quizzes.scenario: Following the sudden resignation of the Attorney General of the Federation, the Solicitor General of the Federation enters a nolle prosequi in a high-profile corruption case pending at the Federal High Court. The defendant challenges the validity of this action. What is the correct position of the law?
options: The action is valid because the SG is an officer in the department of the AG.
The action is invalid because the power to enter a nolle prosequi cannot be exercised when there is no incumbent AG.
The action is valid as the AG is a corporation sole.
The action is valid only if the President expressly authorized the SG.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: By the rule in AG Kaduna v. Hassan , while the power to institute can be exercised by officers without a sitting AG, the power to enter a nolle prosequi under Section 174 or 211 of the 1999 Constitution requires an incumbent Attorney General [1, 2].

quizzes.scenario: Sergeant Okoro, a police officer who is not a legal practitioner, attempts to initiate and prosecute a case of simple stealing at the High Court of the Federal Capital Territory, Abuja. The defense counsel objects to his appearance. Will the objection succeed?
options: No, because the Police Act 2020 allows all police officers to prosecute.
No, because the High Court of FCT Act allows police prosecution.
Yes, because under Section 106 of the ACJA 2015, only a legal practitioner can prosecute in a court where the Act applies.
Yes, because police can only prosecute in Magistrate Courts.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Under Section 106 of the Administration of Criminal Justice Act (ACJA) 2015, prosecution in the Federal High Court, National Industrial Court, or FCT High Court must be undertaken by a law officer or a police officer who is a legal practitioner [3, 4].

quizzes.scenario: Chief Bayo seeks to initiate a private prosecution for murder against his business rival. He presents an Information to the Registrar of the High Court of Lagos State without an endorsement from the Attorney General. What is the consequence?
options: The Information will be accepted but the AG will later take it over.
The Information is incompetent as it must be endorsed by the AG showing a refusal to prosecute at public instance.
The Information is valid provided Chief Bayo enters a recognizance of N10,000.
The Information is valid because private persons have an unfettered right to prosecute.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Pursuant to Section 254 of the ACJL Lagos and the rule in Fawehinmi v. Akilu , a private person must obtain an endorsement from the AG stating they have declined to prosecute before an Information can be received [5, 6].

quizzes.scenario: Alhaji Musa was alleged to have committed the offense of Treason against the Federal Republic of Nigeria in January 2020. The prosecution attempts to file the charge in June 2023. Can Alhaji Musa be validly prosecuted?
options: Yes, because there is no time limit for criminal proceedings in Nigeria.
No, because the offense of Treason must be commenced within two years of its commission.
Yes, provided the Attorney General gives his consent.
No, because the offense is statute-barred after one year.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Under Section 43 of the Criminal Code, a person cannot be tried for treason or treasonable felonies unless the prosecution is commenced within two years after the offense was committed [7].

quizzes.scenario: The Attorney General of the Federation files an Information to commence a trial for money laundering against a former Minister at the Federal High Court. The defendant objects, arguing the mode of commencement is improper. What is the correct mode?
options: The mode is correct because the AG always proceeds by Information under Section 109 ACJA.
The mode is improper because actions in the Federal High Court must be commenced by a Charge.
The mode is correct as it is a summary trial.
The mode is improper because the AG must use a First Information Report.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Notwithstanding Section 109 of the ACJA, Section 33(1) and (2) of the Federal High Court Act provides that criminal proceedings in that court shall be commenced by a Charge, as established in FRN v. Obinna Ezerioha [8, 9].

quizzes.scenario: In a Magistrate Court in Kano, a police officer lays a First Information Report (FIR) against a suspect. The Magistrate immediately frames a charge and directs trial without hearing any prosecution witnesses. Is this procedure valid?
options: Yes, because the FIR itself constitutes sufficient evidence.
No, the Magistrate must first hear the prosecution's evidence and find a prima facie case before framing a charge in a long summary trial.
Yes, provided the suspect understands the FIR.
No, because only the police can frame a charge in the North.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Under the First Information Report procedure (Sections 126-129 ACJL Kano/Section 112 ACJA), if the defendant denies the allegation, the Magistrate must hear the prosecution's witnesses and determine if a prima facie case exists before framing and signing a formal charge [10, 11].

quizzes.scenario: The Attorney General of Oyo State initiates a prosecution for "Armed Robbery" under the Robbery and Firearms (Special Provisions) Act, a federal enactment. The defendant challenges the jurisdiction. Is the prosecution competent?
options: No, the AG of a State can only prosecute offenses created by the State House of Assembly.
Yes, because the State High Court has concurrent jurisdiction with the Federal High Court in all matters.
Yes, because under Section 315 of the Constitution and the rule in Emelogu v. State, federal laws on such matters are intended to operate as state laws.
No, unless the AG of the Federation specifically issued a fiat.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Per the Supreme Court in Emelogu v. State , certain federal laws like the Robbery and Firearms Act are intended to operate as State laws under Section 315 of the 1999 Constitution, allowing State AGs to prosecute without a specific fiat [12].

quizzes.scenario: A prosecutor in a trial under the ACJA 2015 wishes to discontinue the case after the defendant has already entered his defense. He applies to the court for a withdrawal. What is the effect of the court's order?
options: The defendant will be merely discharged and can be re-arrested.
The defendant will be discharged and acquitted.
The court will strike out the case and award costs to the AG.
The prosecutor must obtain a nolle prosequi from the AG first.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Under Section 108 of the ACJA (and similar provisions in ACJL Lagos Section 73), if a prosecutor withdraws a charge after the defendant has been called upon to make their defense, the court shall acquit the defendant [13].

quizzes.scenario: Mary wishes to prosecute her husband for adultery in a Magistrate Court in Northern Nigeria under the Penal Code. The police decline to charge him. Can Mary validly institute this action as a private person?
options: Yes, as an aggrieved person she has a right to private prosecution.
No, because adultery under the Penal Code can only be instituted by the husband of the woman or the father/guardian of an unmarried woman.
Yes, provided she obtains a fiat from the AG.
No, because adultery is only a civil wrong.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Under Section 387-389 of the Penal Code, there are statutory limitations on who can institute proceedings for adultery; a wife cannot institute such a charge against her husband [14].

quizzes.scenario: An EFCC investigator, who is not a legal practitioner, signs and files a charge for cybercrime at the Federal High Court. The defendant objects to the signature. How should the court rule?
options: The objection succeeds because all prosecutors must be legal practitioners under Section 106 ACJA.
The objection fails because the EFCC Act creates special prosecutors who need not be legal practitioners.
The objection succeeds because only the AG can sign a charge.
The objection fails because police officers have inherent power to sign charges.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Under Section 66 of the Police Act 2020 and the rules governing special prosecutors, certain statutes designate officers to initiate proceedings who may not necessarily be legal practitioners, serving as a derogation from the general rule in Section 106 ACJA [4, 15].

quizzes.scenario: During a trial, the prosecuting State Counsel discovers a police report that clearly exonerates the defendant, but he decides not to disclose it to the defense to ensure a conviction. Which rule of professional conduct has been breached?
options: Rule 1 of the RPC (Uphold the Rule of Law).
Rule 14 of the RPC (Diligence).
Rule 37(6) of the RPC, which mandates the disclosure of evidence that tends to negate the guilt of the accused.
Rule 15 of the RPC (Incompatible interests).
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Rule 37(6) of the Rules of Professional Conduct (RPC) and the rule in State v. Odofin Bello  place a duty on the prosecutor to disclose to the defense all evidence known to the prosecution that tends to negate the guilt of the accused [16, 17].

quizzes.scenario: A police officer in Lagos State attempts to commence criminal proceedings against a suspect in the Magistrate Court by "Laying a Complaint" orally. Is this a valid mode of commencement under the current law?
options: Yes, under the common law power of the police.
No, because under Section 78 of the ACJL Lagos, proceedings in the Magistrate Court must be commenced by bringing the person on a Charge Sheet.
Yes, provided the complaint is on oath.
No, because only the AG can lay a complaint in Lagos.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Unlike other jurisdictions, the Administration of Criminal Justice Law (ACJL) of Lagos State does not permit commencement by way of complaint in the Magistrate Court; Section 78 requires a Charge Sheet [18].

quizzes.scenario: The Police have already instituted a case of manslaughter against a driver in a Magistrate Court. The Attorney General of the State decides to take over the case and upgrade the charge to murder in the High Court. What is the extent of the AG's power here?
options: The AG cannot take over a case once it has started in the Magistrate Court.
The AG's power to take over is absolute and includes the power to change the charges as seen in Edet v. State.
The AG requires the leave of the Magistrate to take over the case.
The AG can only take over if the police are incompetent.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Under Sections 174(1)(b) and 211(1)(b) of the 1999 Constitution and the rule in Edet v. State, the AG has an absolute, unfettered power to take over and continue any criminal proceedings initiated by another authority [19, 20].

quizzes.scenario: A private legal practitioner is briefed by the Attorney General of the Federation to prosecute a case of terrorism. What document must he produce to prove his authority to the court?
options: A letter of engagement from the Ministry of Justice.
A General Power of Attorney.
A Fiat from the Attorney General.
A practicing certificate for the current year.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: A private legal practitioner acting on behalf of the Attorney General is said to enjoy the "Fiat" of the AG, which is the formal instrument of authority to prosecute the specific action [4, 21].

quizzes.scenario: An individual is accused of smuggling prohibited goods into Nigeria in 2014. The Nigeria Customs Service attempts to institute criminal proceedings under CEMA in 2022. Can the action proceed?
options: Yes, because there is no time limit for customs offenses.
No, because proceedings under the Customs and Excise Management Act must be commenced within 7 years of the offense.
Yes, provided the Minister of Finance approves.
No, because all crimes expire after 5 years.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Under Section 176(3) of the Customs and Excise Management Act (CEMA), there is a statutory limitation period of seven years for the commencement of proceedings for offenses under the Act [7].

quizzes.scenario: A defendant is convicted of robbery and filed an appeal to the Court of Appeal. While the appeal is pending, the Attorney General enters a nolle prosequi. What is the effect?
options: The defendant is immediately discharged.
The nolle prosequi is invalid because it can only be entered at the trial court before judgment.
The Court of Appeal must acquit the defendant.
The appeal is automatically dismissed.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Under the 1999 Constitution (Sections 174 and 211), a nolle prosequi can only be entered "at any stage before judgment is delivered." Once judgment is delivered by the trial court, the AG can no longer exercise this power [2].

quizzes.scenario: In the High Court of the Federal Capital Territory, the Attorney General of the Federation initiates a trial against a suspect. According to the ACJA, what is the mandatory mode of commencement?
options: By way of a Charge signed by a police officer.
By way of Information of the Attorney General.
By way of a First Information Report.
By way of an oral complaint on oath.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Under Section 109(b) of the ACJA 2015, where the Attorney General is initiating proceedings in the High Court, it must be done by way of Information [8].

quizzes.scenario: A prosecutor files a criminal charge against a politician purely for the purpose of helping a rival candidate's campaign, knowing that there is no probable evidence to support the charge. What ethical rule has been violated?
options: Rule 1 of the RPC (Duty to the state).
Rule 37(5) of the RPC, which prohibits instituting a charge not supported by probable evidence.
Rule 14 of the RPC (Duty of competence).
Rule 24 of the RPC (Right to decline brief).
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Rule 37(5) of the RPC explicitly states that a prosecuting counsel shall not institute or cause to be instituted a criminal charge if he knows or ought reasonably to know that the charge is not supported by probable evidence [16].

quizzes.scenario: A private person wishes to file an Information in the High Court of Lagos State for the offense of official corruption against a Judge. Whose signature must be on the Information?
options: The private person's signature is sufficient.
The Chief Judge of the State.
The signature of the Attorney General is mandatory under Section 98 of the Criminal Code.
The signature of the Chairman of the EFCC.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Under Section 98 of the Criminal Code, proceedings for official corruption against a judicial officer are specifically restricted, requiring the Information to be signed by the Attorney General [14].

quizzes.scenario: A State Counsel in the Ministry of Justice signs an Information "for the Attorney General" and files it in the High Court. The defense objects, claiming only the AG can sign. Is the objection valid?
options: Yes, the AG cannot delegate the power to sign an Information.
No, because Sections 174(2) and 211(2) of the Constitution allow the AG's powers to be exercised by officers of his department.
Yes, unless the State Counsel produces a gazetted delegation.
No, because any lawyer can sign an Information.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Under Sections 174(2) and 211(2) of the 1999 Constitution and the rule in Ibrahim v. State, the powers of the AG to institute and sign processes can be validly exercised by officers in his department [22, 23].

topics.topicName: week 3 - Jurisdiction and Venue of Criminal Trials

subtopics.subtopicName: Jurisdiction and Venue of Criminal Trials

quizzes.scenario: Ade, a resident of Lagos, is alleged to have stolen a laptop in Ikeja. After the theft, he immediately flees to Enugu where he is eventually arrested with the laptop. In which state can Ade be properly tried for the offence of stealing?
options: Only Lagos State
Only Enugu State
Both Lagos and Enugu States
Only the Federal Capital Territory
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: By virtue of Section 12A(2)(b) of the Criminal Code and the principles of territorial jurisdiction, where a person commits an act in one state and thereafter comes into another state, the state where he relocated to will have jurisdiction to try the matter as the offence is deemed to have occurred there [1].

quizzes.scenario: A Chief Magistrate in Lagos State, after a full trial, finds Okon guilty of an offence that carries a maximum penalty of 20 years imprisonment under the Criminal Law of Lagos. The Magistrate sentences Okon to 18 years imprisonment. What is the legal status of this sentence?
options: The sentence is valid as it is below the statutory maximum for the offence
The sentence is voidable at the instance of the Attorney General
The sentence is invalid because a Magistrate in Lagos cannot sentence a person to a term exceeding 14 years
The sentence is valid because Magistrates have unlimited sentencing powers
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Under Section 29(5) of the Magistrates' Court Law of Lagos 2009, a magistrate shall not sentence a person to a prison term of more than 14 years, regardless of the maximum penalty prescribed by the law creating the offence [2, 3].

quizzes.scenario: Sergeant Musa, an officer of the Nigeria Police Force, is accused of stealing service ammunition. He is brought before a Special Court Martial for trial. Musa's counsel objects to the jurisdiction. What is the likely outcome?
options: The objection will be upheld because Musa is not a person subject to service law
The objection will be overruled as all security agents are subject to military law
The objection will be overruled because the offence involves service ammunition
The objection will be upheld only if Musa is above the rank of Sergeant
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: By virtue of Section 130 and 291 of the Armed Forces Act, the jurisdiction of a Court Martial is limited to persons subject to service law (Army, Navy, or Air Force); police and custom officers are specifically not subject to service law [4].

quizzes.scenario: An offence of treason is allegedly committed by several individuals in Abuja. The Federal Government decides to arraign them before the Federal High Court in Maiduguri for security reasons. The defendants object, citing "forum shopping." Which case is most relevant to this scenario?
options: Madukolu v. Nkemdilim
FRN v. Ibori
Abiola v. FRN
Aoko v. Fagbemi
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: In Abiola v. FRN, the court held that since the Federal High Court has nationwide jurisdiction and treason is an offence of national character, a defendant can be tried at the Federal High Court in any division, though this is now balanced against the doctrine of forum conveniens in Ibori v. FRN [5].

quizzes.scenario: 16-year-old Chidi is charged with armed robbery alongside 30-year-old Tunde. The prosecution intends to try them together in the High Court. Chidi's father argues Chidi must be tried in a Juvenile Court. What is the correct legal position?
options: Chidi must be tried in the Juvenile Court while Tunde is tried in the High Court
Both must be tried in the Juvenile Court because one is a minor
Chidi can be tried in the High Court because he is charged jointly with an adult
Both must be tried by a Court Martial
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: According to Section 6(2) of the Children and Young Persons Law (CYPL), a juvenile court's sole jurisdiction is removed where the juvenile is charged jointly with an adult, in which case they are tried in a regular court [6, 7].

quizzes.scenario: The EFCC arraigns a former Governor for Money Laundering in the State High Court of Oyo State. The defense objects, stating only the Federal High Court can hear the matter. Is this objection valid?
options: No, because State High Courts have unlimited jurisdiction under Section 272 CFRN
Yes, because Section 23 of the Money Laundering (P&P) Act 2022 confers exclusive jurisdiction on the Federal High Court
No, because the EFCC Act provides for concurrent jurisdiction in all courts
Yes, but only if the Governor is still in office
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: While State High Courts generally share concurrent jurisdiction with the Federal High Court for federal offences, the National Assembly has the power to confer exclusive jurisdiction on the FHC via specific enactments like the Money Laundering Act [8].

quizzes.scenario: While traveling on a boat from Warri (Delta State) to Port Harcourt (Rivers State), Mr. X is robbed by a fellow passenger. The crime occurs in the middle of the river where the boundary is unclear. Where can the suspect be tried?
options: Only Delta State
Only Rivers State
Either Delta State or Rivers State
Only the National Industrial Court
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Under Section 96 of the ACJA and Section 58(2) of the ACJL Lagos, where an offence is commenced in one state and completed in another, or committed in the course of a journey, the suspect can be tried in any of the states passed [9].

quizzes.scenario: A Magistrate in Kano State conducts a summary trial for a non-capital offence. After conviction, the Magistrate realizes the appropriate punishment is more severe than his grade allows (e.g., 10 years). What should the Magistrate do?
options: Nullify the trial and release the defendant
Sentence the defendant to his maximum limit of 3 years
State the case to the High Court for the purpose of sentencing
Transfer the case to a Sharia Court
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: In the North, under Section 257(1) of the CPC (and reflected in local practice), a Magistrate who has conducted a valid trial and conviction but lacks jurisdiction to impose the necessary severe punishment may state the case to a higher court for sentencing [10, 11].

quizzes.scenario: John, a British citizen living in Kaduna, is accused of a simple offence under the Penal Code. He is brought before an Area Court Grade I. John's lawyer argues the court has no jurisdiction over a non-African. What is the correct rule?
options: Area Courts only have jurisdiction over indigenous Africans
John can be tried if he consents to the jurisdiction of the Area Court
John must be tried by the International Criminal Court
Area Courts have no jurisdiction over simple offences
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Under Section 15(1)(c) of the Area Courts Act/Edict, while the court primarily serves those indigenous to Africa, it can exercise jurisdiction over any person who consents to be tried by it [12, 13].

quizzes.scenario: A High Court Judge in the middle of a complex criminal trial is elevated to the Court of Appeal. He intends to continue sitting as a High Court Judge to conclude the part-heard matter as permitted by Section 396(7) ACJA. Is this allowed?
options: Yes, to ensure speedy dispensation of justice
No, the section has been declared unconstitutional by the Supreme Court
Yes, provided the Chief Justice of Nigeria gives a fiat
No, unless the defendant consents
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: The Supreme Court in Ude Jones Udeogu v. FRN declared Section 396(7) of the ACJA unconstitutional, meaning an elevated judge cannot return to the High Court to conclude part-heard matters [14, 15].

quizzes.scenario: A passenger is caught with cocaine at the Aminu Kano International Airport. The NDLEA wants to prosecute him. Which court has the appropriate substantive jurisdiction?
options: Magistrate Court Kano
Sharia Court Kano
State High Court Kano
Federal High Court
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Section 26 of the NDLEA Act confers exclusive jurisdiction on the Federal High Court for drug-related offences [8, 16].

quizzes.scenario: A suspect is charged with murder in the Magistrate Court of Lagos. The Magistrate proceeds to take the defendant's plea and begins the trial. What is the status of these proceedings?
options: Valid, provided it is a summary trial
Voidable at the instance of the High Court
A nullity, as Magistrates lack jurisdiction over capital offences
Valid, if the Attorney General gives a fiat
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Magistrates' courts lack original jurisdiction over capital offences such as murder; they can only handle such matters through remand (holding) processes, but cannot take a plea or conduct a trial [17, 18].

quizzes.scenario: A General Court Martial is convened to try a Colonel. The panel consists of a President (a Brigadier General), three members of the rank of Colonel, and a Judge Advocate. Is the court properly constituted?
options: No, it must have at least five members plus the President
Yes, as there are four members total excluding adjuncts
No, members must all be Brigadier Generals
Yes, provided the Judge Advocate has a vote
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Section 129 of the Armed Forces Act requires a General Court Martial to consist of a President and at least four other members. Adjuncts like the Judge Advocate and Liaison Officer are not counted toward this minimum [19, 20].

quizzes.scenario: A 12-year-old boy is accused of murdering his playmate. The prosecution files the charge in the Juvenile Court. The defense objects to the venue. Is the objection valid?
options: No, the Juvenile Court has sole jurisdiction over all minors
Yes, capital offences by juveniles must be tried in the High Court
No, because he is under 14 years old
Yes, he must be tried by a Sharia Court
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Under Section 8(2) of the Children and Young Persons Law, the Juvenile Court's jurisdiction is excluded where the charge is a capital offence, such as homicide [6, 7].

quizzes.scenario: A Chief Magistrate in the FCT is assigned to try an accused person for an offence carrying a 12-year sentence. What is the maximum sentence this Magistrate can actually impose?
options: 12 years
14 years
10 years
5 years
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: In the FCT, the maximum sentencing jurisdiction for a Chief Magistrate I is 10 years or a fine not exceeding N50,000 [21, 22].

quizzes.scenario: A defendant is charged with "Conduct Prejudicial to Public Peace" in a state where no such written law exists. He is convicted. On appeal, what principle will the court apply?
options: Presumption of regularity
Audi Alteram Partem
Section 36(12) of the 1999 Constitution (Offence must be defined in written law)
Nemo Judex in Causa Sua
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Section 36(12) of the 1999 Constitution stipulates that no person shall be convicted of a criminal offence unless it is defined and the penalty is prescribed in a written law. This was the basis for the decision in Aoko v. Fagbemi [23, 24].

quizzes.scenario: A Magistrate in the North is asked to try a Muslim man for drinking alcohol in public. Which court has the most appropriate jurisdiction for this specific offender and offence?
options: Area Court
Sharia Court
High Court
National Industrial Court
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Sharia Courts in the North have original jurisdiction over Muslims, especially for specific Islamic-related offences or those carrying punishments like Haddi lashing (e.g., drinking alcohol) [25, 26].

quizzes.scenario: A military officer is accused of desertion that occurred 5 years ago. His lawyer argues the trial is statute-barred as it is past 3 years. Is this correct?
options: Yes, Section 169(1) AFA imposes a 3-year limit on all military offences
No, there is no time limit for the offence of desertion
Yes, provided he has retired
No, but only if the AGF gives consent
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: While Section 169(1) of the Armed Forces Act generally imposes a 3-year limitation for military trials, subsection (2) specifically excludes desertion, mutiny, and failure to suppress mutiny from this time bar [27, 28].

quizzes.scenario: A civil dispute arises between a landlord and tenant over unpaid rent. The landlord reports the matter to the police, who arrest the tenant for "stealing" the rent money. Which case condemns this use of police for civil matters?
options: Madukolu v. Nkemdilim
Mclaren v. Jennings
Ibori v. FRN
Okoro v. State
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: In Mclaren v. Jennings, the court held that the power of the police to investigate does not extend to civil disputes such as debt collection or breach of contract [29].

quizzes.scenario: A woman is convicted of a capital offence in Lagos. During the allocutus, her counsel proves she is four months pregnant. What is the mandatory sentence?
options: Death by hanging
Death by lethal injection
Life imprisonment
14 years imprisonment
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Under Section 302(2) of the ACJL Lagos, a pregnant woman shall not be sentenced to death but shall instead be sentenced to life imprisonment (Note: this differs from the ACJA position of suspended execution) [30, 31].

topics.topicName: week 4 - Pre-Trial Investigation and Police Interviews

quizzes.scenario: Mr. Okafor is arrested for alleged theft of a transformer in Abuja. Upon arrival at the police station, the Investigating Police Officer (IPO) insists on taking his statement immediately, but Mr. Okafor demands to wait for his lawyer who is on his way. The IPO claims that under the law, a suspect must give a statement upon arrest.
options: Mr. Okafor must give his statement immediately to avoid obstruction of justice
Mr. Okafor has a right to remain silent until he consults with a legal practitioner of his choice
Mr. Okafor can only wait for a lawyer if he is charged with a capital offence
The IPO is right because pre-trial investigation is an executive discretion
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Under Section 35(2) of the 1999 Constitution and Section 17(1) of the ACJA, any person arrested or detained has the right to remain silent or avoid answering questions until after consultation with a legal practitioner or any other person of his own choice [1, 2].

quizzes.scenario: In Ikeja, Lagos, a suspect, Kunle, volunteers to make a confessional statement regarding a robbery. The police station has a functional video recording room, but the IPO decides to write it manually instead, claiming it is faster.
options: The manual statement is perfectly valid and preferred in Lagos
The statement is inadmissible because it was not recorded on video as required by law where facilities are available
The statement is only valid if signed by a Magistrate
The IPO has the discretion to choose the mode of recording
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Under Section 9(3) of the ACJL Lagos, where a suspect volunteers to make a confessional statement, it shall be recorded on video, and only in the absence of video facilities shall it be in writing in the presence of a legal practitioner [3, 4].

quizzes.scenario: Chief Emeka is invited by the Police in Kano to "clarify issues" regarding a N50 Million debt he owes a contractor. The IPO threatens to detain him until the money is paid, claiming the police have wide powers to prevent crime.
options: The police have the power to investigate any matter involving money
The police cannot investigate or involve themselves in purely civil disputes like debt collection
The police can detain him if the contractor provides an indemnity bond
Chief Emeka must pay 10% of the debt to the police to be released
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: The power of the police to investigate is limited to crimes and does not extend to civil disputes such as debt collection or breach of contract, as held in Mclaren v. Jennings and supported by Section 8(2) of the ACJA and Section 32(2) of the Police Act 2020 [5].

quizzes.scenario: During an interview of a suspect, Musa, who does not understand English, the IPO uses a fellow suspect who speaks Musa's language to interpret and record the statement. Musa eventually signs the statement.
options: The statement is valid because it was signed by the suspect
The statement is inadmissible because an interpreter must be independent and cannot be a co-suspect
The statement is valid as long as the co-suspect is not a lawyer
Musa waived his rights by signing the statement
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: A suspect is entitled to a competent and independent interpreter where he does not understand English; using a co-suspect is improper because an interpreter must not be interested in the outcome of the matter [6, 7].

quizzes.scenario: A Legal Practitioner, Barrister Bola, is briefed to represent a suspect detained for simple assault. To ensure his client's release on police bail, Barrister Bola offers to sign the bail bond as a surety since the client's relatives are far away.
options: Barrister Bola is acting in the best interest of his client
Barrister Bola is prohibited from standing as surety for a person for whom he is appearing as counsel
Barrister Bola can stand as surety only in simple offences
The IPO can accept Barrister Bola as a surety if he provides his NBA seal
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Under Rule 37 of the Rules of Professional Conduct (RPC), a legal practitioner shall not stand as surety for any person for whom he is appearing as a counsel or in any matter in which he is professionally engaged [8, 9].

quizzes.scenario: The Police in Lagos decide to conduct an identification parade for a suspect, Tunde. They line up Tunde with three other persons of different heights and status. Tunde's lawyer objects to the composition.
options: The lawyer is wrong as four people are sufficient for a line-up
The identification parade is irregular because there must be at least eight other persons of similar description
Tunde should be the only person in the line-up for clarity
The police can use photographs instead of a physical parade
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: For a valid identification parade, the suspect should be planted in the midst of at least eight other persons (making a total of nine) who are of similar description in height, complexion, and status [10].

quizzes.scenario: A suspect, Chinedu, has a very distinctive tribal mark on his left cheek. During an identification parade, the IPO ensures that all other participants in the line-up are given temporary markings or coverings to hide their cheeks.
options: This is an attempt by the IPO to confuse the witness
The IPO's action is correct as steps should be taken to conceal or replicate distinctive physical features
Chinedu should have been identified alone due to his unique mark
The identification parade is void because participants were altered
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Where a suspect has an unusual physical feature that cannot be easily replicated, steps should be taken to conceal that special feature to ensure the identification is based on more than just that one obvious mark [11, 12].

quizzes.scenario: Alhaji Musa is arrested for a crime committed in Lagos on Monday at 10:00 AM. He claims he was in a board meeting in Kano at that exact time and provides the names of three directors who can verify this. The IPO ignores this and charges him to court.
options: Alhaji Musa can only raise this defence during his trial
The failure of the police to investigate a timeously raised alibi is fatal to the prosecution's case
Alibi is a matter for the court to decide, not the police
Alhaji Musa has the legal burden to prove his alibi in court first
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Alibi must be raised at the earliest opportunity (during investigation) to give the police the chance to verify it. Failure by the police to investigate a properly raised alibi raises reasonable doubt and is fatal to the prosecution's case, as held in Onafowokan v. The State [13, 14].

quizzes.scenario: A suspect, Sarah, is arrested for murder. Her lawyer applies to the DPO for police bail, arguing that Sarah is a nursing mother.
options: The DPO must grant bail because Sarah is a nursing mother
The DPO has no power to grant bail in capital offences
Sarah can be granted bail only if she pays an administrative fee
The DPO can grant bail if a traditional ruler stands as surety
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Police have no power to release a suspect on bail in capital offences, such as murder; such bail can only be granted by a High Court Judge [15, 16].

quizzes.scenario: Mr. John is arrested for a non-capital offence in Lagos. After 72 hours in custody without being taken to court, his lawyer applies to a Magistrate for his release. The police argue the Magistrate has no jurisdiction since the trial hasn't started.
options: The police are right; the Magistrate only has power after arraignment
The Magistrate has power under Section 18 of the ACJL Lagos to order the production of the suspect and admit him to bail
Only the High Court can entertain such an application through Habeas Corpus
Mr. John must wait for the DPP's advice before seeking release
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Under Section 18 of the ACJL Lagos, a Magistrate with jurisdiction over the offence may, upon an oral or written application, order the production of a person detained beyond 24 hours and admit him to bail [17, 18].

quizzes.scenario: During a police interview, an IPO tells a suspect, "If you tell us the truth about how you stole the car, we will ensure the complainant withdraws the case." The suspect then confesses.
options: The confession is admissible because it is the truth
The confession is inadmissible because it was obtained by an inducement or promise which makes it involuntary
The confession is admissible if recorded on video
The IPO's promise is a valid exercise of prosecutorial discretion
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Under Section 29(2) of the Evidence Act, a confession is inadmissible if it was obtained by oppression or in consequence of anything said or done likely to render it unreliable. Inducements or promises from a person in authority vitiate the voluntariness of the confession [19, 20].

quizzes.scenario: An IPO is conducting an interview. He begins by offering the suspect a seat and water, and talks about their common love for football before asking about the alleged crime. What stage of the interview is this?
options: The Challenge Stage
The Rapport Building Stage
The Information Exchange Stage
The Concluding Stage
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: The first stage of a police interview is the Rapport Building Stage (or Meet & Greet), intended to make the witness or suspect feel comfortable and establish a connection [21].

quizzes.scenario: A suspect, Segun, is arrested for conspiracy. The police also arrest Segun's brother, who had no involvement, just to force Segun to confess.
options: This is a valid investigative strategy known as "Target Based Investigation"
This is "Arrest in Lieu" and is strictly prohibited by law
The police can arrest any relative for 24 hours for questioning
Segun's brother can be held until Segun signs a bond
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Arrest and detention of a person in lieu of another person is strictly prohibited under Section 7 of the ACJA, Section 4 of the ACJL Lagos, and Section 36 of the Police Act 2020 [5, 22].

quizzes.scenario: A suspect is arrested for armed robbery. The IPO fails to inform him that he has a right to legal representation and a right to remain silent. The IPO claims Segun should know his rights as a Nigerian.
options: The IPO is right; ignorance of the law is no excuse
The IPO has a mandatory duty to inform the suspect of his rights immediately upon arrest
The IPO only needs to inform the suspect of his rights if he asks
Rights only apply when a suspect is in court
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Under Section 6 of the ACJA and Section 3(1) of the ACJL Lagos, a police officer or person making an arrest shall inform the arrested person of his rights to remain silent, to consult a legal practitioner, and to free legal aid [2, 23].

quizzes.scenario: After a search of a suspect's house, the IPO recovers a laptop and several phones. He places them in his personal locker at the station for "safekeeping" until the next morning.
options: The IPO is following the rule of "Safe Custody"
The recovered items must be handed to the exhibit keeper and registered in the Exhibit Register
The IPO can keep the items if he is the lead investigator
The items should be given back to the suspect's wife for safekeeping
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Items recovered during a search (exhibits) must be handed to the exhibit keeper, registered in the Exhibit Register, and kept in the exhibit room designed for that purpose [24, 25].

quizzes.scenario: A suspect, Okon, is an illiterate and wants to make a statement. The IPO offers to write the statement for him. Before starting, what must the IPO do according to the Judges' Rules?
options: The IPO must ask Okon to pay a fee for the service
The IPO must ask Okon to sign or make his mark to a specific preamble stating he wants the officer to write for him and he has been cautioned
The IPO must call a Magistrate to witness the writing
The IPO must wait for a video camera to be provided
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Under the Judges' Rules, if an illiterate suspect accepts an officer's offer to write a statement, the officer must first have the suspect sign a preamble stating he wishes to make a statement, wants someone to write it, and understands it can be used in evidence [26].

quizzes.scenario: A prosecutor discovers during pre-trial investigation that there is a witness who saw the suspect elsewhere during the time of the crime, but this witness was not included in the police file.
options: The prosecutor should suppress this information to ensure conviction
The prosecutor has a duty to disclose to the defense any evidence that tends to negate the guilt of the accused
The prosecutor should only disclose if the defense counsel asks
The prosecutor must only call witnesses who support the state's case
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Under Rule 37(6) of the RPC, a prosecuting counsel has a duty to disclose to the accused or his counsel the existence of evidence known to the prosecution that tends to negate the guilt of the accused [27, 28].

quizzes.scenario: Following an arrest, the IPO records an inventory of the items recovered from the suspect. The suspect refuses to sign the inventory, claiming some items are missing.
options: The inventory is void because the suspect didn't sign
The failure of the suspect to sign the inventory shall not invalidate it
The IPO must release the suspect if he doesn't sign
The IPO must use force to make the suspect sign
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Under Section 10(2) of the ACJA, the inventory of recovered items is to be signed by the police officer and the arrested person, but the failure of the arrested person to sign shall not invalidate it [24, 29].

quizzes.scenario: A suspect is arrested and taken to a police station in Abuja. The IPO refuses to allow him to call his family, stating that "investigation is secret."
options: The IPO is right; communication is only allowed after charging to court
The authority having custody has a responsibility to notify the suspect's next of kin at no cost to the suspect
Only a lawyer can call the family on behalf of the suspect
The suspect must pay for the phone call before it is allowed
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Under Section 6(2) of the ACJA, the authority having custody of a suspect has the responsibility of notifying the next of kin or relative of the suspect of the arrest at no cost to the suspect [22, 23].

quizzes.scenario: A suspect, Adamu, makes a statement to an undercover police officer who is posing as a fellow inmate in a cell. Adamu later claims the statement is inadmissible because he wasn't cautioned.
options: The statement is inadmissible as it violates the right to silence
A statement made to an undercover police officer posing as a criminal is voluntary and admissible
Undercover evidence is only admissible in terrorism cases
Adamu must be cautioned even by undercover officers
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: A statement made to an undercover police officer who poses as a criminal is considered voluntary and is admissible in evidence, as held in Igbinovia v. The State [30].

topics.topicName: week 5 - Arrest, Searches and Constitutional Rights

quizzes.scenario: Chidinma was arrested in Lagos for shoplifting. A male police officer, Sergeant Bakare, proceeded to search her body in public to recover the stolen items. Chidinma's lawyer challenges the search as a violation of her rights.
options: The search is valid because it was done in a public place.
The search is invalid because a woman must be searched by a person of the same sex with strict regard to decency.
The search is valid because Sergeant Bakare is a law enforcement officer.
The search is invalid only if Chidinma can prove Sergeant Bakare touched her inappropriately.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Under Section 5(2) of the ACJL Lagos and Section 9(3) of the ACJA, the law provides that whenever it is necessary to search a person, the search shall be made decently and by a person of the same sex. [1]

quizzes.scenario: Inspector Danjuma of the Nigeria Police Force believes that a warehouse in Abuja contains stolen kidneys. He issues a search warrant himself and leads a team to search the premises at 2:00 PM.
options: The search is valid because the Inspector is a senior police officer.
The search is invalid because only a Judge, Magistrate, or Justice of the Peace can issue a search warrant.
The search is valid because kidney trafficking is a serious offence.
The search is invalid because it was conducted in the afternoon.
