quizzes.verbatimCorrection: According to Sections 106(1) of the ACJL Lagos and 146(1) of the ACJA, only a Judge, Magistrate, or Justice of the Peace has the authority to issue a search warrant; police officers, regardless of rank, cannot issue search warrants. [2]

quizzes.scenario: Police officers in Ikeja, Lagos, obtained a valid search warrant to search Mr. Emeka's house for illegal firearms. They arrived at the house and executed the warrant at 11:30 PM without any specific endorsement for night execution.
options: The execution is valid as a search warrant can be executed at any time in Lagos.
The execution is invalid because in Lagos, a search warrant can only be executed between 5:00 AM and 8:00 PM unless the court orders otherwise.
The execution is valid because the warrant was for firearms.
The execution is invalid because searches cannot be done on weekdays.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Section 108(1) of the ACJL Lagos stipulates that a search warrant shall be executed between the hours of 5:00 AM and 8:00 PM, unless the court in its discretion orders that it be executed at any hour. [3]

quizzes.scenario: A police officer in Kano seeks to search a house occupied by Aisha, a woman in purdah who is not the subject of the search. The officer breaks the door and enters without giving Aisha notice to withdraw.
options: The search is valid because Aisha is not a suspect.
The search is invalid because the officer failed to give Aisha reasonable notice and facility to withdraw before entering.
The search is valid as long as the officer is a male.
The search is invalid only if Aisha was actually naked at the time.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Under Sections 7(3) of the ACJL Lagos and 149(6) of the ACJA, when a premises to be searched is occupied by a woman in purdah who is not the person to be searched, she must be given reasonable notice and facility to withdraw before the warrant is executed. [4]

quizzes.scenario: Sergeant Okon arrested Musa in Abuja for an alleged robbery. Musa was not informed of the reason for his arrest until 48 hours later while in custody.
options: The arrest is valid because Musa was caught near a crime scene.
The arrest is invalid because a suspect must be informed of the facts and grounds of his arrest in writing within 24 hours.
The arrest is valid because the police have 48 hours to inform a suspect.
The arrest is invalid only if Musa is illiterate.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Section 35(3) of the 1999 Constitution and Section 6(1) of the ACJA provide that any person who is arrested or detained shall be informed in writing within 24 hours of the facts and grounds for his arrest or detention. [5, 6]

quizzes.scenario: Tunde, a private citizen, caught a man jumping over his fence with a stolen laptop in Lagos. Tunde tied the man up and kept him in his generator house for two days before handing him over to the police.
options: Tunde acted lawfully as he has the power of a police officer after a private arrest.
Tunde is liable for false imprisonment because a private person must deliver a suspect to a police officer or the nearest police station without unnecessary delay.
Tunde acted lawfully because the man was caught in the act.
Tunde is only liable if he did not provide the man with food.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Section 14(1) of the ACJL Lagos and Section 23 of the ACJA provide that a private person effecting an arrest without a warrant must without unnecessary delay deliver the suspect to a police officer or to the nearest police station. [7, 8]

quizzes.scenario: The police in Abuja could not find Mr. Adams, who is suspected of fraud. They proceeded to arrest his wife, Sarah, to force Mr. Adams to report to the station.
options: Sarah's arrest is valid as it is an "arrest in lieu."
Sarah's arrest is unlawful as the law prohibits the arrest of any person in place of a suspect.
Sarah's arrest is valid if she is a co-signatory to his accounts.
Sarah's arrest is valid because she is his next of kin.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Section 7 of the ACJA and Section 4 of the ACJL Lagos expressly prohibit "arrest in lieu," stating that no person shall be arrested in place of a suspect. [9, 10]

quizzes.scenario: During a non-violent arrest for a simple offence in Lagos, Sergeant Musa slapped the suspect and used leg chains to restrain him despite the suspect submitting to custody peacefully.
options: The use of force is lawful to assert police authority.
The treatment is unlawful as a suspect shall not be subjected to unnecessary restraint or dehumanizing acts except by court order or for safety.
The use of chains is mandatory for all criminal arrests.
The treatment is lawful if the suspect is a repeat offender.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Section 5 of the ACJA and Section 2 of the ACJL Lagos provide that a person arrested shall not be subjected to more restraint than is necessary to prevent his escape, and Section 34 of the Constitution protects the right to dignity. [11-13]

quizzes.scenario: A male police officer in Abuja arrested a female suspect and searched her handbag without waiting for a female officer to arrive. The suspect claims her right to privacy was violated.
options: The search is invalid because only a woman can search a woman's belongings.
The search is valid because the rule requiring a same-sex search of a person does not extend to the person's appurtenances like a handbag.
The search is invalid because a warrant is always needed for handbags.
The search is valid only if the bag was open.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: The rule that a search of a person must be conducted by a person of the same sex does not extend to a woman's appurtenances, such as a handbag, which can be searched by an officer of the opposite sex. [14]

quizzes.scenario: Police in Lagos arrested Segun using a warrant issued three months prior. Segun had been previously arrested and released using the same warrant for the same offence.
options: The arrest is valid because warrants remain in force for one year.
The arrest is invalid because once a warrant of arrest has been executed, it lapses and cannot be used again.
The arrest is valid because Segun is still a suspect.
The arrest is valid if the Magistrate re-signed the warrant.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: As held in R v. Akinyeju and supported by Section 39(2) of the ACJA, a warrant of arrest remains in force until executed or cancelled; once executed, it loses its lifespan and cannot be reused. [15-17]

quizzes.scenario: Emeka was arrested in Abuja for a felony. He requested the police to inform his brother of his whereabouts, but the police refused, claiming they needed to finish investigation first.
options: The police are correct to keep the arrest secret for investigation purposes.
The police have a duty to notify the next of kin or relative of the suspect of the arrest at no cost to the suspect.
Notification is only mandatory for capital offences.
Notification is only required after 48 hours.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Section 6(2) of the ACJA introduces a novel provision making it the responsibility of the authority having custody of a suspect to notify the next of kin or relative of the arrest at no cost. [9, 18]

quizzes.scenario: A police officer in Ikeja has a warrant to arrest Mr. Kunle. He sees Mr. Kunle enter a private residence and demands entry, which is refused by the landlord. The officer breaks the door to enter.
options: The officer is liable for trespass as he had no search warrant for the house.
The officer acted lawfully as he has the power of ingress to any premises where he reasonably suspects a person to be arrested has entered.
The officer is only allowed to enter if the landlord is a suspect.
The officer should have waited for the suspect to come out.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Sections 7(1) of the ACJL Lagos and 12(1) of the ACJA provide that a person in charge of a premises shall allow free access to an officer executing an arrest warrant, and the officer may use force to break in if ingress is denied. [19-21]

quizzes.scenario: While being interrogated at a police station in Abuja, Ibrahim remained silent and refused to answer any questions until he consulted his lawyer. The IPO threatened to beat him if he did not speak.
options: Ibrahim is guilty of obstruction of justice by remaining silent.
Ibrahim has a constitutional right to remain silent or avoid answering questions until after consultation with a legal practitioner.
Ibrahim can only remain silent if he is charged with a capital offence.
Silence is only a right during the actual trial in court.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Section 35(2) of the 1999 Constitution and Section 17(1) of the ACJA guarantee the right of a suspect to remain silent or avoid answering any question until after consultation with a legal practitioner of his choice. [6, 13, 22]

quizzes.scenario: During a search of a house in Lagos for "stolen laptops" specified in a warrant, the police found and seized a blood-stained shirt they reasonably believed was used in a murder. The owner challenges the seizure as illegal.
options: The seizure is illegal because the shirt was not listed in the search warrant.
The seizure is lawful as an officer executing a warrant can seize items not specified if he reasonably believes they are relevant to other offences.
The shirt is only admissible if the murder happened in the same house.
The police must return the shirt and get a new warrant.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Based on Reynolds v. Commissioner of Police, where an officer executing a search warrant finds items he reasonably believes to be stolen or relevant to other offences, he can lawfully seize them even if not specified. [23]

quizzes.scenario: A Magistrate in Kano issued a warrant of arrest for a suspect but died two weeks later before the warrant could be executed. The suspect claims the warrant is now invalid.
options: The warrant became invalid upon the death of the Magistrate.
The death, resignation, or retirement of the issuing authority does not affect the validity of a search or arrest warrant.
The warrant must be re-signed by the new Magistrate.
The warrant is only valid for 24 hours after the issuer's death.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Under Sections 146(2) of the ACJA and 100 of the ACJL Lagos, the death, resignation, retirement, or cease of jurisdiction of the issuing authority does not affect the validity of the warrant. [16, 24, 25]

quizzes.scenario: A police officer in Abuja arrested a suspect for a simple traffic offence. The suspect tried to run away, and the officer shot and killed him to prevent the escape.
options: The killing is justified to prevent the escape of any suspect.
The killing is unlawful as force that causes death is not permissible unless the suspect is armed with a dangerous weapon or poses a threat of death/grievous harm.
Shooting a fleeing suspect is mandatory under the Police Act.
The officer is only liable if he missed the leg.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: In Queen v. Agbochi and Section 33(2)(b) of the Constitution, the use of force must be reasonable; lethal force is only permitted when necessary to protect life or when the suspect is armed with dangerous weapons. [12, 26]

quizzes.scenario: A private person caught a suspect in Lagos committing a misdemeanour at 11:00 PM. The private person arrested the suspect without a warrant.
options: The arrest is illegal because private persons can only arrest for felonies.
The arrest is lawful because a private person can arrest anyone who reasonably commits a misdemeanour by night.
The arrest is illegal because it was done after 8:00 PM.
The arrest is only valid if the suspect confessed immediately.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Section 12 of the ACJL Lagos and Section 20 of the ACJA empower a private person to arrest without a warrant any person who reasonably commits a misdemeanour by night or an indictable offence in his presence. [7]

quizzes.scenario: A commercial bank in Abuja instructed the police to arrest a customer who had defaulted on a personal loan, claiming it was a criminal act.
options: The arrest is lawful as debt default is a crime in Nigeria.
The arrest is unlawful because the law prohibits arresting a person for a purely civil wrong or breach of contract.
The arrest is valid if the amount exceeds one million Naira.
The arrest is valid only if the bank manager signs the warrant.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Section 8(2) of the ACJA and Section 32(2) of the Police Act 2020 provide that a suspect shall not be arrested merely on a civil wrong or breach of contract. [9, 10]

quizzes.scenario: Police officers entered the chambers of the Lagos State House of Assembly to arrest a lawmaker during a plenary session, without the permission of the Speaker.
options: The arrest is lawful as no one is above the law.
The arrest is invalid because court processes and arrest orders shall not be served or executed within the precinct of a Legislative House without permission.
Lawmakers have absolute immunity from arrest anywhere.
The arrest is valid only if the offence is treason.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Section 23 of the Legislative Houses (Powers and Privileges) Act 2017 provides that court processes or orders shall not be served or executed in the chambers or precinct of a Legislative House. [3, 27, 28]

quizzes.scenario: Chinedu was arrested in Abuja on Monday morning. By Thursday evening, he was still being held in a police cell without being taken to court, despite there being a Magistrate Court 5km away.
options: The detention is lawful as the police have 7 days to investigate.
The detention is a violation of Chinedu's right to personal liberty as he must be brought before a court within a reasonable time (24 hours where a court is nearby).
The police can hold him for as long as necessary if he is not on bail.
Reasonable time means two weeks in Abuja.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Section 35(4) and (5) of the 1999 Constitution define "reasonable time" for arraignment as one day (24 hours) where there is a court of competent jurisdiction within a radius of 40 kilometres. [5, 29, 30]

quizzes.scenario: Justice Alao decides to hear the testimony of a key witness in a high-profile corruption case in his private chambers to prevent the press from reporting sensitive details. The defendant, Ade, objects to this procedure. What is the legal implication?
options: The trial is valid if the Minister of Justice certifies it.
The trial is a nullity as it violates the right to a public trial.
The trial is valid because the Judge has inherent power to regulate his court.
The trial is valid as long as Ade's counsel is present in the chambers.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: S. 36(3) of the 1999 Constitution and S. 200 ACJL require criminal proceedings to be held in public [1, 2]. A Judge's chambers is not a public place because it lacks unfettered ingress and egress by the public [1].

quizzes.scenario: Musa, a native of a remote village in Adamawa who only speaks Idoma, is charged with theft in a Lagos Magistrate Court. The charge is read in English, and Musa remains silent because he does not understand. The court enters a plea of "Not Guilty" and proceeds without an interpreter.
options: The trial is valid because Musa should have provided his own interpreter.
The trial is valid as English is the official language of Nigerian courts.
The trial is a nullity for breach of Musa's right to an interpreter.
The trial is valid if Musa is represented by a lawyer who speaks English.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Under S. 36(6)(e) of the 1999 Constitution, every person charged with an offence is entitled to an interpreter at no cost if he does not understand the language of the court [3, 4]. Failure to provide an interpreter when needed renders the trial a nullity [5].

quizzes.scenario: Chidi is charged with murder. During the trial, his counsel, Mr. Ben, is absent on several occasions. The trial judge, eager to conclude the case, calls on the prosecution to lead evidence and cross-examines the witnesses himself to assist Chidi.
options: The trial is valid because the Judge was helping the defendant.
The trial is a nullity as the Judge descended into the arena of conflict.
The trial is valid because Chidi has no right to a lawyer if he cannot afford one.
The trial is valid as the Judge has power to put questions to witnesses under the Evidence Act.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: A Judge must remain an unbiased umpire and not enter the "arena of conflict" by taking over the prosecution or defense [6, 7]. S. 36(4) of the 1999 Constitution ensures fair hearing by an impartial tribunal [2].

quizzes.scenario: Amadi is caught in 2024 for an act committed in 2022. At the time of the act, it carried a maximum penalty of 2 years imprisonment. However, a new law passed in 2023 increased the penalty for the same act to 10 years. The court sentences Amadi to 10 years.
options: The sentence is valid as the law in force at the time of trial applies.
The sentence is invalid because Amadi cannot be punished under a retroactive law.
The sentence is valid if the Attorney General approves it.
The sentence is valid because it was a continuing offence.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: S. 36(8) of the 1999 Constitution prohibits the imposition of a penalty heavier than the one in force at the time the offence was committed [3, 8]. Trial upon retroactive legislation is unconstitutional [9].

quizzes.scenario: Segun is tried for robbery in a High Court. At the close of the prosecution's case, the judge suo motu determines there is no prima facie case and discharges Segun on a no-case submission. Two weeks later, Segun is re-arrested and charged with the same robbery.
options: Segun can be re-tried because he was only discharged, not acquitted.
Segun cannot be re-tried because the discharge on merit operates as an acquittal.
Segun can be re-tried if fresh evidence is found.
Segun can be re-tried if the Attorney General files a nolle prosequi.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Under S. 36(9) of the 1999 Constitution, a person who has been tried and either convicted or acquitted cannot be tried again for the same offence (autrefois acquit) [10]. A discharge on a no-case submission in the High Court is a discharge on the merits and equivalent to an acquittal [11, 12].

quizzes.scenario: Ibrahim is charged with "disrespecting a village elder" in a High Court in Enugu. While the act is considered immoral by the community, there is no written law in Enugu State defining "disrespect" as a crime or prescribing a penalty.
options: Ibrahim can be convicted because the court must uphold community morality.
Ibrahim cannot be convicted because the offence is not known to a written law.
Ibrahim can be convicted if he pleads guilty.
Ibrahim can be convicted under the inherent powers of the High Court.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: S. 36(12) of the 1999 Constitution provides that a person shall not be convicted of a criminal offence unless it is defined and the penalty is prescribed in a written law [3, 13]. Aoko v Fagbemi established that customary offences like adultery (in the South) are not crimes without statutory definition [13].

quizzes.scenario: During a trial for burglary, the defendant, Wale, elects not to testify or call any witnesses. In his final address, the prosecutor tells the court, "Wale's failure to enter the witness box is a clear admission that he is guilty as charged."
options: The statement is valid as silence implies guilt.
The statement is improper as it suggests Wale is guilty because he exercised his right to silence.
The statement is valid under the Evidence Act.
The statement is valid if Wale's lawyer does not object.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: S. 36(11) of the 1999 Constitution grants the right to silence and provides that no person shall be compelled to give evidence at his trial [3, 14]. While the prosecutor may comment on the failure to testify, S. 181 of the Evidence Act forbids suggesting that such failure constitutes guilt [15, 16].

quizzes.scenario: In a murder trial, the defense counsel is absent. The Judge informs the defendant, Emeka, that since he is not a lawyer, he must defend himself. Emeka is eventually convicted.
options: The trial is valid as Emeka has the right to defend himself in person.
The trial is a nullity because legal representation is mandatory in capital offences.
The trial is valid if Emeka cross-examined the witnesses.
The trial is valid because the court gave Emeka an opportunity to be heard.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: While S. 36(6)(c) allows a defendant to defend himself in person or by a legal practitioner of his choice, the state is duty-bound to provide counsel for defendants charged with capital offences [17, 18]. Josiah v State held that it is a lack of fair trial to allow a murder case to proceed without defense counsel [19].

quizzes.scenario: Tunde is charged with murder. After the prosecution leads evidence for manslaughter, the court convicts Tunde for manslaughter even though he was not specifically charged with it. Tunde appeals, citing a breach of his right to be informed of the nature of the offence.
options: The appeal will succeed as Tunde was not charged with manslaughter.
The appeal will fail because a defendant charged with a graver offence is deemed to have notice of lesser offences.
The appeal will succeed because manslaughter is not a lesser offence of murder.
The appeal will fail only if the Attorney General filed an amendment before judgment.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: S. 36(6)(a) requires an accused to be informed of the nature of his offence [3, 20]. However, an exception exists where a person is charged with a graver offence; he is deemed to have notice of lesser included offences (e.g., murder to manslaughter) [21, 22].

quizzes.scenario: A local Magistrate in Kano tries a man for "culpable homicide punishable with death." The man is convicted and sentenced to death. He later challenges the conviction on the basis of autrefois acquit when the state tries to re-arraign him in the High Court.
options: The plea of autrefois acquit will succeed because the Magistrate already convicted him.
The plea will fail because the Magistrate lacked jurisdiction to try a capital offence.
The plea will succeed if the Magistrate signed the judgment.
The plea will fail only if the High Court trial is for a different offence.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: For a plea of autrefois acquit or convict to succeed under S. 36(9) of the 1999 Constitution, the first trial must have been before a court of competent jurisdiction [10, 23]. Magistrate courts lack jurisdiction to try capital offences like murder or culpable homicide punishable with death [24, 25].

quizzes.scenario: Chief Bola is convicted of money laundering and sentenced to 5 years. Two months later, the President of Nigeria grants him a "full and unconditional pardon." The following year, a new government attempts to re-try him for the same facts.
options: The re-trial is valid if the pardon was politically motivated.
The re-trial is barred because a person cannot be tried for a pardoned offence.
The re-trial is valid if fresh evidence of corruption is presented.
The re-trial is valid because pardon only affects the sentence, not the conviction.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: S. 36(10) of the 1999 Constitution provides that no person who shows he has been pardoned for a criminal offence shall again be tried for that offence [3, 26]. A pardon "obliterates any stigma" and treats the beneficiary as if he never committed the offence [27].

quizzes.scenario: During a trial for rape, the judge orders that the public and press be excluded from the courtroom because the victim is a 15-year-old girl. The defendant argues this violates his right to a public trial.
options: The argument is correct as all criminal trials must be public.
The judge's order is valid as an exception to the rule of publicity to protect the welfare of a minor.
The order is valid only if the Minister of Information agrees.
The order is invalid because rape is a public offence.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: S. 36(4)(a) of the 1999 Constitution allows the court to exclude the public in the interest of public morality or the welfare of persons under 18 years of age [2, 28]. This is a recognized exception to the general requirement for trials to be held in public [29].

quizzes.scenario: Festus is charged with treason. The prosecution calls only one witness who testifies to an overt act of Festus attempting to overthrow the government. Festus makes a no-case submission based on a lack of corroboration.
options: The submission will fail because one material witness is generally sufficient.
The submission will succeed because treason requires at least two witnesses to an overt act.
The submission will fail if the witness is a high-ranking police officer.
The submission will succeed only if Festus also proves he was not at the scene.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Under S. 201 of the Evidence Act, a person charged with treason cannot be convicted except on his own plea of guilty or on the evidence of at least two witnesses to one overt act [30, 31]. This is an instance where corroboration is required as a matter of law [30].

quizzes.scenario: A judge delivers a judgment in a robbery case 120 days after the final addresses were adopted. The defendant, who was convicted, appeals solely on the ground that the judgment was delivered outside the 90-day constitutional limit.
options: The judgment is automatically a nullity for violating S. 294(1) of the Constitution.
The judgment is valid unless the defendant shows the delay occasioned a miscarriage of justice.
The judgment is valid because the 90-day rule only applies to civil cases.
The judgment is a nullity if the judge did not sign it.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: S. 294(1) of the 1999 Constitution requires judgment within 90 days of final addresses [32]. However, S. 294(5) provides that failure to comply does not render the judgment a nullity unless the appellant shows he suffered a miscarriage of justice due to the delay [33, 34].

quizzes.scenario: During a summary trial in a Magistrate court in Lagos, the Magistrate sentences a man to 20 years imprisonment for a non-capital offence. The relevant law creating the offence allows for such a term, but the Magistrate's Court Law limits sentencing power.
options: The sentence is valid as the Magistrate followed the substantive law.
The sentence is invalid because a Magistrate in Lagos cannot sentence a person to more than 14 years.
The sentence is valid if the defendant pleaded guilty.
The sentence is valid because it was a summary trial.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Under S. 29(5) of the Magistrate Court Law of Lagos, a Magistrate shall not sentence a person to a prison term of more than 14 years [35]. While they can try various offences, their sentencing power is strictly limited by statute [36].

quizzes.scenario: Tochukwu is charged with insanity as a primary offence by the police. He argues that he cannot be tried for "being insane" as it is not a crime.
options: The police are correct because insanity is dangerous to the public.
Tochukwu is correct because insanity is a defense, not a defined criminal offence.
The police are correct if they have a medical report.
Tochukwu is correct only if he has a lawyer.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: S. 36(12) of the 1999 Constitution requires that any act or omission for which a person is tried must be specifically regarded as a crime in a written law [37]. Insanity is a defense to a crime under the Criminal/Penal code, not a standalone offence [38].

quizzes.scenario: A defendant, Yusuf, is represented by a Senior Advocate of Nigeria (SAN) in a trial before an Area Court in the North. The prosecution objects, citing a law that prohibits SANs from appearing in inferior courts.
options: The objection will succeed as SANs are restricted from inferior courts.
The objection will fail because Yusuf has a constitutional right to counsel of his choice.
The objection will succeed because the Area Court procedure is simple and technicalities are avoided.
The objection will fail only if the SAN has a fiat from the Attorney General.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: S. 36(6)(c) of the 1999 Constitution grants the right to a legal practitioner of one's choice [17]. In Uzodinma v COP, the Supreme Court held that laws forbidding lawyers from representing accused persons in Area Courts are void for inconsistency with the Constitution [17].

quizzes.scenario: In a trial for theft, the prosecution calls PW1. After the examination-in-chief, the Judge tells the defendant, "I have heard enough from this witness. You are not allowed to ask him any questions." The defendant is later convicted.
options: The trial is valid as the Judge has discretion to stop cross-examination.
The trial is a nullity as the defendant's right to examine prosecution witnesses was breached.
The trial is valid if the defendant was guilty anyway.
The trial is valid because the Judge's questions replaced cross-examination.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: S. 36(6)(d) of the 1999 Constitution guarantees the right to examine witnesses called by the prosecution [39]. Tulu v Bauchi N.A. held that failing to allow a defendant to cross-examine each prosecution witness renders the trial a nullity [39, 40].

quizzes.scenario: Kalu is charged with a federal offence (Money Laundering) in the High Court of Abia State. He objects to the jurisdiction of the court, arguing that only the Federal High Court can try federal offences.
options: The objection will succeed because the Federal High Court has exclusive jurisdiction over federal offences.
The objection will fail because State High Courts have concurrent jurisdiction to try federal offences.
The objection will succeed unless the Abia High Court judge is a federal appointee.
The objection will fail only if the President of Nigeria gave permission.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Under S. 286(1)(b) of the 1999 Constitution, a State High Court has jurisdiction to try federal offences [41]. The Federal High Court and State High Courts generally share concurrent jurisdiction over criminal matters unless a statute specifically confers exclusive jurisdiction on the FHC [42].

quizzes.scenario: A Magistrate in Abuja hears a case for 6 months. On the day of judgment, the Magistrate is sick and cannot attend. The Registrar asks another Magistrate of the same court to read the written and signed judgment of the first Magistrate.
options: The judgment is a nullity because it must be read by the judge who wrote it.
The judgment is valid as ACJA allows another judge of the same court to deliver a signed judgment.
The judgment is valid only if the defendant consents.
The judgment is valid if the Attorney General is present.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: S. 313 of the ACJA provides that where a judge or magistrate is prevented by illness or other cause from delivering a judgment he has written and signed, it may be delivered in open court by another judge or magistrate of the same court [43, 44].

quizzes.scenario: Musa is charged with Culpable Homicide punishable with death in Kano. He has been in custody for 18 months without trial. He applies for bail at the State High Court.
options: Bail must be granted as of right
Musa is entitled to bail as he has been in custody for over a year
Musa is not entitled to bail because the delay must exceed two years in Kano
Bail is at the discretion of the Police
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: The rule applies that in Kano, extraordinary delay constituting a special circumstance for bail in capital offenses must exceed two years as provided under Section 171(3) ACJL Kano [1].

quizzes.scenario: Ade is charged with Murder in Lagos. He applies for bail at the Magistrate Court where he was first brought during a trial proceeding.
options: The Magistrate must grant bail
The Magistrate cannot grant bail for capital offenses during trial proceedings
The Magistrate can grant bail if Ade provides three sureties
The Magistrate has unlimited discretion
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: The rule under Section 115 ACJL Lagos and Section 161(1) ACJA is that only a High Court Judge can grant bail in capital offenses pending trial [2, 3].

quizzes.scenario: A legal practitioner, Mr. Bitrus, decides to sign the bail bond for his client who is charged with stealing in Abuja to ensure his quick release.
options: This is a commendable act of diligence
This is unethical and prohibited
This is only allowed for felonies
This is allowed if the client is indigent
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: The rule under Rule 37 of the Rules of Professional Conduct (RPC) prohibits a legal practitioner from standing as surety for a person for whom he is appearing [4, 5].

quizzes.scenario: Mrs. Okoro is refused as a surety by a Court Registrar in Lagos because the Registrar claims women cannot stand as sureties.
options: The Registrar is correct under the Criminal Code
The Registrar's action is unconstitutional and illegal
Women can only be sureties for other women
Only property owners can be sureties
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: The rule under Section 118(3) ACJL Lagos and Section 167(3) ACJA expressly prohibits discrimination against women standing as sureties, supported by Section 42 of the 1999 Constitution [6].

quizzes.scenario: A defendant is charged with a misdemeanor in Lagos. The Magistrate refuses bail because the defendant is from another state, despite no evidence of flight risk.
options: The Magistrate must grant bail as a matter of course unless there are reasons to the contrary
The Magistrate is correct to prevent flight
Misdemeanors are not bailable
Only the High Court can grant bail for misdemeanors
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: The rule under Section 115(3) ACJL Lagos and Section 163 ACJA is that for misdemeanors or simple offenses, the court shall grant bail as a matter of course unless there are compelling reasons to the contrary [7].

quizzes.scenario: A defendant jumps bail in Abuja. The court immediately orders the surety to pay the bond amount without allowing the surety to explain.
options: The court's order is valid to ensure compliance
The surety must be given a fair hearing before forfeiture
The surety is automatically liable for the crime
The surety must be imprisoned for 10 years
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: The rule is that before a bail bond executed by a surety is forfeited, the surety must be given a fair hearing to show cause as established in Amadutea v. COP [8].

quizzes.scenario: Mr. Kalu is denied bail by a Magistrate in Lagos. His lawyer wants to apply to a higher court to secure his release.
options: The lawyer must file an appeal to the Court of Appeal
The lawyer should file a fresh application by Summons at the High Court
The lawyer must wait for trial to end
The lawyer should file a Motion for Review at the Magistrate Court
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: The rule is that where a Magistrate refuses or neglects to grant bail, the procedure is to make a fresh application to a Judge of the High Court as provided under Section 119 ACJL Lagos [9, 10].

quizzes.scenario: In a High Court in Kano, a defendant applies for bail after it was refused by the Magistrate.
options: The application must be by Summons
The application should be by Motion on Notice
The application must be oral
No application is allowed
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: The rule is that in Northern Nigeria, English rules are excluded by Section 35 High Court Law of Northern Nigeria, thus bail applications are made by Motion on Notice [11, 12].

quizzes.scenario: A defendant is granted bail on the condition that he deposits N1,000,000 in cash with the court registrar instead of providing a surety.
options: This is illegal as bail is always free
The court can direct a deposit of money in lieu of a bond
Only the Police can take cash deposits
Cash deposits are only for capital offenses
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: The rule under Section 165(2) ACJA and Section 116(2) ACJL Lagos allows the court to direct the deposit of a sum of money in lieu of entering into a bond [13].

quizzes.scenario: A surety, fearing that the defendant is about to jump bail, brings the defendant to court and asks to be released from his obligations.
options: The court must refuse until trial ends
The court will discharge the surety and require the defendant to find a new one
The surety must pay half the bond
The surety will be arrested with the defendant
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: The rule under Section 177(1) ACJA and Section 129 ACJL Lagos allows a surety to apply for discharge, leading to the defendant's detention until a new surety is provided [8, 14].

quizzes.scenario: A defendant is charged with Murder in Abuja. He has been in custody for 14 months without trial and cites this as a special circumstance.
options: The delay must be at least 6 months
The delay must exceed one year
The delay must be 2 years
Delay is not a special circumstance
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: The rule under Section 161(2)(b) ACJA states that extraordinary delay in investigation or prosecution exceeding one year constitutes a special circumstance for bail in capital offenses [1].

quizzes.scenario: An applicant for bail on health grounds in a capital case presents a medical report from a local private clinic.
options: The court must accept the report
The report must be from a qualified medical practitioner in a government hospital
No medical report is needed for bail
Health is not a ground for bail in capital cases
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: The rule under Section 161(2)(a) ACJA requires that ill-health in capital cases be certified by a qualified medical practitioner employed in a government hospital [3, 15].

quizzes.scenario: A Judge in the Federal High Court hears a bail application moved orally by a Senior Advocate of Nigeria for a high-profile defendant.
options: This is the standard procedure in High Courts
This is allowed only as an exception with leave of court
Oral applications are strictly for Magistrates
The application must be by Summons only
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: The rule is that while High Court applications are usually in writing, oral applications can be moved with leave of court as an exception, as seen in Abiola v. FRN [16, 17].

quizzes.scenario: A Magistrate in Lagos is presiding over a remand proceeding for a suspect in an armed robbery case (a capital offense).
options: The Magistrate can never grant bail
The Magistrate can grant bail at the end of the remand process
Only the Police grant bail in remand
Remand doesn't allow for bail
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: The rule in remand jurisprudence is that a Magistrate can grant bail to a suspect of any offense, including capital offenses, pending investigation as established in Lufadeju v. Johnson [3, 18].

quizzes.scenario: A defendant is granted bail but is subsequently indicted for another serious offense while awaiting trial.
options: The bail is automatically extended
The Magistrate shall issue a warrant for his apprehension to reconsider bail
The defendant gets a warning
Revocation only happens after conviction
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: The rule under Section 127(1) ACJL Lagos is that if a defendant on bail is indicted for another offense, the Magistrate may issue a warrant for his apprehension to revoke or reconsider the bail [19].

quizzes.scenario: A court grants bail but sets conditions that the defendant's lawyer argues are excessive given the defendant's status.
options: He must apply to the same Judge for a review
He can apply to a higher court for the review of the conditions
Conditions of bail are final
He must file a fresh charge
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: The rule is that where bail terms are excessive, an application for review of the conditions can be made to a higher court under Section 178 ACJL Kano and Section 165(1) ACJA [20].

quizzes.scenario: A defendant's bail is revoked during trial because his only surety died and no replacement was found.
options: The defendant is acquitted
The defendant must be re-arrested until a new surety is provided
The surety's child takes over automatically
The trial is stayed indefinitely
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: The rule applies that upon the death of a surety, his estate is discharged, and the defendant must be re-arrested unless and until he provides another surety [21].

quizzes.scenario: A Judge refuses bail for a felony offense solely because the Prosecution raised a general objection without evidence of a flight risk.
options: The Judge's refusal is proper
The Judge must exercise discretion based on specific factors like flight risk
Refusal is mandatory if the state objects
Discretion is only for capital offenses
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: The rule under Section 162 ACJA is that bail in felonies is discretionary and must be exercised by considering specific factors, such as whether the defendant will appear for trial [1, 7, 22].

quizzes.scenario: A professional bondsperson in Lagos arrests a defendant without a warrant after discovering he is attempting to flee the state.
options: This is an illegal arrest
The bondsperson is empowered to arrest a defendant attempting to escape without a warrant
Only the Police can arrest
The bondsperson must call the court first
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: The rule under Section 138(8) ACJL Lagos and Section 188 ACJA empowers a registered bondsperson to arrest a defendant attempting to escape without a warrant [6].

quizzes.scenario: A defendant is arraigned in court after previously being on Police bail. His lawyer claims the Police bail still subsists.
options: The Police bail continues until judgment
Police bail elapses immediately the suspect is arraigned in court
The court must adopt the Police bail
Arraignment doesn't affect Police bail
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: The rule applies that Police bail is temporary and elapses immediately the suspect is arraigned in court, necessitating a new application for court bail pending trial [23-25].

quizzes.scenario: A, B, and C jointly rob a bank. During the getaway, B shoots a bystander to facilitate escape. The prosecutor wants to charge A, B, and C in the same charge sheet for robbery and B for murder.
options: Only A can be charged
A, B, and C can be charged for robbery, but only B can be charged for murder in the same charge sheet
All three must be charged for everything
Separate charge sheets must be used for each
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Under S. 151 ACJL Lagos and S. 208 ACJA, persons committing different offences in the course of the same transaction (proximity, continuity, community of purpose) can be joined in one charge sheet in separate counts as per Haruna v. State [1, 2].

quizzes.scenario: Five men are involved in a street fight. Two are accused of assault, while the other three are accused of abetting the assault. The prosecutor joins all five in one chargesheet.
options: This is an illegal misjoinder
The joinder is valid under the fight/abetment exception
Only the principals can be joined
Only the abettors can be joined together
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: S. 215(f) ACJA allows persons accused of offences committed during a fight and those accused of abetting them to be charged and tried together in the same charge sheet [13].

quizzes.scenario: Segun steals a gold necklace. Later that evening, he sells it to Kola, who knows it was stolen. The prosecutor charges both Segun for stealing and Kola for receiving in one chargesheet.
options: This is a misjoinder of offences
This is a misjoinder of offenders
The joinder is valid under the theft and receiving exception
Segun and Kola must be tried separately
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: S. 151(b) ACJL Lagos and S. 208(e) ACJA provide an exception to the rule against misjoinder of offenders where one person steals and another receives the subject matter [19].

quizzes.scenario: In Lagos, Musa stole a phone in January, a laptop in May, and a car in November, all from different owners. The prosecutor wants to try Musa for all three in one charge sheet.
options: It is a misjoinder as victims are different
Musa can only be tried for two
The joinder is valid under the 12-month rule
Musa must be tried for each separately
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: By virtue of S. 153(i) ACJL Lagos and S. 209(a) ACJA, any three offences committed within 12 months by one defendant may be charged in the same charge sheet even if against different people [3].

quizzes.scenario: An accused commits five different simple offences within a 12-month period. The prosecutor joins all five in one charge sheet.
options: Valid under the 12-month rule
Bad for misjoinder; only three can be selected
Valid because they are simple offences
Only capital offences can be joined
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: S. 153(i) ACJL Lagos and S. 209(a) ACJA limit the joinder of offences committed within 12 months to a maximum of three [3, 21].

quizzes.scenario: A cashier at a hotel misappropriates various sums of money belonging to his employer totaling N1 million over a 4-month period. The prosecutor drafts a single count for the total sum.
options: The count is bad for duplicity
The count is valid under the general deficiency exception
The cashier must be charged for each daily theft separately
The count is valid only if the sums were taken on the same day
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: S. 148(2) ACJL Lagos and S. 197 ACJA allow for the aggregation of money stolen from the same person over a period of time in a single count to avoid duplicity [4, 5].

quizzes.scenario: In a single count, Emeka is charged with stealing a motorcycle and an iPhone belonging to two different neighbors on different dates.
options: The count is valid under the 12-month rule
The count is bad for duplicity
The count is valid because both items are personal property
The count is an Information and thus immune to duplicity
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Every distinct offence must be in a separate count under S. 152 ACJL Lagos; the general deficiency exception only applies to money belonging to one person, not disparate property from different victims [14, 15].

quizzes.scenario: In a Magistrate Court in Kaduna, a police officer brings a First Information Report (FIR) against Yakubu for theft. After hearing evidence of a prima facie case, a charge is drafted. Who is the proper authority to frame and sign this charge?
options: The Police Officer
The Registrar
The Magistrate
The Attorney General
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: In Magistrate Courts in Northern Nigeria, the Magistrate performs the administrative duty of framing and signing the charge after being satisfied a prima facie case exists, pursuant to S. 129(10) ACJL Kano [6, 7].

quizzes.scenario: A defendant is charged under "Section 390 of the Criminal Code" for stealing, but the charge fails to state the specific subsection or the specific thing stolen.
options: The charge is trivial
The charge is bad for ambiguity as it fails to give adequate notice
The charge is valid since stealing is mentioned
The court can amend it after judgment
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: The rule against ambiguity requires the charge to reflect N-ADPOPS elements, including the thing stolen, to ensure the defendant is informed of the nature of the allegation under S. 36(6)(a) CFRN and Odeh v. State [8, 9].

quizzes.scenario: During the trial of Ade in a Lagos High Court, the prosecutor realizes PW2's testimony implicates Ade in Arson, which was not originally charged. The prosecutor seeks to amend the Information after Ade has already pleaded not guilty.
options: No amendment is possible after plea
The prosecutor needs the leave of the court
The prosecutor can amend orally without leave
The Magistrate must be consulted
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: If an amendment is sought after a plea or commencement of trial, the prosecutor must obtain the leave of court as provided in S. 155 ACJL Lagos and S. 216(1) ACJA [10, 11].

quizzes.scenario: An Information in an Abuja High Court is amended to substitute a charge of Stealing with Armed Robbery. The court allows the amendment but immediately calls the prosecution to continue evidence.
options: The procedure is correct
The court must ask the defendant to take a fresh plea and inform him of his rights
Only the counsel needs to be notified
The trial must start de novo before another judge
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: S. 156 ACJL Lagos and S. 218 ACJA mandate that after an amendment, the charge must be read and explained to the defendant, and he must take a fresh plea [12].

quizzes.scenario: A State Counsel in the Ministry of Justice signs an Information as "For: The Attorney General of Oyo State". The defense objects that the AG must sign personally.
options: The objection is valid
The objection is invalid as the AG's power is delegable to law officers
Only the Solicitor General can sign for the AG
The Police must sign all Informations
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: The Attorney-General may delegate his prosecutorial powers to officers in his department, and a law officer can sign "for the AG" as held in Ibrahim v. State and S. 211(2) CFRN [16, 17].

quizzes.scenario: An Attorney-General decides to charge only two out of five suspects who participated in a gang robbery, using the other three as witnesses. The defense objects to this selective prosecution.
options: The objection is valid as all must be charged
The objection is invalid; the AG has unfettered discretion to decide whom to prosecute
Selective prosecution is a ground for acquittal
Only a Judge can decide who to charge
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Under S. 174/211 CFRN and Akpa v. State, the Attorney General has absolute discretion to decide whom to prosecute and this power cannot be questioned [27, 28].

quizzes.scenario: In a Federal High Court trial, the defense counsel notices after the defendant has pleaded not guilty that the charge is duplicitous. He raises an objection immediately.
options: The court must rule on it immediately
The court shall record the objection but give the ruling during judgment
The objection is waived because it wasn't raised before plea
The court must strike out the charge immediately
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Under S. 396(2) ACJA and S. 390(2) ACJL Kano, objections to a defective charge can be taken after the plea, but the court will not deliver its ruling until judgment [18].

quizzes.scenario: Tunde is charged with burglary and stealing in a single count. The defense argues that these are two separate offences and the count is duplicitous.
options: The defense is correct
The count is valid because it follows the statutory form in the Schedule
Burglary and stealing must always be in separate counts
Only the Magistrate Court can join these
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Use of statutory forms to charge housebreaking/burglary and stealing in one count is a valid exception to the rule against duplicity as held in Willie John v. State [20].

quizzes.scenario: A Magistrate in the North frames a charge. He includes an introduction and the body of the charge but omits the "Directional" paragraph.
options: The charge is perfect
The charge is incomplete as it lacks the directional paragraph
Only High Courts use directional paragraphs
The directional is only for police officers
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: A charge in the Magistrate Court of the North has three paragraphs: Introduction, Body, and the Directional, where the Magistrate directs who shall try the case [22].

quizzes.scenario: A charge refers to the "Armed Robbery Act 1980" instead of the "Robbery and Firearms (Special Provisions) Act." The defendant is not misled by this and knows he is accused of robbery with a gun.
options: The conviction will be set aside
The error is not fundamental as the defendant was not misled
The prosecutor must be reported for ethics violations
The trial is a nullity
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Minor errors in a charge do not invalidate a conviction unless they are material AND the defendant was actually misled, as held in Ogbomor v. State [23, 24].

quizzes.scenario: A trial is commenced in the High Court of Lagos. The document contains a Preamble, Statement of Offence, and Particulars of Offence. What is this document called?
options: A Charge
An Information
A Complaint
A First Information Report
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: In the High Court of the South and Lagos, criminal proceedings are typically commenced by Information, which includes a Preamble and two paragraphs (Statement and Particulars) per count [6, 25].

quizzes.scenario: A and B conspired to steal. During the robbery, A raped a victim without B's knowledge. They are joined in one charge sheet. B objects that he cannot be tried with A for rape.
options: The joinder is bad for everything
They can be in the same charge sheet for stealing (same count) and rape (separate count)
B must be charged for rape as a conspirator
Separate charge sheets are mandatory
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: S. 151 ACJL Lagos and S. 208 ACJA allow joinder in one charge sheet for different offences in the same transaction, provided they are in separate counts [1, 2].

quizzes.scenario: After an amendment to an Information, the court fails to ask the defendant if he is ready to proceed with the trial on the new charge.
options: The trial is still valid
The omission is a trivial error
Failure to comply with post-amendment procedures renders the trial a nullity
Readiness is only for the prosecutor
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Failure to comply with statutory requirements for post-amendments, such as asking if the defendant is ready, renders the trial null and void as held in Ayodele v. State [26].

quizzes.scenario: Justice Adebayo delivered a judgment orally in a High Court proceeding and promised to file the written reasons in the court's registry the following day.
options: The judgment is valid since it was delivered in open court
The judgment is voidable at the instance of the Attorney General
The judgment is a nullity as it must be in writing
The judgment is valid as long as the defendant was present
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Pursuant to Section 294(1) of the 1999 Constitution, Section 308(1) of the ACJA, and Section 275 of the ACJL Lagos, every judgment of a court of record must be in writing. A judge cannot deliver judgment orally and file reasons later [1-3].

quizzes.scenario: A trial judge in Lagos High Court read a typed judgment in open court but forgot to sign the manuscript before the proceeding ended.
options: The judgment is valid because it was read in open court
The judgment is worthless and a nullity for lack of signature
The judgment is valid if the court registrar signed it
The judge can sign it anytime within 90 days
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: A judgment must be dated and signed or sealed by the judge at the time of pronouncing it; an unsigned judgment is worthless and null [3-6].

quizzes.scenario: A judgment in a robbery trial was delivered 105 days after the conclusion of final addresses. The appellant argued the judgment is a nullity solely because of the 90-day rule.
options: The judgment is automatically void
The judgment is a nullity only if the appellant proves a miscarriage of justice resulted from the delay
The judgment is valid as the 90-day rule is merely advisory
The judge is liable for contempt but the judgment stands
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Under Section 294(5) of the 1999 Constitution, failure to deliver judgment within 90 days does not render it void unless the person complaining shows they suffered a miscarriage of justice [7, 8].

quizzes.scenario: Musa was charged with Murder. At the end of the trial, the judge found that the evidence only supported Manslaughter.
options: The judge must discharge Musa as he was not charged with Manslaughter
The judge can convict for Manslaughter as it is a lesser offence supported by the evidence of the main charge
The judge must order a fresh trial for Manslaughter
Musa must take a fresh plea to Manslaughter before conviction
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Under Sections 236(2) of the ACJA and 169 of the ACJL Lagos, a court can convict for a lesser offence not expressly charged if it is supported by the evidence and the ingredients are embedded in the main offence [9, 10].

quizzes.scenario: After finding Emeka guilty of stealing, the Magistrate immediately sentenced him to 3 years imprisonment without asking if he had anything to say.
options: The sentence is a nullity for lack of fair hearing
The proceeding is valid as allocutus is not a mandatory requirement
The sentence is valid but the Magistrate should have asked for a plea in mitigation
The Magistrate is functus officio and cannot be corrected
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: While it is a matter of practice for the registrar or judge to ask for an allocutus (plea in mitigation) after conviction but before sentence, failure to do so does not affect the validity of the sentence [11, 12].

quizzes.scenario: A Judge in Abuja sentenced a convict to death by stating: "You are hereby sentenced to death." The convict's lawyer argued the sentence is void for not following the prescribed form.
options: The sentence is a nullity
The sentence is valid as the form of words is not strictly mandatory to ensure validity
The Judge must be arrested for professional misconduct
The sentence must be changed to life imprisonment
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Although desirable, failure to strictly comply with the prescribed form of words in pronouncing a death sentence is a mere clerical error that does not invalidate the sentence [13, 14].

quizzes.scenario: Zainab was convicted of Culpable Homicide punishable with death in a Kano High Court. Medical examination confirmed she is 4 months pregnant.
options: Zainab must be executed immediately
Zainab shall be sentenced to life imprisonment in lieu of death
The death sentence shall be passed but execution suspended until delivery and a 2-year weaning period
Zainab must be pardoned by the Governor
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Under Section 408(4) of the ACJL Kano, when a woman is pregnant, the court shall sentence her to death but execution is suspended until delivery and a weaning period of two years [15, 16].

quizzes.scenario: Segun was 17 years old when he committed armed robbery but was 19 years old when the trial concluded and he was convicted.
options: Segun can be sentenced to death as he is now an adult
Segun cannot be sentenced to death because he was a young person at the time of the offence
Segun must be sentenced to 10 years imprisonment only
The Governor must decide Segun's fate before sentencing
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: A person who has not attained 18 years at the time of committing the offence cannot be sentenced to death; the relevant time is the time of commission, not conviction [17, 18].

quizzes.scenario: A Magistrate in Ikeja sentenced a convict to 5 years imprisonment on Count 1 and 3 years on Count 2, but remained silent on whether they should run concurrently or consecutively.
options: The sentences run concurrently
The sentences run consecutively
The Magistrate must be recalled to clarify
The sentence is void for ambiguity
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Where a court pronounces more than one sentence and is silent on the mode, the sentences shall run consecutively [19, 20].

quizzes.scenario: A statute provides that the punishment for an offence is "a term of imprisonment not exceeding 10 years." The judge sentenced the convict to 12 years.
options: The sentence is valid due to the gravity of the offence
The sentence is valid but the convict must serve only 10 years
The court has no discretion to go above the maximum penalty prescribed by law
The entire trial is a nullity
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: A court has no discretion to impose a sentence higher than the maximum penalty prescribed by the law creating the offence [21, 22].

quizzes.scenario: In a Sharia Court in Kano, a Muslim male was convicted of defamation (Qadhf). The court sentenced him to Haddi lashing.
options: The sentence is illegal as Haddi lashing is for murder only
The sentence is valid as defamation is one of the specific offences attracting Haddi lashing for Muslims in the North
Haddi lashing can only be applied to non-Muslims who consent
The sentence is void because it is inhuman
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Haddi lashing is a specific punishment in the North for Muslims convicted of offences like adultery, injurious falsehood, and defamation [23, 24].

quizzes.scenario: A 50-year-old male was convicted of theft in Kano and sentenced to 10 strokes of the cane.
options: The sentence is valid because theft is a serious offence
The sentence is illegal because caning cannot be inflicted on males over 45 years of age
The sentence is valid if a medical doctor is present
Caning is only for female convicts
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Under Section 431(3) of the ACJL Kano, caning shall not be inflicted on females, males sentenced to death, or males over 45 years of age [25].

quizzes.scenario: A Judge sentenced a convict to a term of imprisonment without an option of fine, even though the statute allowed for "imprisonment or fine."
options: The sentence is illegal because an option of fine is mandatory
The sentence is valid as sentencing is within the court's discretion unless a minimum is fixed
The convict must be allowed to pay a fine if he can afford it
The judge must provide written reasons for refusing a fine
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Unless a statute provides a mandatory minimum or excludes a fine, the judge has discretion in sentencing within the statutory maximum [22, 26].

quizzes.scenario: A convict has three other pending charges of similar nature. The prosecutor wants the judge to take them into consideration during sentencing for the current conviction.
options: The judge can do so if the convict admits the charges and the prosecutor of those charges consents
The judge can do so only if the victims of those charges agree
This procedure is unknown to Nigerian law
The judge can only do so if the convict has already been tried for those charges
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Under Sections 313 of the ACJA and 279 of the ACJL Lagos, a court may take other pending charges into consideration if the defendant admits them and the prosecutor of those charges consents [27].

quizzes.scenario: A convict sentenced to 5 years imprisonment wants the period he spent in pre-trial detention (2 years) to be deducted from his sentence.
options: The judge has no power to deduct pre-trial detention time
In Lagos and under ACJA, the court must take the period of detention into account when sentencing
Deduction is only for capital offences
Only the Governor can deduct time spent in detention
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Sections 315 of the ACJL Lagos and 419/431 of the ACJA require the court to take the period of pre-trial detention into account when imposing a term of imprisonment [28].

quizzes.scenario: A Magistrate in Lagos wants to sentence a convict to 15 years imprisonment for multiple offences.
options: The Magistrate can do so if the sentences run concurrently
The Magistrate cannot exceed a total of 14 years imprisonment in any circumstance
The Magistrate must refer the case to the High Court for sentencing
The Magistrate's jurisdiction is unlimited for non-capital offences
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Under Section 29(5) of the Magistrate Court Law of Lagos and Section 314 of the ACJL, a Magistrate shall not sentence a person to a prison term exceeding 14 years in aggregate [29-31].

quizzes.scenario: A Judge delivered judgment and later realized he omitted the convict's right of appeal. He reopened the case the next day to add this.
options: The amendment is valid as it protects the convict's rights
The judge is functus officio and cannot amend the judgment except for clerical errors or accidental slips
The judge must start the trial de novo
The amendment is only valid if the prosecutor agrees
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Once a judgment is pronounced, the judge is functus officio and can only amend it to correct clerical errors or accidental slips under Section 318 of the ACJA [32].

quizzes.scenario: A court found a defendant not guilty of robbery but refused to release him until the complainant paid for the "lost" property.
options: This is valid under restorative justice
Any delay in release after a finding of not guilty amounts to unlawful detention
The defendant must stay in custody until the police clear him
The judge can order the defendant to remain in custody for his own protection
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Upon a finding of not guilty and delivery of the verdict, the defendant must be released immediately; any delay is unlawful [33, 34].

quizzes.scenario: An accused was charged with Attempted Rape. The evidence led at trial proved that he actually committed the full offence of Rape.
options: The court must convict him for Rape
The court must convict him for Attempted Rape as charged
The court must discharge him for a wrong charge
The court must order a fresh trial for Rape
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: An accused charged with an attempt can be convicted of that attempt even if the full offence is proved, but an accused charged with the full offence can be convicted of an attempt [35].

quizzes.scenario: A Judge in the High Court of Lagos sentenced a convict to 2 years imprisonment with an order for "Community Service" to be performed simultaneously.
options: The order is valid as it is reformatory
A convict sentenced to community service shall not also be sentenced to a term of imprisonment; it is an alternative
Community service is only for young persons
The order is valid if the convict consents
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Under Section 461(7) of the ACJA, community service is an alternative to imprisonment, and a convict cannot be sentenced to both for the same offence [36].

quizzes.scenario: During the trial of Segun for robbery, the prosecution calls PW1. Segun's wife, who is also a witness for the defense, remains in the gallery during PW1's testimony. The prosecution objects to her evidence being taken later.
options: Her evidence is inadmissible
The trial is a nullity
Her evidence is admissible but less weight will be attached
She must be arrested for contempt
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: According to the rule in Falaju v. Amosu and Uwaexoke v. Commissioner of Police, if a witness remains in court despite an order for witnesses to be out of court and hearing, the testimony is not expunged but less weight is attached to it [1, 2].

quizzes.scenario: In a murder trial, the prosecution calls Dr. Emeka. To save time, the counsel asks: "You are the Chief Pathologist at the National Hospital, Abuja, and you conducted the autopsy on January 10th, 2025, correct?"
options: The question is allowed as an introductory matter
The question is objectionable as a leading question on a material disputed fact
The question is allowed as a professional courtesy
The question is objectionable because only the Registrar can ask this
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Under Section 221(1) of the Evidence Act, leading questions are generally prohibited in examination-in-chief. While Section 221(3) allows them for introductory matters, the qualification of an expert is material and must be established by the witness himself to lay foundation for opinion evidence [3, 4].

quizzes.scenario: During examination-in-chief, PW2, who previously gave a statement to the police identifying the accused, now claims he never saw the accused at the scene. The prosecution counsel is surprised.
options: The counsel should immediately cross-examine the witness
The counsel should ask for an adjournment to "coach" the witness
The counsel should apply to the court to declare the witness hostile
The witness should be charged with perjury immediately
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Under Section 230 of the Evidence Act and the rule in Esan v. State, where a witness is biased against the party calling him or is unwilling to tell the truth, the party may apply to the court to declare him a hostile witness [5, 6].

quizzes.scenario: In a trial for stealing, the defense counsel is cross-examining PW1. He asks questions about a previous business deal between the witness and the accused that was never mentioned during the examination-in-chief. The prosecution objects.
options: The objection should be sustained as cross-examination is limited to the scope of examination-in-chief
The objection should be overruled as cross-examination is not limited to facts elicited in chief
The witness is not obliged to answer
The defense must seek leave of court to ask new questions
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Section 215(2) of the Evidence Act provides that cross-examination need not be confined to the facts to which the witness testified on in his examination-in-chief [7, 8].

quizzes.scenario: Following a rigorous cross-examination where PW3 was discredited regarding the time of the incident, the prosecution counsel in re-examination tries to ask PW3 about the weather conditions to explain the discrepancy.
options: This is allowed to clear ambiguities
This is prohibited as it is a new matter
This is allowed only if the defense consents
This is allowed as of right
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Section 214(3) of the Evidence Act stipulates that re-examination shall be directed to the explanation of matters referred to in cross-examination [9, 10].

quizzes.scenario: An IPO testifying in a kidnapping case wishes to refer to his investigation diary to remember the exact coordinates of the arrest made six months ago.
options: He can read the diary verbatim to the court
He must seek leave of court to refresh his memory
He cannot use the diary as it is a police report
He only needs the prosecution's permission
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Under Section 239(1) of the Evidence Act and the rule in Jimo Amoo v. The State, a witness must seek and obtain leave of court before referring to any writing to refresh his memory [11, 12].

quizzes.scenario: After the IPO refreshes his memory using his diary with the court's permission, the defense counsel demands to see the diary to prepare for cross-examination. The IPO refuses, citing official secrecy.
options: The defense has no right to see the document
The IPO is correct under the Police Act
The defense is entitled to be shown the writing and may cross-examine on it
Only the Judge is allowed to see the diary
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Section 241 of the Evidence Act provides that any writing used to refresh memory must be shown to the adverse party, who may cross-examine the witness upon it [13].

quizzes.scenario: At the close of the defense case for embezzlement, the accused for the first time produces a "receipt" signed by the complainant which the prosecution could not have anticipated. The prosecution seeks to call a witness to prove the signature is forged.
options: This is allowed under the ex-improviso rule
This is prohibited as the prosecution has closed its case
This is allowed only as additional evidence on appeal
The prosecution must file a new charge
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Under Section 241 ACJL Lagos and Section 361 ACJA, the prosecution may, with leave, adduce evidence in rebuttal of new matter raised by the defense which the prosecution could not have reasonably foreseen (ex-improviso rule) [14, 15].

quizzes.scenario: A bank manager is summoned via a subpoena duces tecum to produce the accused's bank statements. The defense wants to cross-examine him on the bank's internal policies.
options: He must answer all questions on oath
He cannot be cross-examined as he was only summoned to produce a document
He can only be cross-examined if he gives oral evidence
Both B and C are correct
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Under Section 219 of the Evidence Act and the rule in Olaniyan v. Oyewole, a person summoned merely to produce a document does not become a witness and cannot be cross-examined unless and until he is called as a witness [16, 17].

quizzes.scenario: In a trial for medical negligence, a specialist doctor called as an expert witness for the defense remains in court while the victim (PW1) describes his symptoms. The prosecution objects.
options: The expert must leave the court
The objection should be overruled as experts are an exception to the rule
The expert can stay but cannot testify later
The expert must be fined
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: The rule requiring witnesses to be out of court and hearing does not apply to expert witnesses because their testimony is based on professional knowledge rather than overheard facts [18, 19].

quizzes.scenario: During cross-examination of a prosecution witness, the defense counsel asks: "Isn't it true that you were fired from your last job for lying?" The prosecution objects to it being a leading question.
options: The objection is sustained
The objection is overruled; leading questions are allowed in cross-examination
The counsel must be reported to the NBA
The witness must answer "yes" or "no"
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Section 221(4) of the Evidence Act expressly provides that leading questions may be asked in cross-examination [8, 20].

quizzes.scenario: The prosecution calls 9-year-old Tobi to testify. Before Tobi is sworn, the Judge asks him several questions to see if he understands the difference between a truth and a lie.
options: This is a preliminary test for intelligence and duty of truth
This is an unlawful interrogation
The Judge is descending into the arena
Tobi must be 14 to testify
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Under Section 209(1) of the Evidence Act and the rule in Okon v. The State, the court must be satisfied that a child under 14 possesses sufficient intelligence and understands the duty of speaking the truth before they can testify [21, 22].

quizzes.scenario: After both parties have closed their cases and adopted final addresses, the Judge realizes he needs clarification on whether the weapon was recovered from the accused's house or car. He recalls the IPO to clarify.
options: This is a breach of fair hearing
This is allowed for the just determination of the case
The Judge must seek the AG's permission
A Judge cannot recall a witness after final addresses
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Section 246(1) of the Evidence Act and Section 256 ACJA empower the court to recall any person who has been examined if his evidence appears essential to the just determination of the case [23, 24].

quizzes.scenario: In re-examination, the prosecution counsel asks: "Was the accused wearing a mask when you saw him?" The defense objects because this was never mentioned in the previous examinations.
options: The objection is overruled
The objection is sustained; new matters cannot be raised in re-examination without leave
The witness should be declared hostile
The question is allowed to clear ambiguity
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Under Section 215(3) of the Evidence Act, re-examination is limited to clearing ambiguities from cross-examination, and new matters shall not be introduced without leave of court [9, 10].

quizzes.scenario: A prosecution witness tells the court the car was "Red," but his statement to the police says it was "Blue." The defense points this out during cross-examination.
options: The court must believe the police statement
The witness is automatically guilty of perjury
The testimony in court is regarded as unreliable under the inconsistency rule
The prosecution must amend the charge
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Under the rule in Dogo v. State, where a witness's testimony in court is inconsistent with a previous extra-judicial statement, the evidence at trial is generally regarded as unreliable [25, 26].

quizzes.scenario: In a trial for assault, the prosecution calls only one witness, the victim. The defense argues that the prosecution has failed to prove its case as there is no corroboration.
options: Assault requires at least two witnesses
Conviction can be based on a single material witness
The victim is an accomplice and needs corroboration
The trial is a nullity
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Section 200 of the Evidence Act provides that no particular number of witnesses shall in any case be required for the proof of any fact, provided the evidence is sufficient [27-29].

quizzes.scenario: A witness for the prosecution gives evidence that actually helps the defense. The prosecution counsel immediately starts asking him leading questions to show he is a liar without saying anything else.
options: This is allowed as the witness is hostile
This is improper; a party must first apply to the court to declare a witness hostile
The Judge should take over the examination
The witness must be arrested
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Under Section 230 of the Evidence Act and the rule in Babatunde v. State, a party calling a witness cannot discredit him until the court has formed an opinion that the witness is hostile and allows cross-examination [5, 6].

quizzes.scenario: The defense calls a witness to give an opinion on a forged signature but fails to ask about the witness's training in handwriting analysis.
options: The evidence is admissible as of right
The evidence is inadmissible for lack of foundation on expertise
Handwriting analysis is not expert evidence
The Judge will assume he is an expert
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Under Section 68 of the Evidence Act and the rule in Azu v. State, proper foundation as to the qualifications and experience of an expert must be laid before their opinion evidence is admissible [30].

quizzes.scenario: A witness who is unable to speak is called to testify. He writes his answers on a chalkboard in open court.
options: This is hearsay evidence
This is inadmissible as he cannot be sworn
This is deemed to be oral evidence
He must use an interpreter
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Section 176(1) and (2) of the Evidence Act provides that a witness who is unable to speak may give his evidence in any manner in which he can make it intelligible, such as by writing, and it shall be deemed oral evidence [31].

quizzes.scenario: During the trial of Ade for theft, the prosecution counsel finds a Supreme Court decision that clearly shows Ade's actions do not constitute theft under the current law. The counsel decides not to mention it.
options: This is good advocacy
This is a breach of the duty to be fair and impartial
The counsel is only responsible for his own case
This is a mandatory ground for Ade's release
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: According to the rule in Anani v. R and Rule 37(4) of the RPC, a prosecuting counsel is a representative of justice and must not withhold the existence of any adverse decision favourable to the defendant [32, 33].

quizzes.scenario: Prosecution closes its case against Chidi for robbery but fails to produce any witness or evidence linking Chidi to the firearm recovered. Chidi's lawyer applies for a discharge at that stage.
options: The court must convict based on the recovered firearm
The court should uphold the no case submission
The court should ask Chidi to prove his innocence
The court should call more witnesses itself
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: A no case submission succeeds where there is no legally admissible evidence linking the accused with the essential elements of the offence. Section 302 ACJA [1, 2].

quizzes.scenario: During the trial of Segun for theft, the sole prosecution witness admitted during cross-examination that he was intoxicated during the incident and cannot identify the perpetrator.
options: Segun must enter a defence to clear his name
The court should adjourn for further investigation
The court should uphold the no case submission
