The court should convict Segun based on the charge
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: A no case submission is proper when the prosecution evidence has been so discredited in cross-examination that no reasonable tribunal could safely convict on it. Ibeziako v. COP [1].

quizzes.scenario: After a no case submission is made, the trial judge delivers a 40-page ruling evaluating the credibility of all prosecution witnesses before overruling the application.
options: The ruling is a valid exercise of judicial duty
The ruling is improper as it fetters the court's discretion by evaluating credibility prematurely
The ruling is a mandatory administrative requirement
The ruling is only appealable with leave
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: A ruling on a no case submission should be brief and must not evaluate witness credibility or fetter the court's discretion before the defence is heard. Ekanem v. R [3].

quizzes.scenario: A High Court rightly overrules John's no case submission. John decides to testify and accidentally provides evidence that proves his guilt. He later appeals the conviction.
options: The conviction will be set aside because he should have been discharged earlier
The conviction will be upheld because the subsequent evidence is admissible
John is entitled to a retrial
The evidence provided by John is inadmissible against him
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: When a no case submission is rightly overruled, any incriminating evidence provided by the defendant during his defence is admissible to support a conviction. Ohuka v. State [4, 5].

quizzes.scenario: A Magistrate wrongly overrules Mary's no case submission. Mary enters her defence, testifies, and provides the missing evidence needed for a conviction.
options: The conviction will be upheld on appeal
The conviction will be set aside because the submission was wrongly overruled
Mary is guilty by her own admission
Mary is entitled to a pardon
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: If a no case submission is wrongly overruled, a conviction founded on evidence supplied by the defendant thereafter cannot stand on appeal. Mumuni v. State [6, 7].

quizzes.scenario: After the prosecution closes its case, Musa's lawyer declares "the defence rests its case on that of the prosecution." Two days later, Musa finds a witness and asks to reopen his case.
options: Musa can call the witness with leave of court
Musa cannot call any witness once he has rested his case
The court must allow it to ensure fair hearing
The witness will be treated as a court witness
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Resting a case on the prosecution means the defence adduces no evidence; the defendant cannot later seek to call witnesses. Sulaiman v. State [8].

quizzes.scenario: The prosecution establishes a strong prima facie case for burglary. The defence counsel, believing the prosecution's witnesses are lying, rests his case on that of the prosecution without calling witnesses.
options: The action is a prudent legal strategy
The action is reckless and likely to result in a conviction
The court must discharge the defendant regardless
The defendant is protected by the right to silence
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: It is considered reckless to rest a case on the prosecution when a prima facie case has been established that calls for the accused's explanation. Babalola v. State [9].

quizzes.scenario: Tunde is unrepresented at his trial. He elects to make a statement from the dock rather than the witness box. The prosecutor moves to cross-examine him on his statement.
options: The prosecutor can cross-examine with leave of court
The prosecutor cannot cross-examine a defendant making a statement from the dock
Tunde must move to the witness box to finish his statement
Dock statements are prohibited under ACJA
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: A statement from the dock is not on oath, and the defendant is not liable to cross-examination, though it carries less weight. Section 240(1)(a) ACJL [10].

quizzes.scenario: Amina, who is unrepresented, is told by the judge she can testify from the witness box on oath. She does so, and her testimony is torn apart during cross-examination.
options: Her testimony is inadmissible
Sworn evidence from the witness box is liable to cross-examination and carries more weight than a dock statement
Amina should have remained in the dock
The judge was wrong to offer the witness box
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Sworn evidence given in the witness box makes the defendant a witness liable to cross-examination, and such evidence is assigned greater weight by the court. Section 240(1)(a) ACJL [10, 11].

quizzes.scenario: In a Magistrate Court in Lagos, the Magistrate calls upon an unrepresented defendant to "start your case" immediately after the prosecution closes, without explaining the three alternatives available.
options: The entire trial is a nullity
The proceeding is valid unless it resulted in a miscarriage of justice
The defendant is deemed to have waived his rights
The Magistrate must be petitioned to the CJ
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Failure to explain the alternatives for defence to an unrepresented defendant is an irregularity that only vitiates trial if a miscarriage of justice occurs. Eme v. State [12].

quizzes.scenario: During his evidence-in-chief, Yusuf raises the defence of alibi for the first time. The prosecution objects because it was not raised during the investigation stage.
options: The objection must be sustained and alibi disregarded
The court must investigate the alibi immediately
Yusuf is entitled to rely on the alibi if he provides particulars now
The prosecution can rely on its existing witnesses to disprove the alibi
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: While alibi should be raised timeously, if raised for the first time at trial, the prosecution may rely on its witnesses to disprove it. Njovens v. State [13].

quizzes.scenario: After the defence has closed its case, the prosecution applies for leave to call a witness to rebut a completely new and unforeseen fact introduced by the defendant.
options: Leave may be granted under the ex-improviso rule
The prosecution is strictly prohibited from calling witnesses after the defence closes
The new witness must be a court witness
This only applies in civil cases
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Under the ex-improviso rule, the prosecution may call witnesses after the defence closes to rebut new matters that human ingenuity could not have foreseen. Section 241 ACJL [14, 15].

quizzes.scenario: In a joint trial of five defendants, the Judge decides to visit the scene of the crime (locus in quo) but only takes the first defendant along for convenience.
options: The procedure is valid if the other four have counsel
The presence of all accused persons is mandatory during a visit to the locus in quo
Locus visits do not require the presence of the accused
Only the complainant must be present
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: In criminal proceedings, all accused persons must be present at the locus in quo; trial in absentia is not permitted. Adunfe v. IGP [16, 17].

quizzes.scenario: During a visit to the locus in quo, the Judge notices a bloodstain not mentioned by any witness in court and uses this discovery as the primary basis for conviction.
options: The judgment is valid as the judge saw it himself
The judge cannot base a decision solely on observations at the locus that are not supported by evidence in court
The judge is an expert witness of the state
Locus visits are intended for discovering fresh evidence
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: A locus visit is to clear doubts; a judge must not base a decision solely on observations there unless they support evidence already led in court. R. v. Dogbe [18, 19].

quizzes.scenario: A Police Legal Practitioner prosecutes a felony in Lagos. After the defence counsel delivers his final address, the police lawyer seeks to reply, even though the defence called no witnesses.
options: The police lawyer has no right of reply
As a law officer in Lagos, the police legal practitioner always has a right of reply
Only the Attorney-General can reply in such cases
The right of reply is only on points of law
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: In Lagos, a law officer (which includes a police legal practitioner) always has a right of reply regardless of how the defence is conducted. Section 271 ACJL [20].

quizzes.scenario: The defence calls only one witness to testify solely as to the good character of the defendant. A prosecutor who is not a law officer seeks a right of reply.
options: The prosecutor has an absolute right of reply
The prosecutor has no right of reply when only character witnesses are called
The defence must first give leave for the reply
Character evidence automatically grants a right of reply
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: If the defence calls only the defendant or character witnesses, a prosecutor who is not a law officer is not entitled to a reply. Section 270 ACJL [21, 22].

quizzes.scenario: A Judge refuses to allow the defence counsel to deliver a final written address, stating that he has already made up his mind based on the overwhelming evidence.
options: This is an efficient use of court time
It is a breach of the defendant's constitutional right to fair hearing
Final addresses are optional in felony cases
Only the prosecution is entitled to a final address
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: The right to deliver a final address is a fundamental element of fair hearing; its denial can lead to the conviction being quashed. Section 294(1) CFRN [23].

quizzes.scenario: After the defence closes its case, the trial Judge is elevated to the Court of Appeal. He attempts to conclude the matter by delivering the judgment at the High Court.
options: The judgment is valid under Section 396(7) ACJA
The judgment is a nullity as the Judge has vacated his seat at the High Court
The Judge has inherent power to conclude his cases
He can deliver it as a private arbitrator
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: A judge elevated to the Court of Appeal cannot return to the High Court to deliver judgment in a part-heard matter. Udeogu v. FRN [24].

quizzes.scenario: During the defence stage, Emeka offers to plead guilty to "stealing" instead of the "robbery" charge. The prosecutor consents to this plea.
options: The court must reject the plea and finish the robbery trial
The court may accept the plea and proceed to convict Emeka of the lesser offence
Emeka must be discharged for double jeopardy
Only the Attorney-General can authorize such a plea
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: A defendant may plead guilty to a lesser offence with the prosecutor's consent, and the court may accept the plea and convict accordingly. Section 214 ACJL [25, 26].

quizzes.scenario: Felix pleads guilty to a lesser offence, but the prosecutor refuses to consent. The court tries Felix for the grave offence but finds him guilty only of the lesser one.
options: The court can convict Felix of the lesser offence
The court must acquit and discharge Felix because the prosecutor refused the plea earlier
Felix must be retried by another judge
The prosecutor's refusal is final and binding
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: If a court rejects a plea to a lesser offence and tries the defendant but finds him not guilty of the grave offence, it cannot then convict him of that lesser offence. R. v. Kelly [27].

topics.topicName: week 12 - Attendance Of Parties And Arraignment

quizzes.scenario: Emeka is on trial for stealing in Lagos. During the trial, he constantly shouts and attacks the court registrar. The Judge orders his removal from the courtroom and continues the trial.
options: The trial must stop immediately as a defendant must be present throughout.
The trial can continue in Emeka's absence due to his misconduct rendering the presence impracticable.
The Judge must grant an adjournment until Emeka calms down and promises to behave.
The trial is a nullity if any part of it is continued without the physical presence of the defendant.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Under Section 208 of the ACJL Lagos, the general rule is that the defendant must be present, but this presence may be dispensed with if he misconducts himself by so interrupting the proceedings as to render their continuance in his presence impracticable. [1, 2]

quizzes.scenario: Alhaji Bako is charged with a simple offence carrying a maximum fine of N8,000 in Abuja. He is served with a summons but decide to stay home and sends his lawyer to plead guilty on his behalf.
options: The court cannot accept the plea and must issue a warrant for his arrest for non-attendance.
The court can dispense with his presence and accept the plea of guilty entered by his legal practitioner.
The court must adjourn the matter for the personal attendance of the defendant before accepting a plea.
The court must record a plea of not guilty and proceed to hear the evidence of the prosecution.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Section 135 of the ACJA allows the court to dispense with the presence of a defendant if the offence carries a penalty not exceeding N10,000 or imprisonment not exceeding 6 months, and he pleads guilty by a legal practitioner or in writing. [2, 3]

quizzes.scenario: At the High Court of Lagos, the Prosecutor is absent on the trial date despite being served with hearing notice, but the defendant, Musa, is present in court.
options: The court must discharge and acquit the defendant Musa because the prosecution has failed to appear.
The court may dismiss the complaint and discharge the defendant, which does not amount to an acquittal.
The court must issue a bench warrant for the Prosecutor and adjourn the case for 7 days.
The court has no choice but to adjourn the matter to another date for the attendance of the prosecutor.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Under Section 232 of the ACJL Lagos and Section 351 of the ACJA, if the complainant is absent after due notice, the court may dismiss the complaint and discharge the defendant, though such discharge does not amount to an acquittal and is not a bar to further proceedings. [4]

quizzes.scenario: Tunde is standing trial for murder in a State High Court. On the day fixed for the trial to begin, his defense counsel is absent. The Judge decides to proceed by taking the evidence of the first prosecution witness.
options: The trial is valid because Tunde was personally present in the dock and heard the evidence.
The proceeding is a nullity because a defendant in a capital case must be represented by counsel throughout the trial.
The Judge acted within his discretion as the defendant is the primary party and counsel's presence is secondary.
The trial should only be adjourned if the prosecutor agrees that the absence of the defense counsel is prejudicial.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: In the trial of a capital offence, both the accused and his counsel must be present, and proceeding in the absence of counsel is a serious irregularity that renders the trial a nullity as held in the Supreme Court case of Josiah v. The State. [4, 5]

quizzes.scenario: During Bitrus's trial for robbery, the Court Registrar read the charge to him in English. The defendant pleaded not guilty, but the Judge did not record being satisfied that Bitrus understood the charge.
options: The arraignment is valid as long as the plea was taken and recorded by the court.
The arraignment is a nullity because the court failed to record its satisfaction that the defendant understood the charge.
The court is presumed to be satisfied unless the defendant or his counsel raises a formal objection.
Arraignment is only valid if the defendant signs a written copy of the charge after it has been read.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Under Section 211 of the ACJL Lagos and Section 271 of the ACJA, the court must record the fact that it is satisfied that the defendant understands the charge read over and explained to him. Failure to follow this mandatory step renders the trial a nullity as held in Kajubo v. State. [6, 7]

quizzes.scenario: Three defendants, X, Y, and Z, are charged together for conspiracy. The Registrar read the charge once to all three simultaneously and then recorded: "The defendants plead not guilty."
options: This is a valid "block plea" because the defendants are joint offenders in the same transaction.
The arraignment is a nullity as pleas must be taken and recorded individually for every defendant.
Only the 1st defendant's plea is validly taken, and the trial should proceed for him only.
The Judge can rectify this at any time by asking the defendants to confirm their collective plea.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: In Nigerian criminal law, there is no such thing as a block plea; every defendant must plead separately to the charge and the court must record the individual plea of each defendant to avoid a nullity as held in Duval v. State and Section 271(3) ACJA. [8]

quizzes.scenario: Kola stands mute in the dock when asked to plead. The Judge immediately enters a plea of not guilty on his behalf and directs the prosecution to call its first witness.
options: The Judge acted correctly to ensure a speedy trial in accordance with the Constitution.
The Judge should have first conducted an inquiry to determine whether Kola was mute of malice or by visitation of God.
The Judge should have ordered Kola to be remanded for 14 days to see if he would speak.
Standing mute is an automatic admission of guilt, and the court should have convicted Kola immediately.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: When a defendant stands mute, the court must conduct an inquiry to determine whether the muteness is of malice or due to the visitation of God (e.g., deafness/dumbness) before entering a plea as held in Yesufu v. The State and Section 276 ACJA. [9, 10]

quizzes.scenario: Chima is charged with murder and pleads guilty. The Judge, impressed by his honesty, convicts and sentences him to death immediately based on the plea without hearing evidence.
options: The conviction is valid as a plea of guilty is a formal confession that dispenses with the need for trial.
The court should have recorded a plea of not guilty notwithstanding the plea of guilty and required the prosecution to prove the case.
The court should have asked for an allocutus before the sentence to see if Chima had any regrets.
The court should have adjourned for a psychiatric test to see if Chima understood the consequence of his plea.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Under Section 213(3) of the ACJL Lagos and Section 274(3) of the ACJA, where an offence is a capital offence, a plea of not guilty shall be recorded notwithstanding a plea of guilty by the defendant, and the prosecution must still lead evidence to prove the guilt. [11]

quizzes.scenario: Okon is charged with the murder of his wife. At the end of the trial, the court finds him not guilty of murder but guilty of manslaughter and sentences him to 15 years imprisonment.
options: The conviction is a nullity because Okon was not expressly charged with manslaughter in the information.
The conviction is valid because manslaughter is a lesser offence of murder and he is deemed to have notice.
The court should have amended the charge to manslaughter before delivering the final verdict.
Okon's constitutional right to be informed of the nature of the offence was breached by the court's verdict.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Under Sections 160-171 ACJL Lagos and 223-231 ACJA, a defendant charged with a grave offence like murder may be convicted of a lesser offence like manslaughter if the facts proved support it, as he is deemed to have notice of the lesser offence. [12, 13]

quizzes.scenario: A prosecutor and a defendant in Abuja agree on a plea bargain. The Presiding Judge was actively involved in the negotiations and suggested the final sentence.
options: The plea bargain is valid as the Judge's involvement ensures the agreement is fair and legal.
The plea bargain is invalid because the presiding judge or magistrate is prohibited from participating in the negotiations.
The Judge is the final arbiter of justice and is encouraged by the ACJA to lead plea bargain discussions.
Participation is only allowed for the Judge if the victim of the crime is also present.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Section 270 of the ACJA and Section 76(5) of the ACJL Lagos provide that the presiding judge or magistrate before whom criminal proceedings are pending shall not participate in the plea-bargaining agreement between the parties. [14, 15]

quizzes.scenario: Sani was previously charged with theft, but the Attorney-General entered a nolle prosequi. Sani is now charged again for the same theft and pleads autrefois acquit.
options: The plea will succeed because a nolle prosequi ends the criminal case against the defendant.
The plea will fail because a nolle prosequi results in a mere discharge and not an acquittal.
The plea will succeed as Sani has already been "tried" once for the same offence.
The plea will fail because the plea of autrefois acquit is only available in capital offences.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: For a plea of autrefois acquit to succeed under Section 36(9) of the Constitution and Section 238 ACJA, the previous trial must have ended in an acquittal or conviction. A nolle prosequi is a discharge that does not bar further proceedings as held in Clark v. A.G. Lagos State. [16-18]

quizzes.scenario: During an inquiry into why a defendant is mute, the court finds he is deaf and dumb. Evidence shows he cannot be made to understand the proceedings.
options: The court must enter a plea of not guilty and proceed with the trial in the interest of justice.
The court shall remand the defendant in custody or release on bail until the Governor's pleasure is known.
The court should discharge and acquit him immediately as he cannot defend himself.
The court should appoint a family member to take the plea on behalf of the defendant.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: In the case of muteness due to visitation of God (deaf and dumb), if the defendant cannot be made to understand the proceedings, the trial shall not proceed and he shall be remanded or released on bail until the Governor's pleasure is known. [19]

quizzes.scenario: On the day of arraignment, the Police bring the defendant to court in handcuffs and leg chains. The Judge proceeds with the arraignment despite the defendant being peaceful.
options: The arraignment is valid because the police have the final say on the security of the defendant.
The arraignment is defective because a defendant must be brought before the court unfettered unless there is cause otherwise.
Fettering is mandatory for all defendants charged with felony offences.
The defendant is presumed guilty once the court allows him to be fettered in the dock.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Under Section 211 of the ACJL Lagos and Section 271 of the ACJA, a defendant must be brought before the court unfettered unless the court sees reasonable cause otherwise, such as apprehension of violence or attempt to escape. [20, 21]

quizzes.scenario: Chidi is charged with stealing. When asked to plead, his lawyer stands up and says, "My Lord, the defendant pleads not guilty to the charge."
options: This is a valid plea because the lawyer has the legal authority to represent his client's interests.
The plea is a nullity as it must be made by the defendant personally and not by his counsel.
The court must record "not guilty" because the lawyer is an officer of the court speaking for the client.
Proxy pleas are generally allowed in all Nigerian courts to speed up the arraignment process.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: The rule is that a plea is the formal response of the defendant and must be taken from the mouth of the accused person himself and not through his counsel, as held in R. v. Pepple. [22, 23]

quizzes.scenario: Segun is charged with armed robbery but pleads "not guilty to armed robbery but guilty to simple robbery." The prosecutor objects, but the Judge accepts the plea.
options: The Judge acted correctly in his discretion to accept a lesser plea and avoid a lengthy trial.
The Judge's acceptance of the plea is invalid as the court requires the prosecutor's consent to accept a plea to a lesser offence.
Segun is automatically acquitted of the armed robbery charge once the Judge accepts the plea.
Segun should have been allowed to plead to both offences separately in different counts.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Where a defendant pleads guilty to a lesser offence not charged, the court may only accept the plea and proceed to conviction with the consent of the prosecution; otherwise, it must proceed with the trial of the original charge. [24, 25]

quizzes.scenario: Femi pleaded guilty to stealing and was convicted by the court. Before he was sentenced, he realized he had a defense and applied to withdraw his guilty plea.
options: The application must be granted by the court because he has not yet been sentenced.
The application must be refused because a plea of guilty cannot be withdrawn once the court has convicted the defendant.
Femi can only withdraw the plea if the Attorney-General gives his express consent to the court.
Femi is entitled to withdraw the plea as of right at any time before the trial ends.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: A plea of guilty can be withdrawn with the leave of court before the court accepts it or before conviction; however, once the court has convicted the defendant, such plea can no longer be withdrawn as held in R. v. The Guest. [26]

quizzes.scenario: Due to a renovation of the courtroom, the Judge decided to take the plea of the defendant in his private chambers with only the registrar and counsel present.
options: The arraignment is valid as long as the defendant's counsel was present to protect his rights.
The arraignment is a nullity because all criminal proceedings, including arraignment, must be held in public.
The Judge has the inherent power to sit anywhere within the court building to dispense justice.
Public access is only constitutionally required for the delivery of the final judgment, not for the plea.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Section 36(4) of the 1999 Constitution and Section 200 ACJL require criminal proceedings to be held in public. In Oviasu v. Oviasu, it was held a trial in chambers violates the requirement of publicity. [27, 28]

quizzes.scenario: In Lagos, an Information is served on a defendant in custody only 24 hours before he is brought for arraignment. The defendant objects to taking his plea.
options: The court should overrule the objection because the defendant has already read the charges against him.
The objection is valid because a defendant in custody is entitled to at least 3 days' notice of hearing.
The court should proceed with the trial only if the defendant is represented by a Senior Advocate of Nigeria.
Service requirements for Informations do not apply if the defendant was arrested without a warrant.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Under Section 256 of the ACJL Lagos, where an accused person is in custody, he should be served with at least 3 days' notice by the court informing him of the date of hearing. [20, 29]

quizzes.scenario: In a trial in Abuja, the defendant, who is on bail, fails to attend court for the third time without any explanation. The court has previously adjourned twice for this reason.
options: The court must strike out the case and discharge the defendant.
The court may use its discretion to continue with the trial in the absence of the defendant.
The court must wait indefinitely for the defendant to appear to avoid a breach of fair hearing.
The court is required to sentence the defendant in absentia to 6 months imprisonment for contempt.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Under Section 352(4) of the ACJA, the court may continue with a trial in the absence of a defendant who was granted bail if he fails to attend the court without reasonable explanation and the court has made an adjournment two times. [30, 31]

quizzes.scenario: At the start of a trial, the Judge orders all witnesses out of court. PW2 stays in the gallery and hears the evidence of PW1 before being called to testify.
options: The witness's testimony is automatically inadmissible due to the violation of the court order.
The testimony is admissible, but the court will attach less weight to it because the witness remained in court.
The trial is a nullity because the presence of the witness corrupted the entire proceeding.
The witness must be disqualified from testifying and the prosecution's case should be struck out.
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: In Falaju v. Amosu and Section 212 of the Evidence Act, if a witness remains in court despite an order to leave, the evidence is not inadmissible, but the omission would affect the weight to be placed on it by the court. [32-34]

quizzes.scenario: Okon is charged with stealing a motorcycle. He raises the defense of alibi, claiming he was in Calabar at the time of the theft in Lagos. What is the standard of proof required for Okon to establish his alibi?
options: Beyond reasonable doubt
Balance of probabilities
Proof beyond iota of doubt
Absolute certainty
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: The standard of proof for criminal defenses or exceptions, including the defense of alibi, is on the balance of probabilities as provided in Section 137 of the Evidence Act 2011 [1, 2].

quizzes.scenario: During a trial for robbery, the prosecution calls 8-year-old Emeka to testify. The trial judge immediately swears Emeka on the Bible before he begins his testimony. What is the status of this procedure?
options: The procedure is correct because all witnesses must be sworn
The procedure is irregular because a child under 14 should generally give unsworn evidence after a preliminary intelligence test
The procedure is correct because Emeka is a competent witness
The procedure is irregular because children cannot testify in robbery cases
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: A child under 14 is competent but should generally not be sworn; the court must first be satisfied that the child is possessed of sufficient intelligence and understands the duty of speaking the truth under Sections 175 and 209 of the Evidence Act [3-5].

quizzes.scenario: The Governor of Kaduna State witnessed a fatal motor accident involving a reckless driver. The prosecution seeks to issue a subpoena to compel the Governor to testify in court. Can the Governor be compelled?
options: Yes, because he is a competent witness
Yes, because no one is above the law in criminal matters
No, because he is competent but not compellable while in office
No, because a Governor can never be a witness
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Under Section 308(1)(c) of the 1999 Constitution, the President, Vice-President, Governor, or Deputy Governor is competent but not compellable to give evidence and no process requiring their attendance shall be issued [6].

quizzes.scenario: Ibrahim is standing trial for treason. The prosecution calls one material witness who testifies to an overt act of Ibrahim attempting to overthrow the government. Ibrahim does not plead guilty. Can the court convict Ibrahim?
options: Yes, if the court believes the single witness
No, treason requires at least two witnesses to an overt act or one witness to each of two overt acts
Yes, because corroboration is only a matter of practice for treason
No, treason can only be proved by documentary evidence
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Under Section 201 of the Evidence Act, a person charged with treason cannot be convicted except on his own plea of guilty or on the evidence of at least two witnesses to one overt act or one witness to each of two overt acts [7].

quizzes.scenario: To prove a series of fraudulent transfers, the prosecution tenders a printout from a bank's central server. The defense objects because no certificate of compliance was attached to the document. What is the likely ruling?
options: Objection sustained; computer-generated evidence requires a certificate identifying the document and describing the device
Objection overruled; bank statements are public documents and don't need certificates
Objection sustained; only the original server can be brought to court
Objection overruled; oral testimony from the bank manager is sufficient without a certificate
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Section 84(4) of the Evidence Act 2011 requires a certificate signed by a person occupying a responsible position in relation to the operation of the device to identify the document and describe the manner it was produced [8, 9].

quizzes.scenario: A police officer is testifying about a search conducted three years ago. He struggles to remember the specific items recovered and asks the court for permission to look at the search inventory he signed at the time of the search. Can he do this?
options: No, witnesses must testify from memory only
Yes, with leave of court, provided the document was made when the transaction was fresh in his memory
Yes, as of right, and the defense cannot see the document
No, because he didn't write the inventory himself
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Section 239(1) of the Evidence Act 2011 allows a witness, with leave of court, to refresh his memory by referring to a writing made by himself at the time of the transaction or soon after [10, 11].

quizzes.scenario: PW2, a witness for the prosecution, begins to give evidence that contradicts his earlier statement to the police and clearly favors the defendant. The prosecutor is surprised and wants to discredit PW2. What step should the prosecutor take?
options: The prosecutor should immediately start cross-examining the witness
The prosecutor should ask the court to declare the witness hostile to allow for cross-examination of his own witness
The prosecutor should ask the court to strike out the entire testimony of PW2
The prosecutor should call the police officer who took the statement to testify instead
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Under Section 230 of the Evidence Act, a party may, with leave of court, cross-examine his own witness if the court declares the witness hostile due to an adverse animus and unwillingness to tell the truth [12, 13].

quizzes.scenario: After the defense closes its case, the prosecution applies to call a new witness to rebut a very specific and surprising piece of evidence about a secret hideout that the defendant mentioned for the first time during his testimony. Will the court allow this?
options: No, the prosecution cannot call any witness after the defense has closed
Yes, under the ex-improviso rule, to rebut a new matter that could not have been reasonably foreseen
No, because the prosecution should have investigated everything before trial
Yes, but only if the defendant consents
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: The ex-improviso rule allows the prosecution, with leave of court, to call a witness after the close of the defense to rebut new matters raised by the defense which human ingenuity could not have foreseen under Section 361 ACJA or 241 ACJL [14, 15].

quizzes.scenario: In a case of arson, the judge decides to visit the remains of the building (locus in quo). During the visit, the judge leaves the defendant in the prison van while he inspects the site with the lawyers. What is the legal effect?
options: The procedure is proper since the lawyers are present
The procedure is irregular as the defendant must be present throughout the visit to the locus in quo
The procedure is proper as long as the judge records his findings
The procedure is irregular only if the defendant is unrepresented
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: In criminal trials, the defendant must be present at the visit to the locus in quo because a trial in absentia is unknown to Nigerian law and he must be able to clarify points raised at the scene [16, 17].

quizzes.scenario: Musa is charged with stealing his neighbor's cattle. The prosecution calls Musa's wife, Amina, to testify against him. Amina refuses to testify. Can she be compelled?
options: Yes, because a wife is always a compellable witness against her husband
No, a spouse is generally not a compellable witness for the prosecution except in specific offenses like sexual crimes or violence against the spouse
Yes, provided she is sworn on the Quran
No, because spouses are incompetent to testify against each other
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Under Section 182(2) of the Evidence Act, a spouse is only a competent and compellable witness for the prosecution in cases involving sexual offenses, property offenses against the spouse, or violence against the spouse [18].

quizzes.scenario: Chuka is charged with robbery. At the close of the prosecution's case, Chuka decides to remain silent and calls no witnesses. The trial judge notes that the prosecution's evidence is weak. Who has the burden of proof?
options: The burden shifts to Chuka to prove his innocence because he remained silent
The burden remains on the prosecution to prove the case beyond reasonable doubt
The burden is now on the judge to investigate the truth
The burden is shared equally between the prosecution and the defense
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: The legal burden of proof in criminal cases is static and remains on the prosecution throughout the trial to prove the defendant's guilt beyond reasonable doubt under Section 135(2) of the Evidence Act [19].

quizzes.scenario: Segun was convicted of burglary based solely on the uncorroborated testimony of his accomplice, Dayo. The trial judge did not warn himself about the danger of convicting on such evidence. What is the effect on appeal?
options: The conviction is automatic nullity because accomplice evidence must be corroborated by law
The conviction is not illegal, but the failure to warn itself makes it unsafe and may lead to it being set aside
The conviction is perfectly valid as corroboration is never required for accomplices
The conviction is only valid if Segun also confessed
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Section 198(1) of the Evidence Act provides that an accomplice is competent, but the court should warn itself that it is unsafe to convict on uncorroborated accomplice testimony; failure to do so may vitiate the conviction [20, 21].

quizzes.scenario: A junior counsel is preparing a trial plan for a case of "Obtaining by False Pretences." Which of the following is the most essential element to include in the plan according to standard practice?
options: The judge's personal history
The specific ingredients of the offence as defined by law
A list of all lawyers in the opponent's firm
The weather forecast for the trial date
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: A trial plan is a graphic strategy that should contain the offence charged, the punishment section, and the specific actus reus and mens rea ingredients to be proved [22, 23].

quizzes.scenario: A 9-year-old girl gives unsworn evidence that she saw the defendant hit the victim. There is no other evidence or witness linking the defendant to the crime. Can the defendant be convicted?
options: Yes, if the judge finds the girl very intelligent
No, unsworn evidence of a child must be corroborated by other material evidence implicating the defendant
Yes, because children never lie in court
No, because unsworn evidence is completely inadmissible
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Under Section 209(3) of the Evidence Act, a defendant cannot be convicted on the unsworn testimony of a child unless it is corroborated by some other material evidence implicating him [5, 24].

quizzes.scenario: A witness who is unable to speak is called to testify. He communicates his answers by writing them down in open court after the questions are read to him. How is this evidence classified?
options: It is inadmissible because all oral evidence must be spoken
It is deemed to be oral evidence
It is classified as documentary evidence
It is inadmissible unless an interpreter is used
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Section 176(1) and (2) of the Evidence Act provides that a witness who is unable to speak may give evidence in any manner such as writing or signs, and such evidence is deemed to be oral evidence [25].

quizzes.scenario: A lawyer is called as a witness and asked to disclose a confession made to him by his client regarding a robbery committed two years before the lawyer was hired. Can the lawyer be compelled to answer?
options: Yes, because there is no privilege in criminal matters
No, it is a privileged communication made in the course of professional employment
Yes, if the prosecutor insists
No, but only if the lawyer is also a priest
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Under Section 192 of the Evidence Act, a legal practitioner cannot be compelled to disclose any communication made to him in the course of and for the purpose of his employment by his client [26].

quizzes.scenario: Sani is charged with perjury for lying under oath in a previous civil case. The prosecution calls one witness who proves that Sani lied. There is no other evidence. Can Sani be convicted?
options: Yes, one witness is generally enough to prove any crime
No, a person cannot be convicted of perjury on the uncorroborated testimony of one witness
Yes, but only if the previous case was also criminal
No, perjury can only be proved by the judge of the previous case
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Section 202 of the Evidence Act provides that a person shall not be convicted of perjury solely upon the testimony of one witness as to the falsity of any statement [27].

quizzes.scenario: The prosecution seeks to tender a tape recording of a conversation between the defendant and a victim. The defense objects, claiming it is hearsay. What foundation must the prosecution lay for it to be admitted?
options: None, tape recordings are always admissible
The maker must testify, the recording must be authentic/original, and the voice must be identified as the defendant's
Only a certificate of compliance under Section 84 is needed
Tape recordings are never admissible in Nigerian criminal trials
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: For a tape recording to be admitted, the prosecution must show it is authentic, identifying the voices and ensuring there was no opportunity for tampering [9, 28].

quizzes.scenario: During a visit to the locus in quo (a warehouse), the judge notices a hidden door not mentioned in court. He asks a bystander to explain where it leads. The bystander gives a detailed explanation. Is this proper?
options: Yes, the judge's duty is to find the truth at the scene
No, a visit to the locus in quo is for inspection and not an opportunity to take fresh evidence from persons not called as witnesses
Yes, provided the bystander is sworn on the spot
No, but the judge can use the info if he tells the lawyers later
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: A visit to the locus in quo is for the purpose of inspection to clarify evidence already led; it is not an opportunity to receive fresh evidence [29].

quizzes.scenario: At the end of a trial for murder, the judge is 95% sure the defendant is guilty but has a slight, reasonable doubt based on the lack of a murder weapon. What should the verdict be?
options: Guilty, because 95% is a very high probability
Not Guilty, because the prosecution must prove the case beyond reasonable doubt
Guilty, provided the defendant didn't prove his innocence
Not Guilty, but the judge should order a fresh investigation
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Section 135(1) of the Evidence Act 2011 requires the prosecution to prove the guilt of the defendant beyond reasonable doubt; any reasonable doubt must be resolved in favor of the defendant [1, 19].

quizzes.scenario: In a criminal trial at the High Court of Lagos, Chief Adewale, who was the complainant and victim of a battery case, is aggrieved that the defendant was discharged and acquitted. He approaches you to file an appeal. What is the correct legal position?
options: Chief Adewale can appeal as of right as an interested party
Chief Adewale can only appeal with the leave of the court
Only the Attorney-General or the defendant can appeal
Chief Adewale can appeal because he is the victim
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Under Section 243(1)(a) of the 1999 Constitution and the rule in Akinbiyi v. Adelabu, a right of appeal in criminal proceedings is exercisable only at the instance of an accused person or the Attorney-General, and not the victim or complainant [1, 2].

quizzes.scenario: Emeka was convicted of stealing by a Magistrate Court in Kano on the 10th of May. He intends to file a notice of appeal against the judgment on the 15th of June. How should the court treat his intended appeal?
options: The appeal is valid as it is within 90 days
The appeal is incompetent as it was filed outside the 30-day limit
The appeal is valid since he is a convict
Emeka must pay a fine to have the appeal heard
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: According to Section 485(4) of the ACJA and Section 461(4) of the ACJL Kano, an appeal from a Magistrate Court to the High Court must be lodged within 30 days from the date of the decision [3, 4].

quizzes.scenario: During the pronouncement of a judgment in a Lagos Magistrate Court, the defendant stood up and told the Magistrate, "I want to appeal this decision," while the prosecutor was still present. He did not file any other document within 30 days. Is the appeal validly entered?
options: Yes, verbal notice is sufficient in all Magistrate Courts
No, verbal notice is not recognized in Lagos State
Yes, provided the Magistrate recorded it
No, because he did not pay the filing fees
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Section 69(1) of the Magistrate Court Law of Lagos State and Section 485(3) of the ACJA provide that verbal notice of appeal is not recognized in Lagos, Abuja, or Kano from a Magistrate Court to the High Court; it must be in writing [3, 5].

quizzes.scenario: A defendant convicted of armed robbery at the Federal High Court intends to appeal to the Court of Appeal solely on the ground that the trial judge was biased. What is the requirement for this appeal?
options: He requires leave of the Federal High Court
He requires leave of the Court of Appeal
He can appeal as of right
He must wait for the Attorney-General's consent
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Under Section 241(1) of the 1999 Constitution, an appeal lies as of right from the High Court to the Court of Appeal where the ground involves questions of law alone, such as an allegation of bias which goes to fair hearing [6, 7].

quizzes.scenario: An appellant who filed a notice of appeal from the Magistrate Court to the High Court of Lagos decides to abandon the appeal. He files a notice of abandonment one day before the date fixed for hearing. What is the status of this notice?
options: The notice is valid and effective
The notice is invalid as it must be filed at least 2 days before the hearing
The notice is valid but he must pay double costs
The appeal cannot be abandoned once entered
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Order 2 Part I Rule 13 of the High Court of Lagos (Appeal) Rules requires a notice of abandonment to be filed not less than 2 days before the date fixed for the hearing [8].

quizzes.scenario: Mr. Yusuf was convicted of a crime and sentenced to pay a fine of N500,000. He filed an appeal but died while the appeal was still pending. His family wants to continue the appeal to clear his name. What is the legal position?
options: The appeal abates immediately upon his death
The appeal survives because it involves a sentence of fine
The appeal abates because criminal liability is personal
The appeal survives only if the Attorney-General agrees
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: In line with Section 64 of the Magistrate Court Law and the rule in Re: Abdullah, a criminal appeal survives the death of the appellant only if the sentence involves a fine, which can be executed against the estate [9, 10].

quizzes.scenario: A lawyer in a criminal appeal from the High Court to the Court of Appeal couched his omnibus ground of appeal as follows: "That the judgment is against the weight of evidence." The Respondent has filed a preliminary objection. What is the likely outcome?
options: The objection will be overruled as the ground is standard
The ground will be struck out as it is improper in criminal appeals
The court will allow the ground if the lawyer apologizes
The ground is valid under the Court of Appeal Rules
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: According to the rule in Enitan v. State and Olaiya v. State, the proper omnibus ground in criminal cases is "that the decision is unreasonable and cannot be supported having regard to the evidence," and using the civil format is a fatal error [11, 12].

quizzes.scenario: An appellant at the Court of Appeal fails to file his brief of argument within 45 days of receiving the record of appeal. The Respondent applies for the appeal to be dismissed. What can the court do?
options: The court must wait for 90 days before dismissing
The court may suo motu or on application dismiss for want of diligent prosecution
The court cannot dismiss a criminal appeal for failure to file a brief
The court will automatically grant the appeal to the Respondent
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Under Order 19 of the Court of Appeal Rules, if an appellant fails to file his brief within the 45-day period, the court has the discretion to dismiss the appeal for lack of prosecution [13-15].

quizzes.scenario: Musa is convicted of murder and sentenced to death. He applies for bail pending his appeal to the Court of Appeal, citing the fact that he was on bail throughout the trial and never jumped bail. How will the court likely rule?
options: Bail is granted as of right for all convicts
Bail is granted since he did not jump bail previously
Bail is rarely granted and requires "exceptional circumstances"
Bail is granted if the prosecutor does not object
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: For bail pending appeal, the burden shifts to the convict to show "special or exceptional circumstances," and previous good conduct alone is often insufficient for a capital offence conviction [16, 17].

quizzes.scenario: After a High Court in the North delivered its judgment convicting Okon, the judge realized he made a clerical error in the date of the judgment. He amends it the following day in his chambers. Is this amendment valid?
options: No, a judge is functus officio once judgment is pronounced
Yes, a judge can amend to correct clerical errors or accidental slips
Yes, provided the defendant is informed by post
No, only the Court of Appeal can correct such errors
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: While a judge is generally functus officio after judgment, Section 318 of the ACJA allows amendments specifically to correct clerical errors or accidental slips [18].

quizzes.scenario: A Magistrate in Abuja convicted a defendant of a felony but felt the maximum sentence he could impose (10 years) was insufficient for the crime. He decided to refer the case to the High Court for sentencing. Is this procedure allowed?
options: No, a Magistrate must sentence within his jurisdiction
Yes, the Magistrate can state the case for punishment in the North
No, he should have transferred the case before conviction
Yes, but only with the consent of the Chief Judge
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: In the North and under the ACJA, a Magistrate who has conducted a valid trial and conviction can state the case to a higher court for a more severe punishment than his jurisdiction allows [19, 20].

quizzes.scenario: An appellant filed his notice of appeal at the High Court on the 91st day after judgment was delivered. He did not seek leave for extension of time. The Respondent objects. What is the status of the appeal?
options: The appeal is valid as the delay is only one day
The appeal is incompetent and must be struck out
The court will waive the delay in the interest of justice
The appellant can file for leave after the objection is heard
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Since the 90-day time limit for HC-to-CA appeals is statutory under Section 24 of the Court of Appeal Act, an appeal filed outside this time without an order for extension is a nullity [21, 22].

quizzes.scenario: In an appeal before the Court of Appeal, the Respondent fails to file a brief of argument within 30 days of being served the Appellant's brief. What is the consequence?
options: The Appellant automatically wins the appeal
The Respondent will not be allowed to make oral arguments at the hearing
The court will file a brief for the Respondent
The appeal will be dismissed for lack of a Respondent's brief
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: According to the Court of Appeal Rules, the failure of a Respondent to file a brief within time prevents them from being heard in oral argument, although the Appellant must still succeed on the merit of their own brief [15].

quizzes.scenario: A defendant is convicted of a non-capital offence at the Magistrate Court. He appeals to the High Court and applies for stay of proceedings of the enforcement of the fine. What is the rule regarding stay of proceedings?
options: Stay of proceedings is granted as a matter of course in appeals
Stay of proceedings is prohibited in all criminal matters
Stay of proceedings is granted only by the Attorney-General
Stay of proceedings is granted if the defendant pays a deposit
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Section 306 of the ACJA and Section 273 of the ACJL Lagos expressly provide that applications for stay of proceedings in criminal matters shall not be entertained [23, 24].

quizzes.scenario: The Supreme Court is sitting to hear an appeal that involves the interpretation of the Fundamental Rights provisions of the Constitution. How many Justices must be present to form a quorum?
options: Not less than three Justices
Not less than five Justices
Not less than seven Justices
All the Justices of the Supreme Court
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Under Section 234 of the 1999 Constitution, while the Supreme Court typically sits with five Justices, it must be constituted by seven Justices when hearing matters involving the interpretation of the Constitution or Chapter IV rights [25].

quizzes.scenario: A prosecutor intends to appeal against an acquittal for murder at the Court of Appeal to the Supreme Court. Within how many days must he file his notice of appeal?
options: Within 30 days
Within 90 days
Within 7 days
Within 14 days
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Under Section 4 of the Judicial, etc. Official and Appeals by Prosecutors Act 1963, a prosecutor appealing a murder/manslaughter verdict must do so within 7 days, and there is no provision for extension of time [26].

quizzes.scenario: An appellant at the Court of Appeal discovers a vital piece of evidence that was not available during his trial at the High Court. He applies to adduce this "additional evidence" on appeal. Under what condition can the court grant this?
options: The court grants it as a matter of right
Only if it would not lead to a rehearing and is in the interest of justice
Only if the Attorney-General consents
The Court of Appeal can never take additional evidence
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: As held in Abiola v. Police and Oladipupo v. State, an appellate court admits fresh evidence only in exceptional circumstances where it is relevant and was genuinely unavailable during the trial [27, 28].

quizzes.scenario: A trial judge in a criminal case delivered judgment 95 days after the final addresses were adopted. The convict appeals on the ground that the judgment is a nullity for being outside the 90-day constitutional limit. Is he correct?
options: Yes, any judgment after 90 days is an automatic nullity
No, it is only a nullity if he proves miscarriage of justice
No, the limit is 120 days in criminal cases
Yes, because the judge is functus officio after 90 days
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Section 294(5) of the 1999 Constitution provides that a judgment delivered outside the 90-day limit is voidable, not void, and will only be set aside if the appellant proves that the delay caused a miscarriage of justice [29, 30].

quizzes.scenario: A defendant was convicted by a Magistrate for an offence beyond the Magistrate's sentencing power. The convict appeals. What order can the High Court make?
options: It must quash the conviction entirely
It can affirm the conviction but reduce the sentence to the Magistrate's limit
It must order a retrial before a High Court
It can increase the sentence to the maximum for the offence
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Following the rules in Emone v. Police and Quartey v. IGP, the appellate court can affirm the conviction but must alter the sentence so it does not exceed the trial court's maximum jurisdiction [31-33].

quizzes.scenario: A convict in prison custody has filed an appeal to the Court of Appeal and is being represented by a Senior Advocate of Nigeria. He insists on being physically present in court during the hearing. What is the legal position?
options: He is entitled to be present as a matter of right
He is not entitled to be present unless the court so orders
He can only be present if he pays for the transport of the warders
He can only be present if he argues the appeal himself
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Section 40 of the High Court (Lagos) Law provides that a convict in custody who is represented by a legal practitioner is not entitled as of right to be present at the hearing of the appeal unless the court makes a specific order [34].

quizzes.scenario: Mr. Ade, a Nigerian citizen in 1905, has no university degree but has served for five consecutive years in the law office of a practicing solicitor in the Gold Coast and passed the prescribed examinations. Under which category is he eligible to be admitted to practice?
options: Professionally Qualified Lawyer
Article Lawyer
Local Attorney
Solicitor-Advocate
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Under Section 73 of the Supreme Court Ordinance 1876, a person who served 5 years in the office of a practicing barrister/solicitor in Nigeria or Gold Coast and passed the CJN's exam could be admitted as an Article Lawyer [1], [2].

quizzes.scenario: In 1900, Mr. Williams, a fit and proper person with basic academic qualifications, passes an examination set by the Chief Justice to test his knowledge of English law. For how long is his initial license to practice valid?
options: One year
Three months
Six months
Indefinite
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Section 74 of the Supreme Court Ordinance 1876 provided that Local Attorneys were given a license to practice for 6 months, renewable for another 6 months [3], [4].

quizzes.scenario: An English-trained Solicitor arrives in Lagos in 1890 and applies for enrollment. Upon successful enrollment, what is his professional status in the colony of Nigeria?
options: Solicitor only
Barrister only
Barrister and Solicitor
Local Attorney
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: The Supreme Court Ordinance 1876 fused the legal profession by providing that every English-trained barrister or solicitor was, upon enrollment, qualified to practice as both a barrister and solicitor [5], [6].

quizzes.scenario: Under the 1945 Rules, an English-trained barrister wishes to enroll in Nigeria. He provides evidence that he has read in the chambers of a practicing barrister of 6 years standing in London for 12 months. Does he satisfy the additional requirements for enrollment?
options: No, he needs 2 years in London
Yes, he has read for 1 year with a practitioner of at least 5 years standing
No, he must read in Nigeria only
Yes, provided he also has a law degree
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Order 16 Rule 1 of the Supreme Court (Civil Procedure) Rules 1945 allowed enrollment if a person had read for 1 year in the law office of a practicing barrister or solicitor of not less than 5 years standing [7], [8].

quizzes.scenario: A student graduating in law in 1963 from a foreign university finds that the Council of Legal Education does not recognize the syllabus of his university. What is the legal requirement for his admission to practice in Nigeria?
options: He is permanently barred
He must obtain a Nigerian university degree
He must take further examination as the Council may prescribe
He must serve as an Article lawyer for 5 years
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Based on the Unsworth Committee recommendations adopted in 1962, any person graduating from a university with an unaccepted syllabus is required to take further examination as prescribed by the Council [9], [10].

quizzes.scenario: Mr. Okon is a law graduate employed as a Legal Officer in the Federal Ministry of Justice but has not been called to the Nigerian Bar or enrolled on the roll. What is his status regarding legal practice?
options: He cannot practice under any circumstances
He is entitled to practice generally
He is entitled to practice for the purposes of that office
He is a Local Attorney
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Section 2(3) of the Legal Practitioners Act allows persons exercising the functions of specific offices in the public service to practice as a barrister and solicitor for the purpose of that office [11], [12].

quizzes.scenario: A distinguished advocate from Ghana, where the legal system is similar to Nigeria's, wishes to represent a specific client in a high-profile case in Abuja. What must he obtain to practice for this specific proceeding?
options: A qualifying certificate from the CLE
A warrant from the Chief Justice of Nigeria
Automatic enrollment by the NBA
A visa from the Ministry of Justice
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Section 2(2) of the Legal Practitioners Act empowers the CJN to issue a warrant to a person entitled to practice as an advocate in a country with a similar legal system to practice as a barrister for specific proceedings [11], [13].

quizzes.scenario: A Nigerian citizen qualified for the Nigerian Law School in 1985 but was unable to attend because he was held as a political prisoner for ten years. He has since acquired significant legal experience. What can he apply for?
options: Partial Exemption from Bar Part I
Enrollment by virtue of A.G. regulation
Total exemption from attending the Nigerian Law School
Admission as a Local Attorney
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Under Legal Notice No. 439 of 1989, a Nigerian citizen who qualified for NLS but lost the opportunity for reasons beyond his control and has acquired at least 5 years of necessary experience may be granted total exemption [14], [15].

quizzes.scenario: A law professor who graduated from a university in the United States (a non-common law jurisdiction) has been teaching law at a Nigerian university for 11 years. What NLS exemption is he eligible for?
options: Total exemption from NLS
Exemption from Bar Part I (Partial Exemption)
Exemption from dining terms
No exemption available
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Legal Notice No. 446 of 1989 provides that law graduates from non-common law jurisdictions who have taught in a Nigerian law faculty for at least ten years are eligible for partial exemption from the Bar Part I course [16], [17].

quizzes.scenario: A law student arrives at the dining hall for his first dining term at 4:40 PM for a dinner scheduled to commence at 5:00 PM. What is the likely consequence?
options: He will be seated immediately
He will be allowed to enter but must apologize
He will be disallowed from keeping the dining term for being late
He must pay a fine to enter
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Dining regulations require all students to be seated quietly in the Dining Hall by 4:30 p.m. at the latest; breach of dinner rules attracts sanctions [18], [19].

quizzes.scenario: During the main course of a law dinner, a student realizes he needs the salt, which is located near a student on the other side of the table. What is the proper decorum?
options: Reach across the table to grab it
Stand up and walk to the other side
Whisper to the colleague nearest to the salt to pass it
Ask a steward to get it
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Table manners dictate that students should not stretch their hands across other diners but should whisper to the colleague nearest to the item to pass it [20], [21].

quizzes.scenario: A student is very impressed by the speech of a Bencher during dinner and decides to take a quick photograph with his mobile phone to commemorate the event. Is this allowed?
options: Yes, if the flash is off
Yes, after dinner has ended
No, photograph taking is strictly forbidden during dinner
Only with the permission of the Bencher
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Decorum rules for law dinners state that photograph taking is strictly forbidden and all mobile phones must be switched off [22], [21].

quizzes.scenario: A male student attends a dining term wearing a dark charcoal grey suit, white shirt, and a black bow tie. Is he properly dressed for the occasion?
options: Yes, charcoal grey is a dark color
Yes, a black bow tie is professional
No, bow ties are not allowed; he must wear a black tie
No, only black suits are permitted
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Regulation dressing for male students requires dark suits with white shirts and black ties; bow ties are expressly excluded [23], [24].

quizzes.scenario: A female student attends her final call-to-bar dinner. She is wearing a dark navy blue suit with a skirt that ends 4 inches above her knee. Does she meet the dressing requirements?
options: Yes, navy blue is a dark color
No, the skirt must be at least knee length
Yes, if she wears black stockings
No, only black suits are allowed
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Female students' regulation dress prohibits miniskirts or uneven hemlines; skirts must be at least knee length [18], [19].

quizzes.scenario: At the conclusion of the Bencher's speech, the students are asked to rise and charge their glasses for the Loyal Toast. What is the correct response after the proposer says "To the prosperity of the Federal Republic of Nigeria"?
options: "God bless Nigeria"
"To the Federal Republic of Nigeria"
"Long live the Republic"
"To the prosperity of Nigeria"
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: The prescribed response to the Loyal Toast is "To the Federal Republic of Nigeria" [25], [26].

quizzes.scenario: Which of the following historical figures was the first female lawyer and the first female magistrate in Nigeria?
options: Folake Solanke
Mariam Aluma Muktar
Stella Mark
Stella Oduah
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Stella Mark, qualified in 1935, was the first female lawyer and first female magistrate in Nigeria [16], [27].

quizzes.scenario: An applicant for a warrant to practice in a specific proceeding is appearing before the Chief Justice of Nigeria. The CJN agrees to the request but stipulates a fee of N100. Is this fee within the statutory limit?
options: Yes, for international advocates
No, the fee must not exceed N50
Yes, if the case is complex
No, there is no fee for warrants
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Section 2(2) of the Legal Practitioners Act provides that the fee for a warrant to practice shall not exceed fifty naira [11], [13].

quizzes.scenario: In 1958, a committee was formed to overhaul legal education in Nigeria due to the "half-baked" problem of English-trained lawyers. Who chaired this committee?
options: Justice Taslim Elias
E.I.G. Unsworth
Chief Rotimi Williams
Adetokunbo Ademola
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: The committee appointed in April 1959 to consider the future of legal education was under the chairmanship of the Attorney General of the Federation, E.I.G. Unsworth [28], [29].

quizzes.scenario: Mr. Jide was called to the Nigerian Bar in November 2023. He has completed his dining terms and NLS course but has not yet enrolled his name with the Registrar of the Supreme Court. Can he practice generally?
options: Yes, call to bar is sufficient
No, he must be on the roll of legal practitioners
Yes, if he has his call certificate
Only in the Magistrate court
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Section 2(1) of the Legal Practitioners Act states a person is entitled to practice generally if, and only if, his name is on the roll [30], [31].

quizzes.scenario: In 1913, Mr. Osho Davies applied to the Chief Justice to be admitted as a Local Attorney. His application was refused, and his appeal was dismissed. What was the primary reason for the cessation of Local Attorney licenses?
options: Lack of academic qualifications
The protest of professionally qualified lawyers
The amalgamation of 1914
A change in the Supreme Court Ordinance
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Licenses for Local Attorneys were not granted after 1908 because qualified lawyers protested to the CJN that there was no further need for them [3], [32].

quizzes.scenario: Mr. Ade is stuck in heavy traffic and realizes he will be 40 minutes late for his hearing. He decides to call his clerk to tell the court orally.
options: He should wait until he arrives to explain himself
He must write to the court and opposing counsel requesting the case be stood down or adjourned to a reasonable time
He should enter the courtroom quietly and sit down
He should only inform the opposing counsel
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Under professional etiquette, when counsel is unavoidably late, he should write to the court and the opposite counsel requesting a stand down or adjournment as per Source [1, 2].

quizzes.scenario: A male legal practitioner appears before the Court of Appeal wearing a light brown three-piece suit under his wig and gown.
options: He is properly dressed for a superior court
He is improperly dressed as he must be in a dark suit (black, blue-black, or charcoal grey)
The color of the suit does not matter once robed
He should have worn a white suit to match the collar
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Rule 36(a) and Rule 45 RPC require counsel at Superior Courts to be attired in a dignified manner, specifically in dark suits (black, blue-black, or charcoal grey) as per Source [3, 4].

quizzes.scenario: Barrister Chidi is a defendant in a personal debt recovery suit. He appears in the High Court fully robed in his wig and gown to conduct his defense.
options: He is entitled to robe as a member of the bar
He is prohibited from robing when conducting his own case as a party
He can only robe if he is appearing for a co-defendant
He must robe to show respect to the court
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Rule 45(2)(b) and Rule 36(1)(f) RPC 2023 provide that a lawyer shall not wear the Barrister's or Senior Advocate's robe when conducting his own case as a party to a legal proceeding as per Source [5-7].

quizzes.scenario: During a trial, Barrister Musa discovers a recent Supreme Court judgment that directly contradicts his client's position. He decides not to mention it since the opposing counsel is unaware of it.
options: He has no duty to help the opponent's case
He must disclose the decided case to the court even if it is against him
He should only disclose it if the judge asks
He should distinguish the case without citing it
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Rule 32 RPC requires counsel to make a full disclosure of evidence and not fail to cite a decided case that is against him, as he is an officer of the court assisting in ascertaining the truth as per Source [8, 9].

quizzes.scenario: While the opposing counsel is cross-examining a witness, Barrister Tunde becomes angry and begins a loud verbal argument directly with the opposing counsel across the table.
options: He is protecting his client's interests
He is in breach of court decorum as he must address all objections and arguments to the judge
He is allowed to engage in banter to unsettle the opponent
He should wait until the court rises to continue the argument
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Rule 36(d) RPC provides that a lawyer shall not engage in any banter or controversy with the opposing party but must address his objections and arguments to the judge as per Source [10, 11].

quizzes.scenario: A female lawyer appears in the High Court wearing a bright red skirt that is several inches above her knees under her gown.
options: She is properly dressed
She is improperly dressed as her skirt should be at least knee length and in a dark color
The gown covers the skirt so it is acceptable
Robing rules only apply to the jacket and wig
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: For female lawyers, Rule 36 RPC and professional standards require a black, blue-black or charcoal grey dress or skirt which must be at least knee length as per Source [12, 13].

quizzes.scenario: Immediately after a heated court session, Barrister Joy grants a TV interview where she argues that the Judge's refusal to grant her bail application is proof of corruption and partiality.
options: She is exercising her right to free speech
She has committed contempt of court by making extra-judicial statements scandalizing the court
She is right to inform the public of the judge's bias
She can only be punished if the case is finished
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Rule 33 RPC and Rule 31(2) prohibit extra-judicial statements likely to prejudice a fair trial, and making complaints against a judge to the press constitutes contempt as per Source [14-16].

quizzes.scenario: Barrister Sani is appearing before a Magistrate in Lagos State. He persistently addresses the Magistrate as "Your Worship" throughout the proceedings.
options: He is using the correct mode of address
He is incorrect as Magistrates in Lagos State are addressed as "Your Honour"
He should use "My Lord"
"Your Worship" is the only allowed term for Magistrates nationwide
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: While Magistrates are generally addressed as "Your Worship," in Lagos State they are addressed as "Your Honour" pursuant to the Magistrates Courts Law of Lagos State as per Source [17, 18].

quizzes.scenario: A legal practitioner realizes that the Judge presiding over his case is his former law school classmate. He visits the Judge in chambers privately to discuss the "good old days" while the case is pending.
options: It is a harmless social visit
It is improper as he must not conduct himself to give the impression of gaining special personal consideration from a judge
He should invite the judge to dinner instead
He must disclose the friendship in open court only
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Rule 34 RPC provides that a lawyer shall not do anything to give the impression that his conduct is calculated to gain special personal consideration or favor from a judge as per Source [14, 19].

quizzes.scenario: A State Prosecutor discovers a witness statement that clearly exonerates the accused person. He decides to hide the statement to ensure he gets a conviction for the State.
options: He is performing his duty to convict
He is in breach of his duty as a prosecutor whose primary duty is to see that justice is done, not just to convict
He should only show it if the defense asks
He can use it to negotiate a plea bargain instead
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Rule 37(6) RPC states that the primary duty of a lawyer engaged in public prosecution is not to convict but to see that justice is done, and suppression of facts establishing innocence is unethical as per Source [20].

quizzes.scenario: A client on trial for murder confesses his actual guilt to his defense counsel in confidence but insists on testifying that he was in another city during the crime.
options: The lawyer must call the client to testify as instructed
The lawyer must refuse to call the client to give false evidence or present a positive defense known to be false
The lawyer must immediately report the confession to the police
The lawyer must withdraw and tell the judge why
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: While a confidential confession does not require withdrawal, a lawyer cannot take part in putting forward a case he knows is false or supported by perjury under Rule 15(3)(f) RPC as per Source [21, 22].

quizzes.scenario: During the delivery of a judgment, a lawyer stands up and shouts, "This is a most unjust remark!" at the presiding Judge.
options: It is a fair criticism of the judgment
It amounts to criminal contempt in facie curiae
He is protected by professional immunity
He should be allowed to finish his statement
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Interrupting court proceedings with insulting remarks in the presence of the judge constitutes contempt in facie curiae, which can be punished summarily as per Source [23-25].

quizzes.scenario: Barrister Obi has a matter for hearing but fails to attend for three consecutive sittings without obtaining leave of court or informing the other party.
options: The court must wait for him
Persistent absence without leave can be treated as interference with the course of justice and contempt of court
He is only liable to pay a small fine
The case should be adjourned indefinitely
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Persistent absence of counsel from court without leave is treated as interference with the course of justice and may be held to be contempt of court as per Source [3, 26].

quizzes.scenario: While the Judge is delivering a ruling, Barrister Funmi's phone rings loudly and she proceeds to answer the call while sitting at the bar.
options: She is managing her law office effectively
She has breached court decorum as mobile phones must be switched off or silenced
The judge should pause for her to finish
It is acceptable if it is an emergency
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Maintaining correct decorum under Rule 36 RPC requires that mobile phones be switched off and counsel remain silent and attentive while the Judge is speaking as per Source [11, 27].

quizzes.scenario: A Senior Advocate of Nigeria (SAN) appears alone in the High Court to conduct a complex civil trial for a multi-national corporation.
options: He is entitled to appear alone by his rank
He is restricted from appearing in a civil case in a superior court without a junior or another SAN
Only the Attorney General can challenge him
He only needs a junior for criminal cases
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Under the SAN (Privileges and Functions) Rules, a SAN shall not appear as counsel in any civil case before a superior court of record except with a junior or another SAN as per Source [28-30].

quizzes.scenario: A lawyer is dissatisfied with the conduct of a High Court Judge. He decides to write a series of defamatory articles in a national newspaper to "expose" the judge.
options: He is performing his duty to the state
He is in breach of Rule 31(2) RPC which requires complaints against judicial officers to be made to the appropriate authorities
He should have used social media instead
The newspaper is an appropriate authority
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Rule 31(2) RPC provides that if a lawyer has a proper ground for complaint against a judicial officer, he shall make the complaint to the appropriate authorities (like the NJC), not the press as per Source [10, 15, 31].

quizzes.scenario: During a civil trial, the Judge enters the courtroom. Barrister Peters remains seated and continues reading his case file until the Judge calls his case.
options: He is maximizing his time
He is in breach of court decorum which requires him to stand when the Judge enters and remain standing until the Judge sits
He only needs to stand if he is the lead counsel
Bowing is sufficient
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Rule 36 RPC and established etiquette require counsel to stand when the court orderly bangs the door and remain standing until the judge has sat down as per Source [10, 32].

quizzes.scenario: A lawyer prepares an affidavit for his client to sign, knowing that the facts stated in the affidavit regarding a land transaction are completely false.
options: He is fulfilling his duty to his client
He is in breach of his duty of candor and fairness to the court
The client is solely responsible for the affidavit
It is only a breach if he also signs it
quizzes.verbatimCorrection: Rule 32 RPC and the duty of candor prohibit a lawyer from knowingly misleading the court or using false evidence, such as untrue affidavits, as per Source [8, 33].
